# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Span of an RCC Lintel (KTU Standard)

**Step 1 — Statement of the Problem.**
Given a clear opening of width $L_{clear} = 1200$ mm, effective depth of lintel $d = 150$ mm, and bearing provided on each jamb = 200 mm. Determine the effective span for design.

**Step 2 — Definition Recap.**
As per IS 456:2000, for simply supported members, effective span is the **least** of:

$$
L_{eff} = L_{clear} + d \quad \text{AND} \quad L_{eff} = \text{center-to-center distance of supports}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Option 1 (Clear Span + Effective Depth).**

$$
L_{eff,1} = 1200 \text{ mm} + 150 \text{ mm} = 1350 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Option 2 (Center-to-Center of Supports).**

$$
L_{eff,2} = L_{clear} + L_{bearing,left} + L_{bearing,right}
$$

$$
L_{eff,2} = 1200 + 200 + 200 = 1600 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Choose the LESS Value (Governing Criterion).**

$$
L_{eff} = \min(1350, 1600) = 1350 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer for Design Use.**

$$
L_{eff} = 1.35 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note:** The "lesser" criterion is conservative — the designer adopts the smaller value to be safe. The 1350 mm value assumes the lintel does NOT rest on the very edge of the support but begins a depth $d$ inside the opening.

## 3.2 Derivation: Horizontal Thrust in a Semicircular Arch

**Step 1 — Set Up the Geometry.**

Consider a semicircular arch of span $l$ and rise $h = l/2$, subjected to a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of $w$ per unit run (per metre length of arch).

**Step 2 — Identify Vertical Reaction.**
By symmetry of the arch and the load:

$$
V_A = V_B = \frac{w \cdot l}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bending Moment Equation at the Crown.**
Taking moments about the crown (right half considered as a free body):

$$
V_B \cdot \frac{l}{2} - H \cdot h - w \cdot \frac{l}{2} \cdot \frac{l}{4} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Horizontal Thrust $H$.**

$$
H \cdot h = V_B \cdot \frac{l}{2} - w \cdot \frac{l^2}{8}
$$

Substituting $V_B = w \cdot l / 2$:

$$
H \cdot h = \frac{w l}{2} \cdot \frac{l}{2} - \frac{w l^2}{8} = \frac{w l^2}{4} - \frac{w l^2}{8} = \frac{w l^2}{8}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Horizontal Thrust Equation.**

$$
\boxed{H = \frac{w \cdot l^2}{8 h}}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Check.**
Let $l = 4$ m, $h = 2$ m, $w = 10$ kN/m:

$$
H = \frac{10 \cdot 4^2}{8 \cdot 2} = \frac{160}{16} = 10 \text{ kN}
$$

This is the **horizontal thrust** pushing the abutments outward. Abutments must be designed to resist this thrust through their self-weight and backfill weight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A flatter arch (small $h$) gives a LARGER horizontal thrust — this is why flat arches require massive abutments, while semicircular arches are more efficient. A semicircular arch with $h = l/2$ has minimum thrust for a given span and load.

## 3.3 Derivation: Voussoir Taper Angle (KTU Standard Question)

**Step 1 — Setup.**
For a semicircular arch with span $l$ and number of voussoirs $n$, find the taper (included) angle $\alpha$ of each voussoir at the **intrados**.

**Step 2 — Geometric Principle.**
Each voussoir subtends an equal angle at the center of the circle. The full semicircle = $180°$. So the **central angle** per voussoir:

$$
\phi = \frac{180°}{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Taper (Included) Angle Relationship.**
For a wedge-shaped voussoir, the included angle $\alpha$ (taper) is related to the central angle by the geometry of isoceles triangles. The standard result used in KTU textbooks is:

$$
\boxed{\alpha = 180° - 2\phi = 180° \left(1 - \frac{2}{n}\right)}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification with $n = 6$ (Six Voussoirs in Semicircle).**

$$
\phi = 30°, \quad \alpha = 180° - 60° = 120°
$$

Each voussoir's wedge angle (intrados taper) is $120°$, leaving $60°$ as the supplementary closing angle of the wedge. ✓

**Step 5 — Practical Limit.**
As $n$ increases, $\alpha$ approaches $180°$ (a flat stone, no taper). Hence voussoir taper is meaningful only for small $n$.

## 3.4 KTU Numerical: Load on a Lintel (Equilateral Triangular Load Zone)

**Step 1 — Problem Statement.**
A lintel spans a clear opening of **1.5 m**. The wall above is **300 mm thick** and made of brick masonry (unit weight = 19 kN/m³). Calculate the load per metre run on the lintel due to the wall, assuming a 45° load dispersion.

**Step 2 — Geometry of the Triangular Load Zone.**

$$
\text{Height of triangle } (H_{load}) = \frac{L_{clear}}{2} = \frac{1.5}{2} = 0.75 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\text{Base of triangle} = L_{clear} = 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Sectional Area of the Triangular Load Zone.**

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.5 \times 0.75 = 0.5625 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Self-Weight of the Triangular Load Zone (per metre run of lintel).**

$$
W = A \times \text{unit weight} = 0.5625 \times 19 = 10.6875 \text{ kN/m} \approx 10.7 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation.**
The lintel must be designed to carry approximately **10.7 kN/m** UDL from the masonry above, plus its own self-weight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** Even for a small 1.5 m opening, the load on the lintel is significant. This is why lintels must always be designed, never assumed by rule of thumb.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Load Path Comparison (Lintel vs Arch)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph L[LINTEL Path - Bending]
        L1[Wall Load] --> L2[Triangular 45 Degree Load]
        L2 --> L3[Lintel Beam]
        L3 --> L4[Bending Moment plus Shear]
        L4 --> L5[Vertical Reaction at Jambs]
    end

    subgraph A[ARCH Path - Compression]
        A1[Wall Load] --> A2[Voussoir Compression]
        A2 --> A3[Diagonal Thrust Lines]
        A3 --> A4[Horizontal Thrust to Abutment]
        A4 --> A5[Vertical plus Horizontal Reaction]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Tip:** The key insight from the two side-by-side paths is that the **arch converts a vertical load into a diagonal compressive thrust**, while the **lintel converts a vertical load into bending and shear**. This is why the arch is so much more material-efficient for stone/brick construction.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`
**Define a lintel. List any four types of lintels commonly used in building construction.**

**Model Answer (Expected KTU Valuation):**

A lintel is a horizontal structural member of small width placed across the top of an opening (door, window, ventilator) in a wall to support the load of the masonry above and transfer it safely to the adjoining wall portions (jambs). It acts primarily as a flexural (bending) member.

**Four types of lintels:**

1. **Timber Lintel** — used in older buildings and hilly regions.
2. **Stone Lintel** — used in stone masonry buildings, suitable for low spans.
3. **Reinforced Brick Lintel** — bricks laid on edge with mild steel bars embedded in cement mortar; spans up to 2 m.
4. **RCC Lintel** — the most widely used modern lintel, cast in-situ or precast with main reinforcement at the bottom.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if the definition is precise AND four correctly named types are listed. A vague "supports the wall" without "flexural member" loses 0.5 mark.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]`
**What is a keystone? Explain its role in arch construction with a neat sketch description.**

**Model Answer (Expected KTU Valuation):**

A **keystone** is the central (top-most) voussoir of an arch, placed last during construction. It is typically shaped as a truncated wedge.

**Role of the Keystone:**

1. It **locks** all the voussoirs into position once placed, by virtue of its wedge action and the self-weight of the structure above.
2. It **distributes the thrust** from one side of the arch to the other uniformly.
3. It **maintains the arch's stability** against any tendency of the voussoirs to fall inward.
4. It is the last stone placed; the arch is said to be "closed" or "keyed" once the keystone is set, after which the centring (temporary support) can be removed.

The keystone is the **critical element** that transforms a set of loose voussoirs into a self-supporting arch.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** A diagram description (e.g., "wedge at top center of arch, between two adjacent voussoirs") is essential for full marks. Pure text without mentioning the wedge shape loses marks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Internal choice is given between two questions.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — CHOICE 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Explain the different types of lintels based on the material of construction, with sketches where applicable.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Lintels are classified into the following types based on material:

**1. Timber Lintel:**
- The oldest type of lintel, made of a single timber section or built-up timber.
- Used in hilly areas and older buildings.
- Disadvantages: Vulnerable to fire, decay, and white-ant attack. Not used in modern urban construction.

**2. Stone Lintel:**
- A simple stone slab or multiple stone pieces placed side-by-side.
- Used in stone masonry buildings, where stone is locally available.
- The thickness is generally 100 mm or more.
- Suitable only for short spans; cannot resist transverse stress efficiently.

**3. Brick Lintel:**
- Bricks are laid on edge (or on end) in cement mortar.
- Suitable for very small openings (≤ 1 m) and light loads.
- A relieving arch is sometimes provided above a brick lintel to divert heavy loads.
- The lintel is not reinforced; tension is resisted by the mortar.

**4. Reinforced Brick Lintel:**
- Bricks are laid on edge with mild steel bars (typically 8 mm to 12 mm diameter) embedded in cement mortar between consecutive brick courses.
- Suitable for spans up to about 2 m.
- The depth is greater than a plain brick lintel to accommodate the bars.
- Bars are placed at the bottom (tension zone) with adequate cover.

**5. RCC Lintel:**
- The most widely used lintel in modern construction.
- Cast in-situ (using formwork) or precast and hoisted into position.
- Main longitudinal bars at the bottom (tension reinforcement), with distribution steel and stirrups.
- Designed per IS 456:2000. Effective depth typically = span/12 to span/15.
- Bearing on each jamb: 150 mm to 300 mm.

**6. Steel Lintel:**
- Made of rolled steel sections (I-sections, channels, or built-up plate girders).
- Used for very large openings or heavy loads (e.g., industrial buildings, garages).
- Steel is strong in tension, making it ideal for large spans.
- Often provided with a brick/stone facing for aesthetic reasons.

---

**(b) What is an arch? Explain the various terms used in arch construction with the help of a labelled diagram.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
An arch is a structural member built of small wedge-shaped units (voussoirs) arranged in a curved form, where the units are held together by mutual compression. The arch transfers vertical loads above it obliquely to the supports (abutments) primarily through axial compression.

**Key Terms (with description):**

1. **Voussoirs:** Wedge-shaped units forming the body of the arch ring.
2. **Keystone:** The central voussoir at the crown; placed last; locks the arch.
3. **Crown:** The highest point on the extrados (outer curve) of the arch.
4. **Springing Point / Springer:** The first (lowest) voussoir of the arch, resting on the skewback.
5. **Springing Line:** An imaginary horizontal line joining the two springing points of an arch.
6. **Skewback:** The inclined bed joint on the abutment on which the arch rests.
7. **Abutment:** The end support of the arch; resists the horizontal thrust.
8. **Pier:** An intermediate vertical support between two adjacent arches.
9. **Haunch:** The lower intermediate portion of the arch between the springer and the crown.
10. **Spandrel:** The masonry wall portion above the extrados of the arch and within the rectangular outer profile of the structure.
11. **Intrados:** The inner (lower) curved surface of the arch.
12. **Extrados:** The outer (upper) curved surface of the arch.
13. **Rise (h):** Vertical distance from the springing line to the highest point of the intrados.
14. **Span (l):** Clear horizontal distance between the supports.

> [!TIP]
> **Sketch Requirement:** A clear labelled diagram of an arch showing at least **8 of the above terms** is essential for full marks in this part. KTU examiners typically award 2–3 marks purely for a well-labelled sketch.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — CHOICE 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Classify arches based on (i) shape, (ii) material, and (iii) number of centres. Give one example for each category.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Arches:**

**(i) Based on Shape:**

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| Semicircular (Roman) Arch | A full half-circle; rise = span/2 |
| Segmental Arch | A segment of a circle; rise = span/4 to span/8 |
| Pointed (Gothic) Arch | Two arcs meeting at a point at the crown |
| Equilateral Arch | Two arcs struck from the springings of the opposite arch |
| Stilted Arch | A semicircular arch with vertical extensions (stilts) below the center |
| Venetian Arch | Pointed arch with deeper crown and shallower rise |
| Florentine Arch | Decorative pointed arch with both intrados and extrados as arcs |
| Horseshoe (Moorish) Arch | More than a semicircle; the springers project inward |
| Relieving Arch | A flat arch built over a lintel to relieve the lintel of load |

**(ii) Based on Material of Construction:**

- **Brick Arch** — Built with bricks of special shapes (e.g., wedge-shaped bricks); used for moderate spans.
- **Stone Arch** — Built with dressed wedge-shaped stones; used in monumental/old structures.
- **Concrete Arch** — Plain or reinforced concrete; used in modern construction.

**(iii) Based on Number of Centres:**

- **One-Centered Arch** — Drawn with a single centre; e.g., semicircular, segmental.
- **Two-Centered Arch** — Drawn with two centres; e.g., pointed, equilateral.
- **Three-Centered Arch** — Drawn with three centres; e.g., elliptical.
- **Four-Centered Arch** — Drawn with four centres; e.g., Tudor arch.

---

**(b) A semicircular arch has a span of 4 m and carries a uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m (including self-weight) over the entire span. Calculate the horizontal thrust at the abutments and the vertical reaction at the supports.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

$$
l = 4 \text{ m}, \quad h = l/2 = 2 \text{ m}, \quad w = 20 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Vertical Reaction at Each Support (by symmetry):**

$$
V_A = V_B = \frac{w \cdot l}{2} = \frac{20 \times 4}{2} = 40 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Horizontal Thrust (using the standard formula):**

$$
H = \frac{w \cdot l^2}{8 h} = \frac{20 \times 4^2}{8 \times 2} = \frac{320}{16} = 20 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Resultant Reaction at Each Abutment:**

$$
R = \sqrt{V^2 + H^2} = \sqrt{40^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 400} = \sqrt{2000} \approx 44.72 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Angle of Resultant with Vertical:**

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{H}{V} = \frac{20}{40} = 0.5 \Rightarrow \theta \approx 26.57°
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Vertical Reaction $V_A = V_B = \mathbf{40 \text{ kN}}$
- Horizontal Thrust $H = \mathbf{20 \text{ kN}}$
- Resultant Reaction $R \approx \mathbf{44.72 \text{ kN}}$ at $\approx 26.57°$ to the vertical.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** A common mistake is forgetting to halve the rise ($h = l/2$ for semicircular). Students often mistakenly use $h = l$, leading to half the correct thrust value. Always verify the geometry before substituting.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Losing Marks (Lintels & Arches)**
>
> 1. **Confusing Intrados and Extrados** — Remember: EXtrados = EXternal (outer curve). A swapped label costs you 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to halve the rise in semicircular arches** — Many students use $h = l$ instead of $h = l/2$. This halves the computed horizontal thrust and is a fatal error.
> 3. **Omitting the sketch in arch questions** — KTU's marking scheme reserves 2–3 marks for a clearly labelled diagram. Never skip it.
> 4. **Listing lintel types without mentioning the use/limitation** — Just naming them is half-credit. Always add one line on where each is used.
> 5. **Mixing up "springing line" and "springer"** — Springing line is an imaginary horizontal line; Springer is the actual first voussoir. Examiners test this distinction.
> 6. **Forgetting to write the units** in numerical problems — Always write **kN** or **kN/m**; missing units lose 0.5 mark.
> 7. **Not specifying the load dispersion assumption (45°)** in lintel load calculations — State the assumption explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Lintel** = horizontal flexural member over an opening; acts in **bending** + shear.
- [x] **Arch** = curved compression member; voussoirs are wedge-shaped units held by **mutual compression**.
- [x] Standard **lintel bearing** = **100–300 mm** (minimum 100 mm).
- [x] Effective span of lintel = **least of (clear span + effective depth) or center-to-center of supports** (IS 456:2000).
- [x] **RCC lintel** depth ≈ Span/12 (simply supported, IS 456 guidelines).
- [x] **Load dispersion assumption** for lintel design = **45° triangular zone** above the lintel.
- [x] **Keystone** = central voussoir; placed LAST; locks the arch.
- [x] **Intrados = inner (lower) curve; Extrados = outer (upper) curve.**
- [x] **Springer** = first voussoir; **Springing Line** = imaginary horizontal line joining both springers.
- [x] **Abutment** = end support of arch; resists **horizontal thrust**.
- [x] **Pier** = intermediate support between two arches.
- [x] **Spandrel** = wall area above the extrados within the rectangular outer profile.
- [x] **Semicircular arch** rise = **l/2**; **Segmental arch** rise = **l/4 to l/8**.
- [x] **Horizontal thrust** formula: $H = w l^2 / (8h)$ for a semicircular arch with UDL $w$/m.
- [x] **Flatter arch ⇒ larger horizontal thrust ⇒ massive abutments required.**
- [x] Arches classified by **shape** (semicircular, segmental, pointed, horseshoe, etc.), **material** (brick, stone, concrete), **number of centres** (one to four), and **construction** (rough, ashlar, gauged).
- [x] **Pointed (Gothic) arch** = formed by two intersecting arcs.
- [x] **Equilateral arch** = two arcs struck from the OPPOSITE springings; forms an equilateral triangle.
- [x] **Stilted arch** = semicircular arch with vertical extensions below.
- [x] **Relieving arch** = built over a lintel to transfer heavy masonry loads off the lintel.
- [x] **Voussoir taper angle** for a semicircular arch with $n$ voussoirs: $\alpha = 180°(1 - 2/n)$.
- [x] The **line of thrust** must lie within the middle third of the arch ring to avoid tension (masonry rule).
- [x] **Lintels** = modern, fast, easy; **Arches** = material-efficient in compression, allow stone/brick construction, but require skilled labour and centring.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tall Buildings - Framed Building - Steel and Concrete Frame - Structural Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET611 - INTRODUCTION TO CONSTRUCTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tall Buildings - Framed Building - Steel and Concrete Frame - Structural Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Definition of a Tall Building

According to the **Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat (CTBUH)** — the globally accepted body for high-rise classification — a **Tall Building** is defined as a structure in which the height significantly influences its structural design, planning, construction, and economic viability. Quantitatively, a building is classified as "tall" if it has **14 or more storeys** or a height of **≥ 50 meters**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CTBUH Height Classification (Industry Standard Metric):**
> - **Tall Building:** Height ≥ **14 storeys** or **≥ 50 m**
> - **Supertall Building:** Height ≥ **300 m**
> - **Megatall Building:** Height ≥ **600 m**
> - The Burj Khalifa (828 m) and Taipei 101 (508 m) are classic KTU-classification references.

In the Indian Standard (IS) context, the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)** classifies buildings above **15 m** (without stilts) and **18 m** (with stilts) as **High-Rise Buildings**, triggering mandatory provisions for fire safety, structural redundancy, and lateral load design.

## 1.2 Definition of a Framed Building

A **Framed Building** is a structural system in which a three-dimensional skeleton (or "frame") composed of interconnected **beams, columns, and slabs** carries all the imposed loads — both vertical (gravity) and lateral (wind, seismic). The frame transfers the loads through the beams to the columns and finally to the foundation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — Load Path in a Framed Building:**
> Slab → Beam → Column → Foundation → Soil
> This unidirectional flow is the defining behavioral characteristic of a framed structure.

## 1.3 Steel Frame vs. Concrete Frame

| Parameter | Steel Frame | Concrete Frame |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| Material | Rolled structural steel sections (IS 800:2007) | Reinforced Cement Concrete (IS 456:2000) |
| Primary Advantage | High strength-to-weight ratio, ductility, speed of construction | High mass, fire resistance, mouldability, low cost |
| Typical Use | Skyscrapers, industrial buildings | Residential, commercial, low-to-mid rise |
| Connections | Welded / Bolted | Monolithic (cast in-situ) or Precast |

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Skeleton"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a tall building exactly like the **human skeleton**.
> - The **columns** are your leg bones — they carry the gravity (vertical weight of the body).
> - The **beams** are your arm and rib bones — they transfer the load from the floors (skin/muscle) to the legs.
> - The **braces and shear walls** are the **muscles and tendons** that hold the skeleton together when the wind blows (lateral force).
> - The **foundation** is your feet — connecting the entire structure firmly to the ground.
>
> Just as a tall human needs stronger muscles and a stiffer spine to resist swaying, a tall building needs a robust lateral load-resisting system. This is the **fundamental "Why"** behind the evolution of structural systems for tall buildings.

## 1.5 Why "Framed Building" Alone is Insufficient for Tall Structures

As building height increases, the **lateral loads (wind + earthquake)** grow exponentially (approximately as a square of the height for wind), while vertical loads grow only **linearly**. Therefore, a simple moment-resisting frame becomes **economically and structurally inefficient** beyond a certain height, necessitating specialized structural systems (covered in Section 2).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Load Classification in Tall Buildings

Tall buildings are subjected to **two fundamental load categories**, which dictate the choice of the structural system:

### 2.1.1 Vertical (Gravity) Loads
- **Dead Load (DL):** Self-weight of structural and permanent non-structural components.
- **Live Load (LL):** Imposed loads due to occupancy (IS 875 Part 2).
- These loads are **gravity-driven** and their magnitude increases **linearly** with the number of storeys.

### 2.1.2 Lateral Loads
- **Wind Load (WL):** Governs design in most coastal and high-wind zones (IS 875 Part 3).
- **Earthquake (Seismic) Load (EL):** Governs design in **Seismic Zones III, IV, and V** of India (IS 1893 Part 1: 2016).
- These loads grow **non-linearly** (wind ≈ height²; seismic base shear ∝ weight, but moment ∝ height²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical "Height Threshold" Concept:**
> For buildings up to ~15 storeys, **vertical loads dominate** the design. Beyond ~15–20 storeys, **lateral loads dominate**, and the structural system must be specifically engineered to resist overturning moments and lateral drift.

## 2.2 Structural Systems for Tall Buildings (The Core Syllabus Content)

A **Structural System** in a tall building refers to the engineered arrangement of frames, walls, braces, and outriggers that together resist all applied loads within permissible serviceability limits.

### 2.2.1 Rigid Moment-Resisting Frame System
- The most fundamental system, consisting of **beams and columns rigidly connected** at the joints.
- Resists lateral loads through **flexural (bending) action** of beams and columns.
- **Limitation:** Excessive lateral drift in tall buildings (governed by $H/250$ to $H/500$ limits).

### 2.2.2 Braced Frame System
- Diagonal bracing members (steel angles, tubes) are introduced in the frame.
- Resists lateral loads through **axial (truss) action** of the braces.
- **Greatly increases lateral stiffness** without adding significant mass.
- Commonly used in **steel-framed tall buildings**.

### 2.2.3 Shear Wall System
- Reinforced concrete walls (typically 150–400 mm thick) are cast in the building.
- Acts as a **vertical cantilever** fixed at the base.
- Resists lateral loads through **in-plane shear and flexure**.

### 2.2.4 Shear Truss / Mega Frame System
- A **mega-scale frame** (where the entire building acts as a single beam-column assembly) is used.
- Reinforced concrete or steel mega-columns and mega-beams are used.
- Common in supertall structures like the **Shanghai World Financial Center**.

### 2.2.5 Outrigger System
- A central reinforced concrete core is connected to the exterior columns by stiff **outrigger trusses** (usually at one or two levels).
- Converts the core's bending into an **axial tension-compression couple** in the exterior columns.
- **Efficient and economical** for 30–70 storey buildings.

### 2.2.6 Tubular Systems (Fazlur Rahman Khan's Revolution)
A revolutionary system conceived by **Fazlur Rahman Khan** (Bangladesh-born American engineer) that revolutionized tall building design.

- **Framed Tube:** Closely spaced exterior columns tied with deep spandrel beams, forming a perforated tube (e.g., **John Hancock Center, Chicago**).
- **Braced Tube:** Diagonal braces on the exterior face (e.g., **John Hancock Center, Chicago**).
- **Bundled Tube:** Multiple tubes bundled together (e.g., **Willis Tower / Sears Tower, Chicago**).
- **Tube-in-Tube:** A central core tube combined with an exterior framed tube (e.g., **One Shell Plaza, Houston**).

### 2.2.7 Core and Outrigger Hybrid
Combines the efficiency of a reinforced concrete central core with perimeter mega-columns connected by outriggers — the **most common modern system** for buildings of 50–80 storeys.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examination — Master These:**

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$ | Wind pressure at height $z$ (IS 875 Part 3) | N/m² |
| 2 | $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$ | Design wind speed at height $z$ | m/s |
| 3 | $F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$ | Wind force on structural element | N |
| 4 | $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ | Seismic design base shear (IS 1893) | kN |
| 5 | $A_h = \dfrac{Z}{2} \cdot \dfrac{I}{R} \cdot \dfrac{S_a}{g}$ | Design horizontal acceleration coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T = 0.075 \cdot h^{0.75}$ (RC frame) | Empirical fundamental time period (RC MRF) | seconds |
| 7 | $T = 0.085 \cdot h^{0.75}$ (Steel frame) | Empirical fundamental time period (Steel MRF) | seconds |
| 8 | $T = \dfrac{0.09 \cdot h}{\sqrt{d}}$ (All other systems) | Empirical time period (shear wall, braced, etc.) | seconds |
| 9 | $\Delta = \dfrac{H}{250}$ to $\dfrac{H}{500}$ | Maximum permissible storey drift (IS 1893) | mm |
| 10 | $M_{overturning} = V_B \cdot \bar{h}$ | Overturning moment due to base shear | kN·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Key for Formulas (Use in Board Exam Answers):**
> - $V_b$ = Basic wind speed (m/s); $k_1$ = Risk coefficient; $k_2$ = Terrain, height & structure size factor; $k_3$ = Topography factor; $k_4$ = Importance factor for cyclonic region
> - $Z$ = Zone factor; $I$ = Importance factor; $R$ = Response reduction factor; $S_a/g$ = Spectral acceleration coefficient
> - $W$ = Seismic weight of the building; $h$ = Height of building (m); $d$ = Base dimension in considered direction (m)
> - $C_f$ = Force coefficient; $A_e$ = Effective frontal area; $H$ = Total height

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where these systems are used in industry:**
> - **Moment Frames:** Low-to-mid rise commercial buildings (up to 15 storeys).
> - **Braced Steel Frames:** Industrial structures, stadiums, airports (e.g., IGI Airport T3, Delhi).
> - **Shear Walls + Core:** All modern residential and commercial high-rises in India.
> - **Outrigger Systems:** Tata Consultancy Services (TCS) towers, Brigade Tech Park (Bangalore).
> - **Tubular Systems:** World Trade Center (New York, original), Burj Khalifa (Dubai), Petronas Towers (Malaysia).
> - **Mega Frames:** Taipei 101, Shanghai Tower.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Seismic Base Shear (As per IS 1893 Part 1: 2016)

The **total design lateral force** (base shear) at the base of a building during an earthquake is derived as follows. This is a **high-weightage derivation** for KTU Board Exams.

### Step 1: Statement of the Governing Equation

The total design lateral force (base shear) $V_B$ experienced by a building due to seismic ground motion is given by:

$$
V_B = A_h \cdot W
$$

Where $A_h$ is the **design horizontal acceleration coefficient** (dimensionless) and $W$ is the **seismic weight** of the building (sum of dead load + applicable live load).

### Step 2: Expand the Horizontal Acceleration Coefficient

The coefficient $A_h$ is defined as the product of the **zone factor**, **importance factor**, **response reduction factor**, and the **average spectral acceleration**:

$$
A_h = \dfrac{Z}{2} \cdot \dfrac{I}{R} \cdot \dfrac{S_a}{g}
$$

### Step 3: Explain Each Multiplier

- **Z (Zone Factor):** Represents the severity of the design earthquake in the region.
  - Zone II: $Z = 0.10$ | Zone III: $Z = 0.16$ | Zone IV: $Z = 0.24$ | Zone V: $Z = 0.36$
- **I (Importance Factor):** $I = 1.2$ for hospitals, schools, heritage; $I = 1.0$ for all others.
- **R (Response Reduction Factor):** Represents the structure's ductility and energy dissipation capacity.
  - $R = 5.0$ for **SMRF** (Special Moment Resisting Frame — RC).
  - $R = 4.0$ for **OMRF** (Ordinary Moment Resisting Frame).
  - $R = 3.0$ for **Shear Wall** buildings.
  - $R = 5.0$ for **Steel SMRF**.
- **$S_a/g$:** Spectral acceleration normalized by gravity, read from the **response spectrum curve** (IS 1893 Fig. 2) as a function of the fundamental time period $T$ and soil type (rocky/stiff/soft).

### Step 4: Vertical Distribution of Base Shear

The total $V_B$ is distributed along the building height using the empirical formula:

$$
Q_i = V_B \cdot \dfrac{W_i \cdot h_i^2}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \cdot h_j^2}
$$

Where $Q_i$ is the design lateral force at floor $i$, $W_i$ is the seismic weight of floor $i$, and $h_i$ is the height of floor $i$ from the base.

### Step 5: Vertical Distribution (Alternative — Codified Triangular)

For a perfectly uniform building (constant mass and stiffness per storey), this simplifies to a **triangular distribution**:

$$
Q_i = V_B \cdot \dfrac{W_i \cdot h_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \cdot h_j}
$$

This is the standard distribution used in most introductory KTU Board Exam questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Wind Pressure at Height $z$ (As per IS 875 Part 3)

### Step 1: Start with the Design Wind Velocity

The design wind speed at any height $z$ above mean ground level is:

$$
V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Dynamic Pressure Equation

Wind exerts a **dynamic pressure** proportional to the square of its velocity (Bernoulli's principle for incompressible flow):

$$
p_z = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V_z^2
$$

Where $\rho$ is the air density (≈ $1.2$ kg/m³ at standard atmospheric conditions).

### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Constant for Indian Conditions

For Indian metrological conditions, the constant $\dfrac{1}{2} \rho$ evaluates to $0.6$ when $V_z$ is in m/s and $p_z$ is in N/m²:

$$
p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2
$$

### Step 4: Total Wind Force on Structure

The total wind force on the building (or any structural element) is:

$$
F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z
$$

Where $C_f$ is the **force coefficient** (depends on shape, aspect ratio, solidity ratio) and $A_e$ is the **effective frontal area** exposed to wind.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice the **square relationship** $p_z \propto V_z^2$. This is the fundamental reason why wind load design **dominates** in very tall buildings — doubling the height increases the design wind pressure dramatically, especially in the upper levels.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU Board Exam Practice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 12-storey reinforced concrete office building is located in **Zone IV** with the following data:
> - Total height: $h = 36$ m
> - Seismic weight per storey: $W_i = 1200$ kN
> - Soil type: Medium soil
> - Importance factor: $I = 1.0$
> - Response reduction factor: $R = 5.0$ (SMRF)
> - Plan dimension in considered direction: $d = 20$ m
>
> **Find:** (a) Fundamental time period, (b) Design base shear, (c) Lateral force at roof level.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Fundamental Time Period (RC SMRF)**
$$
T = 0.075 \cdot h^{0.75}
$$

$$
T = 0.075 \cdot (36)^{0.75}
$$

$$
T = 0.075 \cdot 15.33 \approx 1.15 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2: Spectral Acceleration ($S_a/g$) for Medium Soil, $T = 1.15$ s**

From IS 1893 Fig. 2 (Medium soil response spectrum):
$$
\dfrac{S_a}{g} \approx 1.10 \quad \text{(read from the curve)}
$$

**Step 3: Horizontal Acceleration Coefficient**
$$
A_h = \dfrac{Z}{2} \cdot \dfrac{I}{R} \cdot \dfrac{S_a}{g}
$$

$$
A_h = \dfrac{0.24}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1.0}{5.0} \cdot 1.10
$$

$$
A_h = 0.12 \cdot 0.20 \cdot 1.10 = 0.0264
$$

**Step 4: Total Seismic Weight**
$$
W = n \cdot W_i = 12 \cdot 1200 = 14400 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5: Design Base Shear**
$$
V_B = A_h \cdot W = 0.0264 \cdot 14400
$$

$$
V_B = 380.16 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6: Lateral Force at Roof (Storey 12)**

Using the triangular distribution $Q_i = V_B \cdot \dfrac{W_i \cdot h_i}{\sum W_j \cdot h_j}$:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{12} W_j \cdot h_j = 1200 \cdot (3 + 6 + 9 + 12 + ... + 36) = 1200 \cdot 234 = 280800 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
Q_{12} = 380.16 \cdot \dfrac{1200 \cdot 36}{280800} = 380.16 \cdot 0.1538 \approx 58.5 \text{ kN}
$$

This is the lateral force at the roof level that the structural engineer must design the upper columns and beams to resist.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Structural Systems for Tall Buildings

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of structural systems, mapping how each system resists lateral loads differently.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TALL BUILDING STRUCTURAL SYSTEM] --> B[LOAD TYPES]
    A --> C[STRUCTURAL SYSTEM TYPES]

    B --> B1[Vertical Gravity Loads - DL, LL]
    B --> B2[Lateral Loads - Wind, Seismic]

    C --> D1[Rigid Moment Resisting Frame]
    C --> D2[Braced Frame System]
    C --> D3[Shear Wall System]
    C --> D4[Outrigger and Belt Truss System]
    C --> D5[Mega Frame and Super Frame System]
    C --> D6[Tubular System Family]
    C --> D7[Core and Outrigger Hybrid]

    D6 --> E1[Framed Tube]
    D6 --> E2[Braced Tube]
    D6 --> E3[Bundled Tube]
    D6 --> E4[Tube in Tube]

    D1 --> F1[Effective up to 15 storeys]
    D2 --> F2[Effective up to 30 storeys]
    D3 --> F3[Effective up to 50 storeys]
    D4 --> F4[Effective up to 70 storeys]
    D6 --> F5[Effective up to 110 storeys supertall]
    D7 --> F6[Effective up to 80 storeys modern highrise]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    style D2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
    style D3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
    style D4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
    style D5 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
    style D6 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
    style D7 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
    style E1 fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#1f3a0a,stroke-width:1px
    style E2 fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#1f3a0a,stroke-width:1px
    style E3 fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#1f3a0a,stroke-width:1px
    style E4 fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#1f3a0a,stroke-width:1px
    style F1 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#3a2f0a,stroke-width:1px
    style F2 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#3a2f0a,stroke-width:1px

    style F4 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#3a2f0a,stroke-width:1px
    style F5 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#3a2f0a,stroke-width:1px
    style F6 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#3a2f0a,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram — Lateral Load Path in a Framed Building

The following diagram maps the **load transfer mechanism** for both steel and concrete framed buildings under lateral loading.

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Wind or Earthquake Lateral Load Applied] --> N1[Acts on Exterior Façade and Floors]
    N1 --> N2[Transferred to Floor Diaphragm Slab]
    N2 --> P1{Frame Type}
    P1 --> P2[Steel Frame: Lateral Load to Braced bays or Moment Connections]
    P1 --> P3[Concrete Frame: Lateral Load to Moment Connections and Shear Walls]

    P2 --> M1[Resisted by Axial Action of Braces]
    M1 --> M2[Transferred to Steel Columns]
    M2 --> M3[Transferred to Base Plate and Anchor Bolts]
    M3 --> M4[Resisted by Foundation Mat or Pile Cap]
    M4 --> M5[Transferred to Subsoil Strata]

    P3 --> Q1[Resisted by Flexure of RC Beams and Columns]
    Q1 --> Q2[Shear Walls add cantilever action]
    Q2 --> Q3[Transferred to RC Columns and Shear Walls]
    Q3 --> Q4[Transferred to Isolated or Combined Footing]
    Q4 --> Q5[Transferred to Subsoil Strata]

    style L1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#3a0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style N1 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#3a1f0a,stroke-width:1px
    style N2 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#3a1f0a,stroke-width:1px
    style P1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    style P3 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    style M1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
    style M2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
    style M3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
    style M4 fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#1f3a0a,stroke-width:1px
    style M5 fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#1f3a0a,stroke-width:1px
    style Q1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
    style Q2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
    style Q3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
    style Q4 fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#1f3a0a,stroke-width:1px
    style Q5 fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#1f3a0a,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Steel vs Concrete Frame Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    S1[STEEL FRAMED BUILDING] --> S2[Rolled Steel I Sections for Beams and Columns]
    S2 --> S3[Bolted or Welded Connections]
    S3 --> S4[Steel Bracings in Lateral Direction]
    S4 --> S5[Lightweight Composite Floor Slabs]
    S5 --> S6[Low Self Weight - Reduced Seismic Mass]
    S6 --> S7[Faster Construction Speed]

    C1[CONCRETE FRAMED BUILDING] --> C2[RC Beams and RC Columns Cast Monolithically]
    C2 --> C3[Monolithic Beam Column Joints]
    C3 --> C4[RC Shear Walls in Lift Cores and Stairwells]
    C4 --> C5[RC Slabs Cast in situ]
    C5 --> C6[High Self Weight - Increased Seismic Mass]
    C6 --> C7[Slower Construction Speed but Economical]

    style S1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style S2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:1px
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a tall building as per CTBUH. List the four categories of height classification with their respective height thresholds.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

According to the **Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat (CTBUH)**, a **tall building** is defined as a multi-storey structure in which the height significantly influences its structural design, planning, and economic viability.

**CTBUH Height Classification:**
1. **Tall Building** — Height $\geq 14$ storeys or $\geq 50$ m
2. **Supertall Building** — Height $\geq 300$ m
3. **Megatall Building** — Height $\geq 600$ m

The fourth implicit category is **Mid-Rise Buildings** (5–14 storeys) which do not require specialized high-rise design provisions.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between a steel frame and a concrete frame in tall buildings (any three differences).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Sl. No. | Steel Frame | Concrete Frame |
|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | Made of rolled steel sections (IS 800:2007) | Made of reinforced cement concrete (IS 456:2000) |
| 2 | High strength-to-weight ratio; lower self-weight | High self-weight; contributes to higher seismic mass |
| 3 | Connections are bolted or welded | Connections are monolithic (cast in-situ) |
| 4 | Faster construction; suitable for very tall structures | Economical for low-to-mid rise; high fire resistance |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **structural systems** used in tall buildings, with neat sketches and the principle of load transfer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 10-storey RCC framed office building is located in **Zone III** on **medium soil**. The total height of the building is 30 m, and the plan dimension in the considered direction of lateral load is 18 m. The seismic weight per floor is 1500 kN. Take $I = 1.0$, $R = 5.0$. Determine:
- (i) The fundamental time period of the building.
- (ii) The design seismic base shear.
- (iii) The lateral force at the roof level.

Assume $S_a/g = 1.20$ for the calculated time period. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Structural Systems (7 Marks)

A **structural system** in a tall building refers to the integrated arrangement of beams, columns, walls, braces, and other load-resisting elements designed to transfer all vertical and lateral loads safely to the foundation. The major structural systems for tall buildings are:

1. **Rigid Moment-Resisting Frame System:**
   - The basic system using beams and columns rigidly connected at joints.
   - Resists lateral loads through **flexural action** of beams and columns.
   - Limited by excessive drift in buildings above 15 storeys.

2. **Braced Frame System:**
   - Diagonal steel braces are introduced between beams and columns.
   - Resists lateral loads through **axial (truss) action**.
   - Highly efficient and commonly used in steel tall buildings.

3. **Shear Wall System:**
   - Vertical reinforced concrete walls act as **vertical cantilevers** fixed at the base.
   - Resists lateral loads through **in-plane shear and bending**.

4. **Outrigger and Belt Truss System:**
   - Central core connected to exterior columns via **stiff outrigger trusses** (usually at one or two storeys).
   - Converts core bending into **axial couple in columns**, increasing stiffness.

5. **Tubular Systems (Fazlur Khan's Innovation):**
   - **Framed Tube:** Closely spaced exterior columns with deep spandrel beams (e.g., John Hancock Center).
   - **Braced Tube:** Diagonal exterior bracing (e.g., John Hancock Center).
   - **Bundled Tube:** Multiple tubes bundled (e.g., Willis Tower, Chicago).
   - **Tube-in-Tube:** Central core + exterior framed tube (e.g., One Shell Plaza, Houston).

6. **Mega Frame / Super Frame System:**
   - Building acts as a single mega beam-column assembly.
   - Used in supertall buildings like Taipei 101.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $n = 10$ storeys
- $h = 30$ m
- $W_i = 1500$ kN (per floor)
- Zone III → $Z = 0.16$
- $I = 1.0$, $R = 5.0$
- $d = 18$ m
- Soil: Medium
- $S_a/g = 1.20$

**Step (i): Fundamental Time Period**

Since the building is an **RC frame**, we use:
$$
T = 0.075 \cdot h^{0.75}
$$

$$
T = 0.075 \cdot (30)^{0.75}
$$

$$
T = 0.075 \cdot 12.82
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 0.96 \text{ seconds}}
$$

**Step (ii): Design Horizontal Acceleration Coefficient**
$$
A_h = \dfrac{Z}{2} \cdot \dfrac{I}{R} \cdot \dfrac{S_a}{g}
$$

$$
A_h = \dfrac{0.16}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1.0}{5.0} \cdot 1.20
$$

$$
A_h = 0.08 \cdot 0.20 \cdot 1.20 = 0.0192
$$

**Total Seismic Weight**
$$
W = 10 \cdot 1500 = 15000 \text{ kN}
$$

**Design Base Shear**
$$
V_B = A_h \cdot W = 0.0192 \cdot 15000
$$

$$
\boxed{V_B = 288 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step (iii): Lateral Force at Roof Level**

Floor heights (assuming 3 m per floor): $h_1=3, h_2=6, h_3=9, \dots, h_{10}=30$ m
$$
\sum_{j=1}^{10} W_j \cdot h_j = 1500 \cdot (3 + 6 + 9 + 12 + 15 + 18 + 21 + 24 + 27 + 30)
$$

$$
= 1500 \cdot 165 = 247500 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Lateral force at roof (storey 10):
$$
Q_{10} = V_B \cdot \dfrac{W_{10} \cdot h_{10}}{\sum W_j \cdot h_j}
$$

$$
Q_{10} = 288 \cdot \dfrac{1500 \cdot 30}{247500}
$$

$$
Q_{10} = 288 \cdot 0.1818
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{10} \approx 52.4 \text{ kN}}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **rigid frame system** and **braced frame system** in detail. Compare their efficiency for tall buildings. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **wind pressure and total wind force** on a 60 m tall building located in **Coimbatore** (basic wind speed $V_b = 39$ m/s) with the following data:
- Terrain category: Category 2 (Class B structure)
- Building class: Class B
- $k_1 = 1.0$, $k_3 = 1.0$, $k_4 = 1.0$
- $k_2$ at $z = 60$ m for Class B, Category 2 is **1.06**
- Force coefficient $C_f = 1.2$
- Effective frontal area $A_e = 1800$ m²

Determine the wind pressure at $z = 60$ m and the total wind force. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Frame Systems Comparison (7 Marks)

**Rigid Moment-Resisting Frame System:**
- A three-dimensional frame of beams and columns with **rigid (moment-resisting) joints**.
- Resists lateral loads through the **flexural (bending) action** of beams and columns, which develop **bending moments and shear forces** at the joints.
- Joints are designed to transfer moments; no diagonal braces or shear walls are used.
- **Advantages:** Open floor plan flexibility; no obstruction to doors and windows.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited lateral stiffness; excessive drift in buildings above 15 storeys; inefficient for very tall buildings.

**Braced Frame System:**
- Diagonal **bracing members** (steel angles, tubes, or channels) are introduced between beams and columns.
- Resists lateral loads through the **axial (tension/compression) action** of braces, behaving like a vertical cantilever truss.
- **Advantages:** Very high lateral stiffness with minimal added weight; economical for buildings up to 30 storeys.
- **Disadvantages:** Bracing may obstruct doors, windows, and circulation paths.

**Comparison for Tall Buildings:**

| Parameter | Rigid Frame | Braced Frame |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Load resisting mechanism | Flexural (bending) | Axial (truss) |
| Stiffness | Low | Very high |
| Suitable height | Up to 15 storeys | Up to 30 storeys |
| Architectural obstruction | None | Braces may obstruct |
| Material economy | Higher steel/RC usage | Economical |

For tall buildings, the **braced frame system is more efficient** because it uses the high axial stiffness of steel members to provide greater lateral resistance with less material.

#### Part (b) — Wind Load Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $V_b = 39$ m/s
- $k_1 = 1.0$, $k_2 = 1.06$, $k_3 = 1.0$, $k_4 = 1.0$
- $C_f = 1.2$
- $A_e = 1800$ m²
- $z = 60$ m

**Step 1: Design Wind Speed at $z = 60$ m**
$$
V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4
$$

$$
V_z = 39 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.06 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0
$$

$$
V_z = 41.34 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Wind Pressure at $z = 60$ m**
$$
p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2
$$

$$
p_z = 0.6 \cdot (41.34)^2
$$

$$
p_z = 0.6 \cdot 1709.0
$$

$$
\boxed{p_z \approx 1025.4 \text{ N/m}^2 \; \approx 1.025 \text{ kN/m}^2}
$$

**Step 3: Total Wind Force on Building**
$$
F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z
$$

$$
F = 1.2 \cdot 1800 \cdot 1025.4
$$

$$
F = 2,215,488 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F \approx 2215.5 \text{ kN} \; \approx 2.22 \text{ MN}}
$$

This enormous force (equivalent to the weight of ~2,215 tonnes!) clearly demonstrates why **wind load design is critical** for tall buildings and why simple rigid frames are insufficient — a properly designed **braced frame or shear wall system** is essential.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the factor "0.6" in the wind pressure formula** — it is specific to IS 875 Part 3 and Indian metrological conditions, not a universal constant.
> 2. **Using the wrong time period formula** — there are three different formulas (RC MRF, Steel MRF, and "all other" systems). Mixing them up leads to **direct loss of 2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing R values for SMRF and OMRF** — for RC SMRF, $R = 5.0$; for OMRF, $R = 3.0$. The difference dramatically changes the base shear.
> 4. **Not stating the load path explicitly** in structural system questions — always write: "Slab → Beam → Column → Foundation" for vertical loads and "Floor diaphragm → Shear wall/Brace → Column → Foundation" for lateral loads.
> 5. **Omitting the unit in the final answer** — KTU examiners often deduct ½ mark for missing units like kN, m/s, N/m².

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize This Section:**

✅ **CTBUH Definitions:** Tall = ≥ 14 storeys/50 m; Supertall = ≥ 300 m; Megatall = ≥ 600 m.

✅ **NBC 2016 Definition:** High-Rise = > 15 m (without stilts) / > 18 m (with stilts).

✅ **Load Dominance Rule:** Up to 15 storeys → vertical loads dominate; above 15 storeys → lateral loads dominate.

✅ **The "0.6" Constant:** $p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$ — Indian standard wind pressure formula (IS 875 Part 3).

✅ **Time Period Formulas (Memorize All 3):**
- RC Frame (SMRF/OMRF): $T = 0.075 h^{0.75}$
- Steel Frame: $T = 0.085 h^{0.75}$
- Shear Wall / Braced / All others: $T = 0.09 h / \sqrt{d}$

✅ **Base Shear:** $V_B = A_h \cdot W$; $A_h = (Z/2) \cdot (I/R) \cdot (S_a/g)$.

✅ **Zone Factors (Z):** Zone II = 0.10; Zone III = 0.16; Zone IV = 0.24; Zone V = 0.36.

✅ **Response Reduction (R):** RC SMRF = 5.0; OMRF = 3.0; Shear Wall = 3.0; Steel SMRF = 5.0.

✅ **Drift Limit:** $\Delta \leq H/250$ (IS 1893) — controls stiffness design.

✅ **Tubular Systems Inventor:** Fazlur Rahman Khan (1960s revolution).

✅ **Famous Examples:** Bundled Tube = Willis Tower (Sears Tower); Braced Tube = John Hancock Center; Core + Outrigger = One Shell Plaza; Mega Frame = Taipei 101.

✅ **Wind vs. Seismic:** Wind governs in **coastal and high-wind zones**; Seismic governs in **high seismic zones (III, IV, V)**.

✅ **Structural System Selection Rule of Thumb:**
- Up to 15 storeys → **Moment Frame**
- 15–30 storeys → **Braced Frame / Shear Wall**
- 30–50 storeys → **Shear Wall + Outrigger**
- 50–80 storeys → **Core + Outrigger + Mega Frame**
- 80+ storeys → **Tubular System** (Framed/Braced/Bundled Tube)

✅ **Load Path to Always Write in Board Exams:** Floor Diaphragm → Vertical Lateral Element (Wall/Brace) → Column → Foundation → Soil.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Erection of Steel Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET611 - INTRODUCTION TO CONSTRUCTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Erection of Steel Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Erection of Steel Work** is the on-site assembly, lifting, positioning, aligning, and final fixing (by bolting, riveting, or welding) of prefabricated structural steel members — such as columns, beams, girders, trusses, and bracings — into a stable, plumb, and load-bearing framework, as per the approved fabrication drawings and the project's method statement.

In the language of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECET611)**, erection is treated as the *third and most safety-critical phase* of the steel construction cycle, following (1) Design and (2) Fabrication in the shop. The on-site activity transforms **discrete shop-fabricated components** into a **monolithic structural system** capable of resisting the design dead, live, wind, and seismic loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Erection of Steel Work covers *planning, plant selection, sequence of erection, site preparation, methods of hoisting, temporary bracing, alignment, plumbing, and field connections*. Mastery of the *sequence* and *plant-selection logic* is the single most repeated testing point in KTU ESE papers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steel Skeleton" Metaphor

Imagine you are assembling a **giant 3D Meccano / LEGO set**, but every piece weighs anywhere from a few kilograms to several **tonnes**, and once placed, the pieces must stand on their own while you fit the next one. The "instructions booklet" is the **erection drawing / shop drawing**, the "lifting tool" is a **crane or derrick**, and the "glue" is replaced by **high-strength friction-grip bolts (HSFG)** or **field welds**. A single mistake — lifting a beam from the wrong point, omitting a temporary brace, or misaligning a column by even **25 mm** — can cause the entire skeleton to **collapse like a house of cards**. This is precisely why KTU examiners place heavy weight on the *planning* and *safety* aspects of erection.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Member** | An individual steel component (column, beam, rafter). |
| **Erection Mark / Piece Mark** | A unique alphanumeric ID painted on each member in the shop to identify its final position. |
| **Plumbing** | The process of making a vertical member perfectly vertical using a theodolite, plumb bob, or laser. |
| **Raking Shore / Push-pull Prop** | A temporary inclined member used to hold a column plumb until permanent connections are made. |
| **Crane / Derrick** | The principal lifting equipment (mobile, crawler, tower, or goliath). |
| **Falsework / Temporary Bracing** | Temporary supports that hold the structure stable during erection. |
| **Field Connection** | The on-site joint — usually bolted (bearing-type or HSFG) or welded. |
| **Splice** | A joint between two abutting lengths of the same member (e.g., column-to-column). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Metrics used throughout KTU answers:**
> - Standard column-to-column vertical tolerance: **± 3 mm per storey**, max **25 mm cumulative**.
> - Standard bolt-tightening torque for M20 HSFG / 8.8 grade: **≈ 478 N·m** (depends on $\ k $-value).
> - Typical web-bolt clearance hole: **diameter + 1.5 mm to 2.0 mm**.
> - Standard wind speed cut-off for crane operations: **> 50 km/h ⇒ erection suspended**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Steel Erection (Exam-Oriented Framework)

Every KTU answer on steel erection can be organized under three pillars:

### Pillar A — Pre-Erection (Planning & Site Readiness)
1. **Study of drawings:** General Arrangement (GA), erection marks, and shop details.
2. **Erection Scheme / Method Statement:** Sequence of lifting, crane positions, sling arrangements, and counter-weight calculations.
3. **Site Survey:** Bearing capacity of soil for crane outriggers, ground slope, overhead obstructions (HT lines), and approach roads.
4. **Foundation Check:** Anchor bolts position, projection length (≥ 100 mm above concrete), and concrete strength (≥ 75 % of $f_{ck}$ at 7 days for erection loads).
5. **Material Receipt & Storage:** Stacking on dunnage, lifting slings attached, painted surfaces protected.

### Pillar B — Erection (Lifting, Placing, Aligning)
1. **Lifting the member** using certified slings, shackles, and spreader beams.
2. **Setting the member** on shim packs / leveling nuts on the anchor bolts.
3. **Temporary bracing** using guy ropes, push-pull props, or temporary cleats.
4. **Plumbing & alignment** by theodolite / laser plumb.
5. **Permanent connection** by HSFG bolts or field welding.
6. **Removal of temporary bracing** only after the adjacent members provide full lateral stability.

### Pillar C — Post-Erection (Quality, Safety, Handover)
1. **Final alignment survey** and deviation report.
2. **NDT** (Non-Destructive Testing) of field welds — typically **ultrasonic (UT)** or **magnetic-particle (MPT)**.
3. **Torque audit** of HSFG bolts.
4. **Touch-up painting** of field-welded / scratched areas with matching primer.
5. **As-built documentation** for the client.

## 2.2 Plant & Equipment — Selection Logic

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to know the **right plant for the right job**. The decision tree is:

$$
\text{Height of Lift} \;\uparrow \;\Rightarrow \text{Tower Crane / Derric}
$$

$$
\text{Site Area Large + Heavy Lifts} \;\Rightarrow \text{Crawler Crane}
$$

$$
\text{Site Confined + Repeated Lifts} \;\Rightarrow \text{Mobile / Truck-mounted Crane}
$$

$$
\text{Indoor / Bridge Girder} \;\Rightarrow \text{Goliath / Gantry Crane}
$$

### 2.2.1 Crane Capacity Verification (the "single most-asked derivation")

The rated capacity of a crane must always exceed the **maximum hoisting load** under the most adverse combination of:

$$
L_{\text{actual}} = (W_{\text{steel}} + W_{\text{slings}} + W_{\text{spreader}} + W_{\text{hook block}}) \times \eta
$$

where $\eta$ is the **dynamic / shock factor** for *sudden* lifting, slewing, or wind gusts:

| Lifting Condition | $\eta$ (IS 800 / IS 807) |
|---|:---:|
| Steady, slow lift from rest | **1.10** |
| Normal lifting & placing | **1.25** |
| Lifting with possible snatch / jerk | **1.50** |

The **safe working load (SWL)** of the crane is then:

$$
\text{SWL}_{\text{required}} \;=\; \frac{L_{\text{actual}} \times \eta}{\text{Number of Part Lines of Sling}}
$$

And the operating condition is verified by:

$$
\text{SWL}_{\text{crane, at working radius}} \;\geq\; \text{SWL}_{\text{required}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The crane chart is read at the **maximum working radius** (boom tip to the centre of the lifted load), **with all outriggers fully deployed**, and on **firm, level ground**. KTU questions often deliberately omit the outrigger condition — losing 1 mark here is the most common error.

## 2.3 Methods of Hoisting (Direct KTU Exam Content)

The KTU 2024 scheme lists **three classical methods** of hoisting steel members. Examiner-tip: a 7-mark question is *almost always* a comparison table of these methods.

| Method | Best Suited For | Equipment | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Crane Method** | Most modern sites — multi-storey buildings, industrial sheds. | Mobile / crawler / tower crane. | Needs firm access road; boom radius limits reach. |
| **2. Derrick Method** | Confined sites, very heavy lifts (e.g., bridge girders, factory columns). | Guy derrick, shear-leg derrick, gin pole. | Slow, large set-up time, needs strong anchor for guys. |
| **3. Jigger / Winch Method** | Low-rise sheds, trusses, small-span roofs. | Pulley blocks, winch, scaffold towers. | Highly labour-intensive; not viable above ~15 m. |

## 2.4 Sequence of Erection (High-Yield KTU Point)

The **logical erection sequence** ensures that **the structure is always stable at every intermediate stage**. A wrong sequence can cause a progressive collapse during construction.

**Recommended sequence for a multi-storey steel building:**

1. Erect columns of the ground floor first (start from the **stiffest bay**).
2. Fix base plates, plumb, and grout.
3. Erect ground-floor beams — **bay by bay**.
4. Install permanent / temporary floor decking or bracing.
5. Erect **first-tier columns** of the next floor.
6. Repeat the cycle.
7. The **roof truss / bracing system** is always the last.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule:** *Never* leave a column "standing alone" without at least **two horizontal beams** (or one beam + one brace) attached at the top. A free-standing column behaves like a long pendulum and is the #1 cause of erection-stage collapses, per IS 800:2007 guidelines.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Units | KTU Significance |
|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | $L_{\text{actual}} = (W_s + W_{sl} + W_{sp} + W_h) \times \eta$ | tonnes | Crane selection |
| 2 | $\text{SWL}_{\text{req}} = L_{\text{actual}} \times \eta \;/\; N_{\text{lines}}$ | tonnes | Crane chart reading |
| 3 | Plumb tolerance: $\pm 3$ mm / storey, $\le 25$ mm cumulative | mm | Acceptance criterion |
| 4 | Bolt hole clearance: $\phi_{\text{hole}} = \phi_{\text{bolt}} + 1.5$ to $2.0$ mm | mm | Fit-up tolerance |
| 5 | Foundation concrete strength at erection: $\ge 0.75\, f_{ck}$ | MPa | Early loading check |
| 6 | Tightening torque: $T = k \cdot d \cdot F_b$ | N·m | HSFG bolt tightening |
| 7 | Wind cut-off: erection suspended if $V_{wind} \gt 50$ km/h | km/h | Safety threshold |
| 8 | Two-thirds rule for sling: $L \le 2\, H$ for vertical, else angle reduces SWL | m, m | Sling angle correction |

> **Where, in formula 6:** $k$ = nut-factor (≈ 0.20 for hot-dip galv. / 0.18 for black), $d$ = nominal bolt diameter (m), $F_b$ = proof load (N).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same erection logic governs:
- **Industrial sheds** (PEB — Pre-Engineered Buildings) — primary + secondary members in one shift.
- **High-rise steel frames** — sequence tied to concrete-composite floor cycles.
- **Bridges** — launching girders, cantilever erection, incremental launching.
- **Power-plant trusses & girders** — heavy-lift derricks (e.g., 1500-tonne crawler cranes in nuclear-containment steel liner erection).
- **Telecom / transmission towers** — gin-pole method still dominant in remote hilly sites.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive, board-exam-ready** procedural derivations and worked numerical examples. Every algebraic step is explicitly written out — **no shortcuts, no skipped transitions**.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Crane Selection for a Roof-Truss Lift (Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (synthetic but KTU-typical)

> A roof truss weighing **1.8 tonnes** is to be erected in an industrial building. The truss is **18 m long**. Two equal-length wire-rope slings will be attached at the quarter-points (i.e., 4.5 m from each end). The weight of the spreader beam is **150 kg**, the hook block **80 kg**, and the slings themselves **40 kg**. The crane will lift from a radius of **14 m** with the boom at **30° elevation**. Recommended dynamic factor $\eta = 1.25$. The crane's chart at 14 m radius (with full outriggers on firm ground) gives a SWL of **4.5 tonnes**. Verify whether the crane is safe.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert all weights to tonnes** (this is the KTU-mandated unit; using kg mid-way is a common slip).

$$
W_{\text{truss}} = 1.80 \;\text{t}, \quad W_{\text{spreader}} = 0.150 \;\text{t}, \quad W_{\text{hook}} = 0.080 \;\text{t}, \quad W_{\text{slings}} = 0.040 \;\text{t}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the static (dead) load at the hook**

$$
W_{\text{static}} = W_{\text{truss}} + W_{\text{spreader}} + W_{\text{hook}} + W_{\text{slings}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
W_{\text{static}} = 1.80 + 0.150 + 0.080 + 0.040 = 2.070 \;\text{t}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the dynamic factor $\eta = 1.25$ to get the actual hoisting load**

$$
L_{\text{actual}} = W_{\text{static}} \times \eta = 2.070 \times 1.25 = 2.588 \;\text{t}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the effective part-lines of the sling**

The truss is lifted by a **two-leg sling** (one each side), so $N_{\text{lines}} = 2$. The slings are attached at the **quarter-points**, 4.5 m from each end. The **sling angle** $\theta$ from the horizontal needs to be computed to verify the vertical lift component.

**Step 4a — Compute the sling angle**

The truss is 18 m long. Each sling is attached at 4.5 m from the nearest end. The hook is positioned at the **mid-span** of the truss (centred for balance). The **horizontal distance** from the attachment point to the hook position above is:

$$
x = 4.5 \;\text{m} \quad \text{(from quarter-point to centre of truss)}
$$

Assuming the hook is held at a height such that the **vertical rise** of the sling is $H = 3.0$ m above the truss top chord:

$$
\tan \theta = \dfrac{H}{x} = \dfrac{3.0}{4.5} = 0.6667
$$

Therefore:

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.6667) \approx 33.69^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4b — Compute the tension in each sling leg**

The vertical component of each sling must together support the total load:

$$
2 \cdot T \cdot \sin \theta = L_{\text{actual}}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{L_{\text{actual}}}{2 \cdot \sin \theta} = \dfrac{2.588}{2 \cdot \sin(33.69^{\circ})}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{2.588}{2 \cdot 0.5550} = \dfrac{2.588}{1.110} = 2.332 \;\text{t per leg}
$$

**Step 4c — Sling capacity check (if sling SWL is given as 1.5 t per leg)**

$$
\text{Required per leg} = 2.332 \;\text{t} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{SWL per leg} = 1.5 \;\text{t}
$$

Since $2.332 > 1.5$, **the slings are unsafe** — increase sling diameter or use 4-leg sling.

> If a 4-leg sling is used ($N = 4$):

$$
T = \dfrac{2.588}{4 \cdot 0.5550} = 1.166 \;\text{t per leg} \;\;<\; 1.5 \;\text{t} \;\;(\text{SAFE})
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the crane capacity**

The crane chart at 14 m radius gives $\text{SWL}_{\text{crane}} = 4.5$ tonnes. Compare with the **gross load the crane must lift**, which is $L_{\text{actual}} = 2.588$ t (the slings/spreader/hook are all *below* the hook and counted in $L_{\text{actual}}$).

$$
\text{SWL}_{\text{crane}} = 4.5 \;\text{t} \;\geq\; L_{\text{actual}} = 2.588 \;\text{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{SAFE}
$$

**Step 6 — Final decision matrix**

| Check | Required | Available | Result |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Crane capacity at 14 m | 2.588 t | 4.5 t | ✅ SAFE |
| 2-leg sling per leg | 2.332 t | 1.5 t | ❌ UNSAFE |
| 4-leg sling per leg | 1.166 t | 1.5 t | ✅ SAFE |

> **Conclusion:** *Adopt a 4-leg sling configuration; the crane is otherwise adequate.* This is the **model answer structure** KTU examiners expect — a numerical conclusion, not a vague statement.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Plumb-Deviation Acceptance Check

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> A 4-storey steel column line has the following measured vertical deviations (mm) at each floor level: Storey 1 = +4, Storey 2 = −2, Storey 3 = +6, Storey 4 = −3. (Positive = leaning east, negative = west.) Using the KTU/IS-800 tolerance of $\pm 3$ mm per storey and $\le 25$ mm cumulative, determine whether the column is acceptable. If not, propose the corrective action.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Per-storey check** (each $\le 3$ mm?)

$$
4 > 3 \quad (\text{Storey 1 fails}), \qquad 2 < 3 \quad (\text{Storey 2 ok}), \qquad 6 > 3 \quad (\text{Storey 3 fails}), \qquad 3 \le 3 \quad (\text{Storey 4 ok})
$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative deviation**

$$
\Delta_{\text{cum}} = 4 - 2 + 6 - 3 = 5 \;\text{mm}
$$

Since $\Delta_{\text{cum}} = 5$ mm $\le 25$ mm, the **cumulative** check passes, but the **per-storey** check fails at storeys 1 and 3.

**Step 3 — Corrective action**

Use **push-pull props / raking shores** on the failed floors, apply lateral jacking at the upper floor level, and re-plumb by theodolite. After correction, repeat the survey.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hoisting Methods at a Glance

| Parameter | Crane Method | Derrick Method | Winch / Jigger Method |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Capital cost | High | Medium | Low |
| Speed of erection | Fast | Slow | Slowest |
| Max lift height | Up to ~150 m (tower) | Up to ~50 m | Up to ~15 m |
| Max single-lift weight | Up to **3 000 t** (crawler) | Up to **200 t** | Up to **5 t** |
| Site congestion | Low–medium | High | Very high |
| Skilled labour needed | Medium | High | Low |
| KTU typical marks weight | 3–4 | 3–4 | 2 |

## 3.4 Field-Connection Logic — Bolts vs. Welds (often a 7-mark sub-question)

> **Rule of thumb (and KTU key point):**
>
> - **HSFG bolts** are preferred for *column-beam* moment connections and where *speed of erection* is critical.
> - **Field welding** is preferred for *aesthetic* connections (tube structures, architectural steel) and where *rigidity* dominates.
> - **Bearing-type bolts (ordinary / black bolts)** are used for *temporary* connections, scaffolding, and light bracing only.
> - At *column splices*, both methods are used — the lower 50 % of splice bolts are often bearing-type for shear, the upper 50 % HSFG for tension.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Check — Bolt Torque:**
> For an M20, grade 8.8 HSFG bolt, with $k = 0.20$ and proof load $F_b = 0.7 \times f_{ub} \times A_s$:
>
> $$
> F_b = 0.7 \times 800 \;\text{MPa} \times 245 \;\text{mm}^2 = 137\,200 \;\text{N} = 137.2 \;\text{kN}
> $$
>
> $$
> T = k \cdot d \cdot F_b = 0.20 \times 0.020 \;\text{m} \times 137\,200 \;\text{N} = 548.8 \;\text{N·m}
> $$
>
> This matches IS-4000 / IS-800 tabulated values to within 5 % — examiners love this calculation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Erection Workflow (KTU-Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Design Approval] --> B[Stage 2: Shop Fabrication with Piece Marks]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Site Survey and Foundation Check]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Crane Selection and Method Statement]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Receipt and Stacking of Steel]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Lifting and Sling Verification]
    F --> G[Stage 7: Setting on Shim Packs]
    G --> H[Stage 8: Plumbing and Alignment]
    H --> I[Stage 9: Temporary Bracing Installed]
    I --> J[Stage 10: Field Connection Bolting or Welding]
    J --> K[Stage 11: NDT and Torque Audit]
    K --> L[Stage 12: Touch-up Painting and As-built]
    L --> M[Stage 13: Handover]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Plant Selection (Directly Maps to KTU Theory)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: What is the lift weight?] --> P2{Less than 5 tonnes}
    P2 -->|Yes| P3{Lift height below 15 m}
    P2 -->|No| P4{Lift height above 30 m}
    P3 -->|Yes| P5[Use Winch or Jigger Method]
    P3 -->|No| P6[Use Mobile Crane]
    P4 -->|Yes| P7[Use Tower Crane]
    P4 -->|No| P8{Lift weight above 200 t}
    P8 -->|Yes| P9[Use Heavy Crawler Crane or Stiff-leg Derrick]
    P8 -->|No| P10[Use Mobile or Crawler Crane]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Push-Pull Prop / Temporary Bracing System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Steel Column Plumb Line] --> B((Base Plate and Anchor Bolts))
    A --> C((Top of Column with Lifting Lug))
    D[Raking Shore / Push-Pull Prop] --- C
    D --- E[Ground Anchor or Dead-man]
    F[Horizontal Beam Connects Two Adjacent Columns] --- A
    G[Cross Bracing in Roof Plane] --- F
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **column** (A) is held plumb by **two systems working together** — a **push-pull prop** (D) anchored to a **dead-man / ground anchor** (E) provides lateral stability at the top, while the **horizontal beam** (F) connecting two columns at the floor level, plus the **roof-level cross-bracing** (G), closes the structure into a stable, triangulated unit. The temporary prop (D) is **removed only after** the cross-bracing (G) and beam (F) provide the design lateral stiffness.

## 4.4 Schematic — Crane Boom Geometry (for Numerical Problems)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crane Centre Pivot] --> B((Hook Position above Load))
    A --> C((Boom Tip at Elevation Angle theta])
    C --> D[Vertical Boom Height H]
    B --> E[Horizontal Working Radius R]
    C --> F[Boom Length L]
```

**Geometric relationships** (commonly used in KTU numericals):

$$
H = L \cdot \sin \theta \quad \text{(vertical height of hook above boom foot)}
$$

$$
R = L \cdot \cos \theta \quad \text{(horizontal radius)}
$$

$$
L = \sqrt{H^2 + R^2}
$$

> These three relations are the **minimum you must know** to solve any crane-chart problem. KTU papers often test them in the **first 5 minutes** of a 14-mark question.

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram — Bolted vs. Welded Connections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Field Connection Type?] --> B{Bearing Type Bolt}
    A --> C{HSFG Friction Grip Bolt}
    A --> D{Field Weld}
    B --> B1[Used for Temporary and Shear Connections]
    C --> C1[Used for Moment Connections and Tension Zones]
    D --> D1[Used for Tube Structures and High Rigidity Zones]
    B1 --> E[Needs only a Spanner and Labour]
    C1 --> F[Needs Calibrated Torque Wrench]
    D1 --> G[Needs Certified Welder and NDT Inspector]
```
