## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define slip form construction. List any four advantages of this method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):* Slip form construction is a continuous, mechanised method of constructing tall, vertical concrete structures in which the formwork is jacked upward slowly and continuously (typically 100–300 mm/hr) by hydraulic jacks climbing on embedded rods, while fresh concrete is placed in layers at the top. The concrete becomes self-supporting by the time it emerges from the bottom of the form, producing a jointless, monolithic structure.

*Advantages (1.5 marks – any 4):*
1. **Speed:** A 30 m tall structure can be completed in 8–12 days.
2. **Monolithic Construction:** No construction joints → superior water-tightness.
3. **Economy for tall structures:** Form cost amortised over the entire height.
4. **Reduced labour for repetitive vertical work.**
5. **Better concrete quality** due to continuous, controlled supply.
6. **Smooth, uniform surface finish** requiring minimal plastering.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List the essential components of a slip form system and state the function of any two.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Components (1.5 marks – any 6 listed):*
- Steel forms (sheathing)
- Yokes (waler frames)
- Hydraulic jacks
- Jack rods
- Working platform
- Suspended platform
- Hydraulic power pack
- Levelling tubes
- Embedments

*Functions (1.5 marks):*
- **Hydraulic Jacks (0.75 mark):** Provide lifting force by climbing on jack rods, raising the entire formwork assembly uniformly.
- **Jack Rods (0.75 mark):** High-tensile steel bars embedded in the wall that act as the "track" or reaction member against which the jacks push to lift the formwork.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Explain in detail the construction procedure of slip form construction with the help of a neat sketch. Discuss the various components of the slip form system. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A circular silo of outer diameter 10 m and wall thickness 250 mm is to be constructed using slip form technique. The form height is 1.2 m and the initial setting time of concrete is 4 hours. Determine:
  (i) The slip rate in mm/hr.
  (ii) The hourly concrete requirement in m³/hr.
  (iii) The total time required to slip-form a 35 m tall silo, in days. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) – Construction Procedure & Components (7 marks)

**1. Pre-Construction Stage (1 mark):**
- Detailed design of the slip form system (form height, yoke spacing, jack capacity).
- Manufacture/pre-assembly of forms, yokes, platforms, and the hydraulic system at the casting yard.
- Casting of the foundation and starter wall (typically 0.5–1.0 m above ground) with starter bars projecting upward for continuity.

**2. Formwork Erection (1 mark):**
- The pre-assembled slip form unit is **lifted by crane** and placed on the starter wall.
- **Plumbing and alignment** are done using theodolite, plumb bob, or laser plummet.
- Jack rods are positioned and fixed to the starter reinforcement; initial jacks are mounted on the rods.
- Working and suspended platforms are attached; safety nets and ladders are fixed.

**3. Initial Pour (1 mark):**
- The form is filled in **three equal layers** (each ≈ 400 mm for a 1.2 m form) within 3–4 hours.
- Concrete is compacted using **needle vibrators (40–60 mm dia, 3000–6000 rpm)**.
- Once the bottom layer sets sufficiently, the system begins to **carry its own weight** through the jack rods.

**4. Continuous Slip Cycle (2 marks):**
- Concrete is placed in **150–200 mm lifts** at the top of the form every **10–15 minutes**.
- Hydraulic jacks lift the form by **20–25 mm** every **1–2 minutes** (uniformly across all jacks to prevent differential lift and torsion).
- Vertical reinforcement bars are continuously **spliced** using couplers or lap welding at the top deck.
- Horizontal reinforcement (hoops/spirals for circular structures) is tied progressively.
- As concrete emerges from the bottom, **finishers** on the suspended platform perform **smoothing, rubbing, hole-plugging, and curing** (via water spray or curing compound).
- **Verticality and torsion** are continuously monitored; corrective horizontal jacking is applied if drift exceeds **±25 mm** in 10 m height.

**5. Closure and De-shuttering (1 mark):**
- At the design height, the last lift is placed and allowed to set for a few hours.
- Jacking is stopped, the formwork is **stripped downward** (since it cannot be recovered upward at the top), and all components are dismantled, cleaned, and stored.
- The completed structure is a **seamless, monolithic** tower/silo/chimney with no horizontal construction joints.

**6. Neat Sketch (1 mark):**
*Refer to the Mermaid diagrams in SECTION 4 for the schematic representation. In the exam, a labelled cross-section should show: forms, yokes, jacks, jack rods, working platform, suspended platform, fresh concrete in the form, set concrete below the form, and embedments.*

**Components (integrated within above, key ones for naming):**
- **Steel Forms (Sheathing)** – 3–6 mm thick, 1.0–1.5 m high.
- **Yokes** – Structural steel frames transferring jack force to forms.
- **Hydraulic Jacks** – 20–25 kN capacity each.
- **Jack Rods** – 25/32 mm high-tensile steel.
- **Working & Suspended Platforms.**
- **Hydraulic Power Pack & Manifold.**
- **Levelling Tubes** – for monitoring uniform jacking.

### Part (b) – Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Outer diameter, $D_o = 10$ m
- Wall thickness, $t = 0.25$ m
- Form height, $H_f = 1.2$ m
- Initial setting time, $t_{set} = 4$ hours
- Silo height, $H_{silo} = 35$ m

**Step 1 – Cross-Sectional Area of Wall (0.5 mark):**

Mean diameter:
$$
D_m = D_o - t = 10 - 0.25 = 9.75 \text{ m}
$$

Wall cross-sectional area:
$$
A_c = \pi \cdot D_m \cdot t = \pi \times 9.75 \times 0.25
$$

$$
A_c = 7.655 \text{ m}^2 \quad (\text{approx})
$$

**Step 2 – Slip Rate (2 marks):**

Using the formula:
$$
v = \frac{1000 \cdot H_f}{t_{set,hr}} = \frac{1000 \times 1.2}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{v = 300 \text{ mm/hr}}
$$

*(Note: This is at the upper practical limit. In real practice, the slip rate might be reduced to 150–200 mm/hr to ensure proper setting; for exam calculations, use the formula directly.)*

**Step 3 – Hourly Concrete Requirement (2 marks):**

$$
Q = A_c \times \frac{v}{1000} = 7.655 \times \frac{300}{1000}
$$

$$
Q = 7.655 \times 0.3 = 2.2965 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 2.30 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}
$$

**Step 4 – Daily Concrete Output (0.5 mark):**

$$
V_{day} = Q \times 24 = 2.2965 \times 24 = 55.12 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 5 – Total Time for 35 m Silo (2 marks):**

Convert to consistent units. $H_{silo} = 35$ m = **35,000 mm**.

$$
T_{slip} = \frac{H_{silo}}{v} = \frac{35{,}000 \text{ mm}}{300 \text{ mm/hr}}
$$

$$
T_{slip} = 116.67 \text{ hours} = \frac{116.67}{24} \text{ days}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{slip} \approx 4.86 \text{ days} \approx 5 \text{ days}}
$$

**Final Answer Summary (0 marks, just boxed):**
- Slip rate = **300 mm/hr**
- Hourly concrete = **2.30 m³/hr**
- Total slip time = **~5 days** (continuous, 24/7 working)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Mean diameter vs. Outer diameter confusion (–0.5 mark):** Always use $D_m = D_o - t$ for thin-walled circular sections. Using $D_o$ overestimates $A_c$.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors (–1 mark):** $v$ in mm/hr, but $A_c$ in m². Convert by $v/1000$ in the $Q$ formula.
> 3. **Setting time in hours vs. minutes (–0.5 mark):** Confirm units before applying the formula. The version $v = 1000 H_f / t_{hr}$ requires **hours**, not minutes.
> 4. **Skipping the "initial pour" step in the procedure (–1 mark):** KTU examiners specifically check for the **3-layer initial pour** description.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention monitoring/torsion control (–0.5 mark):** Laser plummet or optical plummet use is a "must-mention" point.
> 6. **Not stating concrete grade or slump in Part (a) (–0.5 mark):** Always specify **M30, 75–100 mm slump** for the mix.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice:**
**(a)** Discuss the advantages, disadvantages, and applications of slip form construction. Compare it briefly with conventional formwork. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** What is the role of hydraulic jacks in slip form construction? Explain the various types of jacks used. A slip form system has 30 jacks, each of 25 kN capacity, lifting a total vertical load of 600 kN. If the friction load is 80 kN, determine:
  (i) Load per jack without friction.
  (ii) Load per jack with friction included.
  (iii) Whether the jacks are safe (state condition for safety). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline for Question B (for reference):**

### Part (a) – Advantages, Disadvantages, Applications, Comparison (7 marks)

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- Speed of construction (5–7 m/day).
- Monolithic, jointless structure → better water-tightness.
- Reduced formwork cost per metre for tall structures.
- Continuous concrete supply → uniform quality.
- Less labour on repetitive vertical work.
- Smooth surface finish.

**Disadvantages (2 marks):**
- High initial investment (specialised equipment).
- Requires continuous 24/7 working.
- Highly weather-sensitive (rain halts work).
- Skilled, experienced crew essential.
- High power dependency (hydraulic system).
- Risk of catastrophic failure if verticality is lost.
- Limited to relatively uniform cross-sections.

**Applications (1.5 marks):**
- Chimneys, silos, grain elevators.
- Water tanks, cooling towers.
- Bridge piers, offshore platform shafts.
- High-rise building cores, lift shafts.
- TV/radio/communication towers.
- Large dam outlet works.

**Comparison with Conventional Formwork (1.5 marks):**
Refer to the **Analytical Matrix** in SECTION 3.5 above. Key contrasts: speed (slip 5–7 m/day vs. conventional 1–2 m/day), joints (slip none vs. conventional many), cost (slip high initial, low per metre vs. conventional low initial, high per metre for tall structures).

### Part (b) – Jacks & Numerical (7 marks)

**Role of Hydraulic Jacks (2 marks):**
- Provide continuous, synchronised lifting force to the entire slip form assembly.
- Climb on embedded jack rods using ratchet and piston mechanism.
- Ensure uniform vertical movement (critical to prevent torsion or differential settlement).
- Modern jacks are **double-acting** (lift and lower) with flow-control valves for precise stroke management.

**Types of Jacks (1.5 marks):**
1. **Hydraulic Jacks** (most common) – powered by central hydraulic power pack; capacity 20–25 kN; 25 mm stroke.
2. **Pneumatic (Air) Jacks** – used for smaller projects; lower capacity (~10 kN).
3. **Screw Jacks (Manual/Mechanical)** – used in low-height, low-budget applications.
4. **Electric Jacks (Ratchet type)** – rare; used where hydraulic power is unavailable.
5. **Differential Slip-form Jacks** – based on differential screw principle for very fine control.

**Numerical Solution (3.5 marks):**

**Given:**
- $n = 30$ jacks
- $F_{capacity} = 25$ kN per jack
- $W_{total} = 600$ kN (gravity load only)
- $F_{friction} = 80$ kN

**(i) Load per jack without friction (1 mark):**
$$
F_1 = \frac{W_{total}}{n} = \frac{600}{30} = 20 \text{ kN/jack}
$$

**(ii) Load per jack with friction included (1.5 marks):**
Total load = $W_{total} + F_{friction} = 600 + 80 = 680$ kN
$$
F_2 = \frac{680}{30} = 22.67 \text{ kN/jack}
$$

**(iii) Safety Check (1 mark):**
With safety factor of 1.5, required jack capacity:
$$
F_{required} = 22.67 \times 1.5 = 34.0 \text{ kN}
$$

Available capacity = **25 kN < 34 kN** → **Jacks are NOT safe.**
*Recommendation:* Use jacks of **40 kN capacity** (standard next size).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors in Part (b) of Question B:**
> 1. Forgetting to **add** friction load to gravity load (–1 mark).
> 2. Not applying **safety factor** before comparing with jack capacity (–1 mark).
> 3. Confusing **stroke length** (25 mm) with **capacity** (25 kN) – they are unrelated parameters (–0.5 mark).
> 4. Listing only "hydraulic jacks" without naming other types (–1 mark in types section).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS – MEMORISE BEFORE EXAM:**

- **Definition:** Slip form = continuous jacking of formwork while placing fresh concrete → monolithic, jointless tall structures.
- **Standard Slip Rate:** 100–300 mm/hr; most common is **150 mm/hr** for routine work.
- **Form Height:** **1.0–1.5 m** (almost always **1.2 m** in exam problems).
- **Concrete Spec:** **M30 grade, 75–100 mm slump, initial set 4–5 hours** (use retarders in hot weather).
- **Jack Spec:** **25 kN capacity, 25 mm stroke, hydraulic**, climbing on **25/32 mm** Dywidag-type jack rods.
- **Key Formula – Slip Rate:** $v = (1000 \cdot H_f) / t_{set,hr}$ **(mm/hr)**
- **Key Formula – Hourly Concrete:** $Q = A_c \cdot v / 1000$ **(m³/hr)**
- **Key Formula – Time:** $T_{slip} = H_{structure} / v$ **(hours)**
- **Key Formula – Pressure:** $P_{max} = \gamma \cdot H_f$ **(kN/m²)**; min design pressure = **24 kN/m²** per IS 14687.
- **Initial Pour Rule:** **3 equal layers of 400 mm each** (for 1.2 m form) — must mention in procedure.
- **Verticality Control:** Use **laser plummet / optical plummet**; corrective horizontal jacking if drift > 25 mm in 10 m.
- **Curing:** Done on the **suspended platform** as concrete emerges from the bottom of the form.
- **Common Applications:** Silos, chimneys, water tanks, high-rise cores, bridge piers, towers.
- **Disadvantages:** High initial cost, weather-sensitive, skilled crew needed, 24/7 working, high power dependency.
- **Comparison Anchor:** Slip form → **fast, jointless, monolithic, mechanised**; Conventional → **slow, jointed, labour-intensive, flexible**.
- **Examiner Traps:** (a) Use **mean diameter** $D_m$ for circular cross-sections, not $D_o$. (b) Convert **mm to m** correctly in $Q$ formula. (c) Always mention **safety factor** in jack capacity checks. (d) Use **hours** (not minutes) in slip rate formula unless otherwise stated.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET611 - INTRODUCTION TO CONSTRUCTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Construction Technology - Cost-Effective Construction - Rapid Wall Construction, Soil-Cement Block Masonry, Voided Slab Technology, Filler Slab Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cost-Effective Construction – Formal Definition

**Cost-Effective Construction (CEC)** refers to the practice of designing and executing building projects that achieve the required structural performance, durability, and functional quality at the **lowest practicable cost**, by optimising materials, labour, time, and technology. In the Indian and specifically the **Kerala housing context**, cost-effective construction is anchored on the *"3R Principle"* – **Reduce, Reuse, Recycle** – and on substituting energy-intensive conventional materials (burnt clay bricks, reinforced cement concrete) with locally available, low-embodied-energy alternatives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 4, the focus is restricted to four specific technologies – *Rapid Wall Construction, Soil-Cement Block Masonry, Voided Slab Technology, and Filler Slab Technology*. Examiners will NOT ask questions on any other cost-effective method (e.g., bamboo, fly-ash bricks) unless explicitly mentioned.

## 1.2 Technology-Wise Conceptual Overview

### A. Rapid Wall Construction
**Rapid Wall** is a **prefabricated, load-bearing walling system** consisting of hollow panels manufactured from a composite of *Gypsum plaster, Glass Fibre Reinforcement, and special additives*. Each panel is typically **12 m long, 2.85 m high, and 124 mm thick**, with internal vertical cells that can be filled with concrete and reinforcement to act as shear walls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of Rapid Wall panels as *"giant hollow Lego bricks made of plaster and glass fibre."* Instead of laying thousands of small bricks one-by-one with mortar, the contractor simply *tilts up* a single pre-cast panel and fixes it in position. The hollow cells are then filled with concrete – like pouring water into the hollow chambers of a Lego block – to give it strength. **One Rapid Wall panel replaces roughly 130 conventional bricks.**

### B. Soil-Cement Block Masonry
Soil-cement blocks (also called **Stabilised Mud Blocks – SMBs** or **Compressed Earth Blocks – CEBs**) are walling units made by **compacting a homogeneous mixture of soil, ordinary Portland cement (5–10 % by weight), and water** in a hand-operated or motorised press. The compacted blocks are then **cured with water for 14–28 days** to develop strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine *making a sandcastle that never collapses.* If you take ordinary soil and just press it into a mould, it crumbles when dry. But if you add a small quantity of cement (5–10 %) and water, then press it hard, the cement "glues" the soil particles together at their contact points – the same way a tiny drop of glue joins two grains of sand. The result is a block strong enough to build a house. The "magic" is in the *compaction pressure* (typically 4–7 N/mm²) which brings the soil grains close enough for cement to bond them.

### C. Voided Slab Technology
A **Voided Slab (also called "BubbleDeck" or "Hollow Flat Slab")** is a two-way reinforced concrete slab in which **hollow plastic or recycled polyethylene spheres (void formers)** are embedded in the slab's depth, replacing a portion of the in-effective concrete in the tension zone and the neutral axis. This reduces the **self-weight (dead load)** of the slab by up to **30–35 %** while retaining the full flexural capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Picture a *chocolate-chip muffin* – the cake (concrete) carries the load, but the chocolate chips (voids) displace a portion of the cake without weakening it. In the same way, the plastic spheres "displace" concrete in regions where it is not structurally needed, dramatically lightening the slab. The result is fewer columns, longer spans, and less steel.

### D. Filler Slab Technology
The **Filler Slab** is a partial-replacement slab system in which **lightweight, low-cost filler materials** (such as clay tiles, broken brick pieces, coconut shells, hollow concrete blocks, or expanded polystyrene) are placed in the **tension zone** of a conventionally reinforced concrete slab. The filler material displaces concrete that is not contributing to structural strength, thereby **saving 20–30 % of concrete and steel**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Visualise a *layered cake.* The bottom layer (tension zone) is where concrete is *least* useful because it tends to crack under bending. So instead of solid concrete at the bottom, we place *lightweight tiles or hollow blocks* that act as a permanent void. Only the top compression layer uses solid concrete with reinforcement. The result: a slab that is almost as strong as a conventional slab, but uses *far less* expensive concrete. The tension steel is placed in the *ribs between* the fillers, like the steel framework of a bridge.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants for KTU)

| Metric | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Rapid Wall panel thickness | **124 mm** |
| Soil-cement cement content | **5 % to 10 %** by dry weight of soil |
| Compressive strength (SMB) | **3.5 to 7.0 N/mm²** (after 28 days) |
| Voided Slab weight reduction | **Up to 35 %** |
| Filler Slab concrete saving | **20 % to 30 %** |
| Curing period (Soil-cement) | **14 to 28 days** |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rapid Wall Construction – Theory of Operation

### 2.1.1 Panel Composition (by mass, typical)
1. **Calcined Gypsum (Plaster of Paris):** ~ **92–95 %**
2. **Chopped Glass Fibre (12 mm length, alkali-resistant):** ~ **4–5 %** (acts as tensile reinforcement)
3. **Water + special additives (retarder, plasticiser):** ~ **1–2 %**

### 2.1.2 Manufacturing & Installation Sequence
- **Step 1 – Casting:** The slurry is poured into horizontal moulds and vibrated; the panel sets within ~ 30 minutes (gypsum is a fast-set binder).
- **Step 2 – Demoulding & Drying:** Panels are demoulded after 45 minutes and air-dried for ~ 24 hours.
- **Step 3 – Transport:** Panels are transported to the site by truck (one truck = ~ 250 m² of wall).
- **Step 4 – Erection:** A small mobile crane erects panels; vertical joints are sealed with gypsum slurry.
- **Step 5 – Cell Filling:** Vertical hollow cells at corners, jambs, and intersections are reinforced and filled with **M20 grade concrete** to act as shear columns.

### 2.1.3 Why "Rapid"?
- **One panel = 11.5 m²** of finished wall → eliminates plastering on both sides.
- **Dry construction → zero curing time.**
- **Time saving:** 60–70 % compared to brick masonry.

## 2.2 Soil-Cement Block Masonry – Theory of Operation

### 2.2.1 The Soil–Cement Stabilisation Concept
When cement is added to a soil and compacted in the presence of water, the cement hydrates to form **calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H) gels** that bond adjacent soil grains. The reaction is *primarily physical* (inter-particle bonding) rather than chemical replacement of soil mineralogy.

### 2.2.2 The "Why" – Forces in a Soil-Cement Matrix
- **Compaction Pressure (P_c):** Brings soil particles into close contact. Higher P_c → higher density → higher strength.
- **Cement Content (C %):** More cement = more C-S-H gel = higher strength, *but with diminishing returns beyond 10 %.*
- **Curing Duration (t):** Strength grows with curing; 7-day strength ≈ 65–70 % of 28-day strength.
- **Soil Gradation:** A well-graded soil (sand + silt + small clay fraction) packs denser and gives higher strength.

### 2.2.3 Suitability of Soil Types

| Soil Type | Suitability for Stabilisation |
| :--- | :--- |
| Gravel & Sand (well-graded) | **Excellent** – preferred |
| Silty soils | **Good** – requires higher cement |
| Clayey soils (liquid limit < 40) | **Marginal** – requires 8–10 % cement |
| Organic clays, peat, expansive black cotton | **Not suitable** |

## 2.3 Voided Slab Technology – Theory of Operation

### 2.3.1 Why Embed Voids?
A conventional solid slab in bending develops:
- **Compression at the top fibre**
- **Tension at the bottom fibre**
- **A neutral axis in the middle where stress ≈ 0**

Concrete in the **tension zone cracks early** (concrete's tensile strength is only ~ 10 % of compressive strength) and is reinforced by steel. The concrete in the **middle third (around the neutral axis)** carries very little stress → it is *structurally redundant*. Replacing it with hollow spheres retains the lever arm of internal forces but **removes dead weight**.

### 2.3.2 Engineering Principle
If the cross-section moment of inertia (I) is preserved and only the "neutral-axis concrete" is removed, the **flexural rigidity (EI)** remains nearly unchanged while the **self-weight (W = ρ·g·V)** drops by ~ 30 %. This is the *core "why"* of the technology.

## 2.4 Filler Slab Technology – Theory of Operation

### 2.4.1 Filler Placement Logic
- The filler material occupies the **bottom 60–70 % of the slab depth** in the *soffit zone* (where bending-induced tension cracks appear).
- The reinforcement is placed *between the fillers* in narrow ribs (~ 100–150 mm wide).
- The top ~ 60–100 mm is a *solid concrete compression layer* with a light mesh.

### 2.4.2 Resulting Section Behaviour
The composite section behaves like a series of **T-beams (T-ribs) running in one direction**, where:
- The **flange** = solid top concrete
- The **web** = narrow rib of concrete + tension steel between fillers
- The **hollow region** = filler block

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $W_s = \rho_c \cdot A \cdot L$ | Self-weight of solid slab (ρ_c ≈ 25 kN/m³) | kN |
| 2 | $W_v = W_s \cdot (1 - k_v)$ | Weight of voided slab; $k_v$ = void volume fraction (0.25 to 0.35) | kN |
| 3 | $V_c^{\text{solid}} = A \cdot L$ | Concrete volume in conventional slab | m³ |
| 4 | $V_c^{\text{voided}} = A \cdot L \cdot (1 - k_v)$ | Concrete volume in voided slab | m³ |
| 5 | $V_c^{\text{filler}} = V_c^{\text{solid}} \cdot (1 - 0.25)$ | Concrete volume in filler slab (≈ 25 % saving) | m³ |
| 6 | $\sigma_{28} = k \cdot \log(t) + C$ | Approximate strength gain of soil-cement with curing time | N/mm² |
| 7 | $\text{Cost saving (\%)} = \dfrac{C_{\text{conv}} - C_{\text{CEC}}}{C_{\text{conv}}} \times 100$ | Generic cost-saving formula | % |
| 8 | $P_c = \dfrac{F}{A_m}$ | Compaction pressure in block press | N/mm² |
| 9 | $W_s = \gamma_c \cdot t_s$ | Slab self-weight per unit area (γ_c = 25 kN/m³) | kN/m² |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{steel}} = \dfrac{A_{s,\text{conv}} - A_{s,\text{CEC}}}{A_{s,\text{conv}}} \times 100$ | Steel saving percentage | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These four technologies together address the four major cost-heads in a building – *walls, foundations, slabs, and finishes.* When a designer combines all four in a 1BHK rural house, the total cost reduction can be **30–40 %** compared to a conventional burnt-brick-and-RCC-frame house, which is why the Government of India's *Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY)* strongly promotes them.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Cost Saving in a Filler Slab

**Problem:** A residential floor slab is **4 m × 6 m**, with overall thickness **150 mm**. It is to be converted into a *filler slab* by placing **50 mm thick hollow clay tile fillers** in the soffit zone over **70 % of the plan area** (the remaining 30 % being the solid ribs and top compression layer). Estimate the **percentage concrete saving**, and the **weight reduction** (kN/m²).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 – Volume of Conventional Solid Slab**

$$
V_{\text{solid}} = L \times B \times t = 4 \times 6 \times 0.150 = 3.600 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 – Filler Block Volume Occupied**

The filler zone occupies the bottom 50 mm (0.050 m) of the slab over 70 % of the plan area.

$$
A_{\text{filler}} = 0.70 \times (4 \times 6) = 0.70 \times 24 = 16.8 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V_{\text{filler}} = A_{\text{filler}} \times t_f = 16.8 \times 0.050 = 0.840 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 – Volume of Concrete in Filler Slab**

$$
V_{\text{concrete}} = V_{\text{solid}} - V_{\text{filler}} = 3.600 - 0.840 = 2.760 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 – Percentage Concrete Saving**

$$
\text{Saving (\%)} = \dfrac{V_{\text{solid}} - V_{\text{concrete}}}{V_{\text{solid}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Saving (\%)} = \dfrac{3.600 - 2.760}{3.600} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.840}{3.600} \times 100 = \mathbf{23.33 \; \%}
$$

**Step 5 – Self-Weight Comparison (per m²)**

*Solid slab:*

$$
w_{\text{solid}} = \gamma_c \times t = 25 \times 0.150 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

*Filler slab (effective, average across plan area):*

$$
w_{\text{filler}} = 25 \times 0.150 - \rho_f \times 0.050 \times 0.70
$$

(Assuming filler density ρ_f ≈ 8 kN/m³ for hollow clay tiles)

$$
w_{\text{filler}} = 3.75 - 8 \times 0.050 \times 0.70 = 3.75 - 0.28 = 3.47 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Weight reduction (\%)} = \dfrac{3.75 - 3.47}{3.75} \times 100 = \mathbf{7.47 \; \%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The concrete-volume saving (23.33 %) is the *real economic benefit* because it reduces cement, aggregate, water, and the labour to place & vibrate concrete. The dead-load saving (7.47 %) is the *structural benefit* – it reduces moments, shear, column sizes, and foundation sizes downstream.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Soil-Cement Block Mix Design

**Problem:** A site engineer wants to produce **300 soil-cement blocks** of size **230 mm × 110 mm × 75 mm** with a target wet compressive strength of **5.0 N/mm² at 28 days**. The soil is well-graded sandy loam with a *loose dry density* of **1.45 g/cm³**, and the chosen cement content is **8 %** by dry weight of soil. The *compaction ratio* (loose → compacted) is **1 : 1.6** (i.e., 1.6 m³ of loose soil yields 1 m³ of compacted block). Calculate the **mass of dry soil** and **mass of cement** required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 – Total Compacted Volume of Blocks**

$$
V_{\text{block}} = 0.230 \times 0.110 \times 0.075 = 0.001898 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 300 \times 0.001898 = 0.5694 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (compacted)}
$$

**Step 2 – Loose Volume of Soil Required**

$$
V_{\text{loose}} = V_{\text{compacted}} \times 1.6 = 0.5694 \times 1.6 = 0.9110 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 – Mass of Dry Soil (using loose density)**

Convert density: 1.45 g/cm³ = 1450 kg/m³

$$
M_{\text{soil}} = V_{\text{loose}} \times \rho_{\text{loose}} = 0.9110 \times 1450 = \mathbf{1320.95 \text{ kg} \approx 1.32 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

**Step 4 – Mass of Cement (8 % of dry soil weight)**

$$
M_{\text{cement}} = 0.08 \times 1320.95 = \mathbf{105.68 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Step 5 – Mass of Water (optimum moisture content assumed ≈ 12 % for sandy loam)**

$$
M_{\text{water}} = 0.12 \times 1320.95 = \mathbf{158.51 \text{ kg (litres)}}
$$

**Step 6 – Summary Table**

| Material | Mass Required | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dry soil | 1320.95 | kg |
| Cement (8 %) | 105.68 | kg |
| Water (12 % OMC) | 158.51 | L |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Voided Slab Weight Reduction

**Problem:** A solid two-way flat slab is **200 mm thick** over a **10 m × 10 m bay**. If a voided slab is used with **hollow plastic spheres of 180 mm diameter** embedded in a single layer, calculate the **percentage weight reduction**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 – Solid Slab Volume**

$$
V_{\text{solid}} = 10 \times 10 \times 0.200 = 20.0 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 – Volume Occupied by One Sphere**

$$
V_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \dfrac{4}{3} \times \pi \times (0.090)^3 = 0.003054 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 – Spheres per m² (single layer, centre-to-centre spacing ≈ 200 mm)**

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{1}{0.200}\right)^2 = 25 \text{ spheres/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 – Total Sphere Volume in the Slab**

$$
V_{\text{spheres}} = 25 \times 0.003054 \times (10 \times 10) = 25 \times 0.003054 \times 100 = 7.635 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5 – Void Fraction**

$$
k_v = \dfrac{V_{\text{spheres}}}{V_{\text{solid}}} = \dfrac{7.635}{20.0} = 0.3818 \quad \text{or} \quad 38.18 \%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> In practice, $k_v$ is capped at **30–35 %** because excessive voids create shear and punching problems around columns. So 38.18 % is the *theoretical maximum*; designers typically adopt **$k_v$ ≈ 0.30**.

**Step 6 – Realistic Weight Reduction (using $k_v$ = 0.30)**

$$
W_{\text{voided}} = W_{\text{solid}} \times (1 - 0.30) = 0.70 \times W_{\text{solid}}
$$

$$
\text{Weight reduction} = \mathbf{30 \; \%}
$$

**Step 7 – Self-Weight per m²**

$$
w_{\text{voided}} = 25 \times 0.200 \times 0.70 = 3.50 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

(vs. 5.00 kN/m² for the solid slab)

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define cost-effective construction and list any four technologies covered in KTU Module 4.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Cost-effective construction is a methodology that delivers buildings of acceptable structural performance, durability, and habitability at the **lowest practicable life-cycle cost** by optimising materials, labour, time, and embodied energy. It is anchored on the **3R principle** – Reduce, Reuse, Recycle.

The four technologies covered in KTU Module 4 are:
1. **Rapid Wall Construction** (prefabricated gypsum-glass-fibre panels)
2. **Soil-Cement Block Masonry** (stabilised mud blocks)
3. **Voided Slab Technology** (hollow-spheres embedded in RCC slabs)
4. **Filler Slab Technology** (lightweight fillers in the slab tension zone)

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the four technologies)*

### **Question 2: Why are plastic spheres embedded in the neutral axis of a voided slab?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

In a bending slab, the **neutral axis** is the plane where bending stress is theoretically **zero**. Concrete in this region contributes negligibly to flexural capacity but still adds self-weight. Embedding **hollow plastic spheres** in the neutral-axis zone:

- **Displaces dead concrete** without reducing the section's moment of inertia significantly.
- **Reduces self-weight by up to 30 %**, which lowers bending moments, shear, column loads, and foundation sizes.
- **Improves seismic performance** by reducing inertial mass.
- **Saves concrete** (and therefore cement) — a major cost and embodied-carbon gain.

*(3 marks: 1 for principle + 2 for benefits)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question:** Describe the manufacturing process of **soil-cement blocks** with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the factors affecting their compressive strength and compare their properties with conventional burnt-clay bricks.

**Model Answer Outline:**

**(a) Manufacturing Process (7 marks):**

The manufacturing process of soil-cement blocks consists of the following steps:

1. **Soil Selection & Testing:** Sandy loam or well-graded soil with a clay fraction < 20 % and liquid limit < 40 is selected. The soil is tested for *gradation, plasticity, and organic content*.
2. **Pulverisation & Sieving:** The soil is pulverised and sieved through a **4.75 mm** sieve to remove gravel and organic debris.
3. **Air Drying:** The soil is spread in a thin layer and air-dried to bring it close to the **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC ≈ 10–12 %)**.
4. **Proportioning:** Cement is added at **5–10 %** by dry weight of soil (typically 8 % for residential construction).
5. **Mixing:** Soil and cement are dry-mixed in a pan mixer for 2–3 minutes until a uniform colour is achieved.
6. **Water Addition:** Water (≈ 12 % by soil weight) is added and mixed thoroughly.
7. **Pressing:** The wet mix is fed into a **mechanical or hydraulic press** and compacted at **5–7 N/mm²** pressure.
8. **Demoulding:** The compacted block is ejected immediately and stacked on a flat surface.
9. **Initial Curing:** Blocks are kept under shade for **24 hours** to allow initial cement hydration.
10. **Water Curing:** Blocks are water-cured by sprinkling or immersion for **14 to 28 days**.
11. **Quality Control:** Random blocks are tested for **wet compressive strength** (target ≥ 5.0 N/mm²) and water absorption (target < 15 %).

**(b) Factors Affecting Strength (4 marks):**

| Factor | Effect |
| :--- | :--- |
| Cement content (5–10 %) | Higher cement = higher strength (linear up to 10 %) |
| Compaction pressure | Higher pressure = higher density = higher strength |
| Soil type & gradation | Well-graded sandy soils give highest strength |
| Curing duration | 28-day strength is ~ 1.4× 7-day strength |
| Water content | Near OMC gives maximum dry density |

**(c) Comparison with Burnt-Clay Bricks (3 marks):**

| Property | Soil-Cement Block | Burnt-Clay Brick |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Embodied energy | **Very low** (~ 0.5 MJ/kg) | High (~ 2.0 MJ/kg) |
| Compressive strength | 3.5–7.0 N/mm² | 3.5–10 N/mm² |
| Water absorption | 10–15 % | 15–20 % |
| Thermal insulation | **Better** (lower conductivity) | Moderate |
| Cost | **20–40 % lower** | Higher |
| Carbon footprint | **Negligible** | High (kiln firing) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question:** Explain with sketches the **filler slab technology** for cost-effective construction. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and the materials commonly used as fillers.

**Model Answer Outline:**

**(a) Concept and Construction (7 marks):**

A filler slab is a modified reinforced concrete slab in which **lightweight, low-cost filler materials** are permanently embedded in the **tension zone (soffit)** to replace concrete that does not contribute to structural strength. The construction sequence is:

1. **Centring & Shoring:** Conventional shuttering is provided and supported by props.
2. **Placing Reinforcement:** Main tension steel bars (10–12 mm dia) are placed in narrow *ribs* (100–150 mm wide) running in one direction at 300–450 mm centre-to-centre.
3. **Placing Fillers:** Filler blocks/tiles (50–75 mm thick) are placed between the ribs in the soffit zone.
4. **Placing Distribution Steel:** A light mesh (6 mm @ 150 mm c/c) is laid in the top compression layer.
5. **Concreting:** Concrete is poured in two layers — the ribs and the top 50–60 mm compression layer. Vibration is done with a needle vibrator.
6. **Curing:** Water-cured for **14 days**.

**Cross-Section Sketch (Textual Description):**

```
  Top Layer      :  50 mm solid concrete + 6 mm mesh
  Filler Zone    :  70 mm hollow clay tiles (placed between ribs)
  Ribs           :  100 mm wide, contain 10 or 12 mm main bars
  Cover          :  20 mm clear cover at soffit
  Slab Total     : 150 mm thick
```

**(b) Advantages (4 marks):**
- **20–30 % concrete saving** → direct cost benefit.
- **Reduced dead load** → smaller columns & foundations.
- **Better thermal insulation** (hollow clay tiles have low conductivity).
- **Uses locally available, low-cost fillers** → sustainable.
- **Simple technology**, can be done by semi-skilled labour.

**(c) Limitations (2 marks):**
- Not suitable for **heavy floor loads** (industrial buildings).
- **Difficult to repair** if filler is damaged.
- **Skilled supervision** required to maintain rib alignment.
- Risk of **cracks between filler and ribs** if workmanship is poor.

**(d) Common Filler Materials (1 mark):**
- Hollow clay tiles
- Broken brick pieces
- Coconut shells (after treatment)
- Hollow concrete blocks
- Expanded polystyrene (EPS) panels

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Do **NOT confuse** the *Voided Slab* (voids in the neutral axis, full-depth spheres) with the *Filler Slab* (fillers in the *tension zone*, soffit placement). Examiners will award **zero marks** if you interchange them.
> 2. In soil-cement blocks, the cement is specified as a **percentage of dry soil weight**, *not* of total mix weight. A common error is using 8 % of the wet mix — this is **wrong**.
> 3. Rapid Wall panels are **not load-bearing on their own** — the edge cells must be filled with reinforced concrete for structural action. Mentioning this is a "must" for full marks.
> 4. In cost-saving calculations, always state the **assumed unit cost** of conventional vs. CEC material — examiners look for the "cost per m²" comparison, not just a verbal claim.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Cost-effective construction** is anchored on the **3R principle** (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle) and aims at **30–40 % total cost reduction** in low-rise buildings.
- **Rapid Wall:** Prefabricated **gypsum + glass-fibre** panels, **124 mm thick**, 11.5 m² per panel, *no curing*, *no plastering*. Edge cells filled with **M20 concrete + rebar** for shear.
- **Soil-Cement Blocks:** Cement content = **5–10 %** of *dry soil weight*. Compacted at **5–7 N/mm²**. Cured **14–28 days**. Target strength = **5 N/mm²** at 28 days. **Not suitable** for organic clays.
- **Voided Slab:** Hollow spheres in the **neutral axis** (mid-depth). Weight reduction = **up to 30 %**. *Common confusion:* voids are placed in the *neutral axis*, not the *tension zone*.
- **Filler Slab:** Fillers in the **tension zone (soffit)**. Concrete saving = **20–30 %**. Common fillers: clay tiles, broken bricks, hollow concrete blocks, EPS, coconut shells.
- **Key ratios to remember:** $k_v$ (void fraction) for voided slab ≈ **0.30**; concrete saving for filler slab ≈ **25 %**; cement for soil-cement block ≈ **8 %** of dry soil.
- **Embodied energy ranking (lowest → highest):** Soil-cement block → Rapid Wall → Filler slab → Voided slab → Conventional burnt-brick + RCC slab.
- **For 14-mark KTU questions**, *always* include a **flow diagram** (moulding → curing → testing for blocks; slurry → mould → erection for Rapid Wall).
- **Quick formula for any cost-saving problem:** $\text{Saving (\%)} = \dfrac{C_{\text{conv}} - C_{\text{CEC}}}{C_{\text{conv}}} \times 100$
- **Quick formula for slab weight:** $w = \gamma_c \cdot t \cdot (1 - k_v)$ for voided, $w = \gamma_c \cdot t - \rho_f \cdot t_f \cdot (\text{coverage ratio})$ for filler.
- **Highest KTU-yield keywords** to use in answers: *embodied energy, 3R principle, neutral axis, stabilised mud block, dry construction, cellular panel, shear column, compaction pressure, OMC, C-S-H gel.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concept of Prestressing - Fundamental Understanding of Pre-Tensioned and Post-Tensioned Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET611 - INTRODUCTION TO CONSTRUCTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Concept of Prestressing - Fundamental Understanding of Pre-Tensioned and Post-Tensioned Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Prestressed Concrete (PSC)** is a high-performance composite construction material in which internal compressive stresses of significant magnitude are deliberately induced in a concrete member, prior to the application of external service loads, by tensioning high-strength steel tendons (wires, strands, or bars) against the concrete section. This engineered state of pre-compression is designed to counteract the tensile stresses that develop under working loads, thereby substantially improving the cracking resistance, serviceability, and load-carrying capacity of the structural element.

The formal classification as per the **Indian Standard IS 1343:2012** (Code of Practice for Prestressed Concrete) and **IS 784:2001** (Prestressed Concrete Poles) recognises two principal construction methodologies:

1. **Pre-tensioning** (also called *pretensioning* or *Hoyer method* when using long-line beds): The tendons are stressed against external abutments *before* the concrete is cast.
2. **Post-tensioning**: The tendons are stressed *after* the concrete has hardened, using mechanical anchorages at the member ends.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental distinction between pre-tensioning and post-tensioning must be articulated in terms of **(a) sequence of stressing, (b) mechanism of force transfer, and (c) typical field application.** Examiners frequently award marks on this three-pronged distinction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stack of Books" Intuition

Imagine you are carrying a horizontal row of **10 books** stacked on top of each other, supported only at both ends. Gravity will try to bend the stack downward, causing the bottom fibre to stretch apart (tension) and the top fibre to compress further. The stack will fail because the friction between the books cannot resist this tension.

Now imagine you **squeeze the entire row tightly with both hands** (applying a horizontal compressive "pre-clamp") *before* you lift the row. The pre-clamp forces the books to press firmly against each other. Even when gravity tries to pull the bottom apart, the pre-existing internal compression counterbalances the tensile pull. The row can now carry the bending load.

**In prestressed concrete:**
- **The "books" = concrete** (strong in compression, weak in tension ≈ **10% of its compressive strength**).
- **The "hand squeeze" = tensile force in the steel tendon**, which is locked into the concrete as an equivalent pre-compression.
- **The "bending due to gravity" = external service loads.**

This pre-compression is what allows PSC beams to span **30 m to 250 m** without intermediate supports — a feat impossible with ordinary reinforced concrete (RCC) above roughly **15–20 m**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant:** High-tensile steel used in PSC has a characteristic ultimate tensile strength (**fpu**) in the range of **1860 MPa to 2070 MPa**, compared to **415 MPa to 550 MPa** for ordinary mild steel reinforcement. The tendons are initially stressed to about **70%–80% of fpu**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Prestress? The Limitation of Reinforced Concrete

Ordinary Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC) has an inherent inefficiency:
- Concrete cracks at a tensile stress of approximately **3–5 MPa**.
- The steel reinforcement only becomes structurally active *after* the concrete cracks (i.e., at the limit state).
- Under service loads, the concrete in the tension zone of an RCC beam is **unusable** for resisting load.

PSC eliminates this inefficiency by ensuring the **entire concrete section remains in compression** under service conditions, thus utilising the full cross-section.

## 2.2 Pre-Tensioning — System Anatomy & Operational Logic

**Definition:** A method of prestressing in which the tendons are tensioned against independent external anchorages *before* the placement of fresh concrete, and the prestress force is transferred to the concrete solely through **bond** at the tendon–concrete interface after the concrete hardens and the tendons are released.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic
1. **Stressing Bed Preparation:** High-strength tendons (7-wire strands, **12.7 mm** or **15.2 mm** diameter) are laid out along a long, heavy-duty steel casting bed (often **100–200 m** long for mass production).
2. **Initial Stressing:** Each tendon is pulled using a hydraulic jack against fixed bulkheads. The initial prestress force **Pi** ≈ **70% of GUTS** (Guaranteed Ultimate Tensile Strength) is applied.
3. **Placing Reinforcement & Casting:** Conventional reinforcement (stirrups, links) is placed, and high-strength concrete (typically **M40 to M60**) is cast around the stressed tendons.
4. **Accelerated Curing:** The concrete is steam-cured to achieve the required transfer strength (**fci** ≈ **0.8 fck**, or roughly **30–35 MPa**) within **24–48 hours**.
5. **Detensioning (Force Transfer):** The hydraulic jacks are released. The tendons, now bonded to the hardened concrete, attempt to shorten, but cannot, so they **transfer the compressive force into the concrete via bond**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bond is the soul of pre-tensioning.** The transfer length (**Lt**) — the distance over which the tendon gradually re-enters the concrete — is typically **50 to 100 times the tendon diameter** (e.g., **≈ 750 mm for a 15.2 mm strand**).

## 2.3 Post-Tensioning — System Anatomy & Operational Logic

**Definition:** A method of prestressing in which the tendons are placed within ducts (or sheaths) embedded in the concrete *after* casting, and the prestress force is transferred to the concrete **after** it has hardened, using **mechanical anchorages** at the live (jacking) end.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic
1. **Duct Placement:** Hollow metal or HDPE ducts (typically **50–100 mm** internal diameter) are positioned inside the formwork following the **cable profile** (parabolic or harped).
2. **Concrete Casting:** Concrete is placed; ducts are prevented from collapsing.
3. **Curing & Hardening:** Concrete attains its design strength (**fck** ≥ **40 MPa**).
4. **Tendon Threading:** Strands or bars are pushed or pulled through the ducts.
5. **Stressing:** A hydraulic jack tensions the tendon from one end (single-end stressing) or both ends (double-end stressing), stretching it to the required force.
6. **Anchoring:** Wedges or anchor nuts lock the tendon at the live end (and dead end).
7. **Grouting (Bonded System):** A cementitious grout is injected into the duct to fill voids, provide corrosion protection, and develop bond. *Unbonded systems* omit this step; tendons remain free inside grease-filled sheaths.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Friction Losses in Post-Tensioning:** The tendon experiences **friction loss** along its length due to the **wobble effect** (unintentional duct deviation) and the **curvature effect** (intentional profile bending). The loss is governed by: **Pt = Pi · e^(-μα - KL)**, where *μ* is the curvature coefficient, *α* is the cumulative angle change, *K* is the wobble coefficient, and *L* is the length.

## 2.4 Comparative Anatomy: Pre-Tensioning vs Post-Tensioning

| Parameter | Pre-Tensioning | Post-Tensioning |
|---|---|---|
| **Sequence of stressing** | Before concrete casting | After concrete hardening |
| **Mechanism of force transfer** | Bond between tendon & concrete | Mechanical anchorage (end bearing) |
| **Typical tendon type** | 7-wire strands (12.7 mm / 15.2 mm) | Strands, wires, or bars in ducts |
| **Concrete strength at transfer** | fci ≈ 30–35 MPa (lower) | fck (full design strength) |
| **Site applicability** | Mostly precast factory production | Cast-in-situ and precast; bridges, slabs, silos |
| **Member size** | Smaller cross-sections, repetitive units | Large members, long spans, heavy loads |
| **Prestress losses (initial)** | Elastic shortening, relaxation, shrinkage, creep | Anchorage slip, friction, elastic shortening |
| **End anchorage hardware** | Not required | Critical — Freyssinet, BBRV, Gifford-Udall, Leonhardt systems |
| **Cost of equipment** | High capital cost (long-line bed) | Lower; portable jacks suffice |
| **Loss of Prestress** | Comparatively higher in precast yard conditions | Engineer can control via stage-wise stressing |
| **Typical application** | Railway sleepers, electric poles, bridge girders, floor planks | Continuous bridges, nuclear containments, high-rise flat slabs, LNG tanks |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pre-tensioning** dominates **mass-produced standard components** where economy of scale justifies the long-line bed. Indian examples: **prestressed concrete sleepers for Indian Railways** (manufactured by companies like UltraTech, PSCI), **PSC electric poles** (Tata, KEC), **hollow core planks** (used in commercial building floors).
- **Post-tensioning** is the **default choice for long-span and in-situ construction**. Iconic examples: the **Bandra-Worli Sea Link** (extradosed post-tensioned cable-stay hybrid), **Chennai Metro viaducts**, **Statue of Unity** (foundation post-tensioned), **Dubai Creek Tower**, and almost every modern **flat slab in a high-rise**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stress in concrete (combined axial + bending) | $\sigma_{c} = \dfrac{P}{A} \pm \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | P = prestress force (N); A = area (mm²); M = moment (N·mm); y = distance from NA (mm); I = moment of inertia (mm⁴) | Sign depends on fibre location |
| 2 | Stress in tendon | $\sigma_{s} = \dfrac{P}{A_{s}}$ | As = area of steel (mm²) | Basic tensile check |
| 3 | Initial prestress | $P_{i} = 0.70 \text{ to } 0.80 \cdot A_{s} \cdot f_{pu}$ | fpu = ultimate tensile strength of tendon (MPa) | Range as per IS 1343 |
| 4 | Effective prestress | $P_{e} = P_{i} - \text{Total Losses}$ | Losses in N | Used for serviceability design |
| 5 | Total loss of prestress | $\Delta P = \Delta P_{es} + \Delta P_{sh} + \Delta P_{cr} + \Delta P_{rel} + \Delta P_{anc} + \Delta P_{fric}$ | All in N; es = elastic shortening, sh = shrinkage, cr = creep, rel = relaxation, anc = anchorage slip, fric = friction | KTU often asks to list/enumerate |
| 6 | Friction loss in PT (post-tensioning) | $P_{x} = P_{i} \cdot e^{-(\mu \alpha + K L)}$ | Pi = force at jack; μ = curvature coef.; α = angle (rad); K = wobble coef. (per m); L = length (m) | Exponential decay along duct |
| 7 | Eccentric prestress moment | $M_{p} = P \cdot e$ | e = eccentricity of tendon (mm) | Magnitude of induced moment |
| 8 | Equivalent (transformed) area | $A_{eq} = A_{c} + (m - 1) A_{s}$ | m = modular ratio = Es/Ec; Ac = gross concrete area | Used in elastic analysis |
| 9 | Transfer length (pre-tensioning) | $L_{t} \approx 50 \phi \text{ to } 100 \phi$ | φ = tendon diameter (mm) | Bond-based transfer zone |
| 10 | Minimum section modulus (no tension) | $Z \geq \dfrac{P \cdot e}{f_{t}}$ | ft = modulus of rupture / allowable tensile stress | Serviceability condition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Never confuse the **initial prestress (Pi)** with the **effective prestress (Pe)**. Pi is *what the jack applies*; Pe is *what remains after all losses*. In design, the load-balancing and stress-checks at service use **Pe**, while transfer-stage checks use **Pi**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Stress Distribution in a Prestressed Concrete Section (Eccentric Tendon)

Consider a rectangular concrete beam of cross-section **B × D** with a straight tendon carrying prestress force **P** at an eccentricity **e** below the neutral axis. The concrete is subjected to:
- An **axial compressive force** = **P** (acting at the centroid of the tendon)
- A **bending moment** = **P·e** (since the force is eccentric to the centroidal axis)

### Step 1: Define Internal Forces in the Section

The internal actions induced by the prestress alone, on any horizontal section, are:
- Axial thrust: N = P (compressive)
- Bending moment: M = P·e

### Step 2: Apply Superposition of Stresses

The stress at any fibre located at distance *y* from the centroid is the **sum** of the uniform compressive stress and the bending stress:

$$
\sigma_{c}(y) = -\dfrac{P}{A} \; - \; \dfrac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}
$$

The **negative sign** indicates compression (using the convention that tension is positive).

### Step 3: Evaluate Stresses at Extreme Fibres

For a rectangular section (A = BD, I = BD³/12, y = ± D/2):

**At the bottom fibre** (y = + D/2, the fibre where the tendon is closer):

$$
\sigma_{c,\text{bottom}} = -\dfrac{P}{BD} \; - \; \dfrac{P \cdot e \cdot (D/2)}{BD^{3}/12} = -\dfrac{P}{BD} \left( 1 + \dfrac{6e}{D} \right)
$$

**At the top fibre** (y = − D/2, opposite to tendon):

$$
\sigma_{c,\text{top}} = -\dfrac{P}{BD} \; + \; \dfrac{6P \cdot e}{BD^{2}} = -\dfrac{P}{BD} \left( 1 - \dfrac{6e}{D} \right)
$$

### Step 4: Verify the Logical Intuition

If the tendon is **straight and concentric** (e = 0), both fibres carry uniform compression $\sigma_{c} = -P/A$. ✓

If the tendon is **eccentric downward** (e > 0, but < D/6), the bottom fibre is *more* compressed and the top fibre is *less* compressed (but still in compression). ✓

If **e > D/6** (over-eccentric), the top fibre experiences **tension** even under pure prestress — this is the basis of the **kern point** concept.

### Step 5: Add the Effect of External Loads

When the beam carries an external downward load, an additional bending moment **M_ext** is induced. The total stress becomes:

$$
\sigma_{c,\text{total}}(y) = -\dfrac{P}{A} \; - \; \dfrac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I} \; + \; \dfrac{M_{\text{ext}} \cdot y}{I}
$$

The **design objective** is to choose **P, e, and the cable profile** such that for the bottom fibre (under service load), the net stress remains compressive (no cracking) — and for the top fibre, it does not exceed the allowable compressive strength **0.45 fck** as per IS 1343.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Stress Check (Standard KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A simply-supported PSC beam of rectangular section **200 mm × 400 mm** (B × D) is prestressed by a straight tendon carrying an effective prestress of **Pe = 800 kN** at an eccentricity of **e = 80 mm** below the centroid. The beam spans **8 m** and carries an external service load producing a maximum bending moment of **M = 100 kN·m** at midspan. Compute the stresses at the top and bottom fibres.

### Given Data
- B = 200 mm; D = 400 mm
- Pe = 800 kN = 800 × 10³ N
- e = 80 mm (below centroid)
- M = 100 kN·m = 100 × 10⁶ N·mm

### Step 1: Section Properties

$$
A = B \times D = 200 \times 400 = 80{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{B D^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{200 \times 400^{3}}{12} = 1.0667 \times 10^{9} \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}} = \dfrac{1.0667 \times 10^{9}}{200} = 5.333 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

### Step 2: Uniform Compressive Stress (Axial Component)

$$
\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{P_{e}}{A} = \dfrac{800 \times 10^{3}}{80{,}000} = 10.0 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

### Step 3: Bending Stress Due to Eccentricity (P·e)

$$
M_{p} = P_{e} \cdot e = 800 \times 10^{3} \times 80 = 64 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
$$

$$
\sigma_{b,p} = \dfrac{M_{p}}{Z} = \dfrac{64 \times 10^{6}}{5.333 \times 10^{6}} = 12.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 4: Bending Stress Due to External Load

$$
\sigma_{b,\text{ext}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{ext}}}{Z} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{6}}{5.333 \times 10^{6}} = 18.75 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 5: Combine for the Bottom Fibre (y = +200 mm)

The tendon causes **additional compression** at the bottom; the external load causes **tension** at the bottom.

$$
\sigma_{c,\text{bottom}} = -10.0 - 12.0 + 18.75 = -3.25 \text{ MPa (compression)}
$$

**Interpretation:** The bottom fibre remains in **3.25 MPa compression** — no cracking. ✓

### Step 6: Combine for the Top Fibre (y = −200 mm)

The tendon causes **tension** at the top (eccentric pull-down); the external load causes **compression** at the top.

$$
\sigma_{c,\text{top}} = -10.0 + 12.0 - 18.75 = -16.75 \text{ MPa (compression)}
$$

### Step 7: Validation

Maximum compressive stress at top = **16.75 MPa** < 0.45 fck (assuming M40, this is 0.45 × 40 = 18 MPa). ✓ **Section is SAFE** for the M40 grade.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** When asked *"check the stresses"*, the KTU model answer expects **two fibre stresses, sign-convention clarity, and a final comment on the safety against the allowable limits (0.45 fck for compression, 0 for no-tension design)**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Loss of Prestress

| Loss Type | Pre-Tensioning | Post-Tensioning | Typical Magnitude | Phased |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Elastic shortening of concrete | ✓ | ✓ | 4–6% of Pi | Immediate |
| Relaxation of steel | ✓ | ✓ | 2–5% of Pi | Time-dependent |
| Shrinkage of concrete | ✓ | ✓ | 3–5% of Pi | Long-term |
| Creep of concrete | ✓ | ✓ | 5–8% of Pi | Long-term |
| Anchorage slip | ✗ | ✓ | 1–3% of Pi | Immediate |
| Friction (wobble + curvature) | ✗ | ✓ | 5–15% of Pi (along length) | At transfer |
| **Typical total loss** | **15–25% of Pi** | **20–30% of Pi** | — | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pre-Tensioning — Process Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STEP 1: TENDON PLACEMENT"] --> A2["STEP 2: INITIAL STRESSING IN LONG-LINE BED"]
    A2 --> A3["STEP 3: HYDRAULIC JACK PULLS TENDON TO 70-80 PERCENT OF GUTS"]
    A3 --> A4["STEP 4: ANCHOR TENDON AGAINST BULKHEAD ABUTMENTS"]
    A4 --> A5["STEP 5: PLACE REINFORCEMENT AND CAST HIGH-STRENGTH CONCRETE M40 PLUS"]
    A5 --> A6["STEP 6: ACCELERATED STEAM CURING FOR 24 TO 48 HOURS"]
    A6 --> A7["STEP 7: CONCRETE REACHES TRANSFER STRENGTH OF 30 TO 35 MPA"]
    A7 --> A8["STEP 8: DETENSION JACKS RELEASE TENDONS GRADUALLY"]
    A8 --> A9["STEP 9: TENDON CONTRACTS AND TRANSFERS COMPRESSION TO CONCRETE VIA BOND"]
    A9 --> A10["STEP 10: MEMBER STORED OR DISPATCHED TO SITE AS PRECAST UNIT"]
```

## 4.2 Post-Tensioning — Process Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1["STEP 1: PLACE DUCTS IN FORMWORK FOLLOWING CABLE PROFILE"] --> B2["STEP 2: FIX ANCHORAGES AT LIVE AND DEAD ENDS"]
    B2 --> B3["STEP 3: CAST CONCRETE AROUND DUCTS WITHOUT COLLAPSE"]
    B3 --> B4["STEP 4: CURE CONCRETE TO FULL DESIGN STRENGTH FCK"]
    B4 --> B5["STEP 5: THREAD TENDONS THROUGH DUCTS"]
    B5 --> B6["STEP 6: STRESS TENDON USING HYDRAULIC JACK FROM LIVE END"]
    B6 --> B7["STEP 7: MEASURE ELONGATION AND JACKING FORCE TO VERIFY"]
    B7 --> B8["STEP 8: LOCK TENDON USING WEDGE ANCHORAGE AT LIVE END"]
    B8 --> B9["STEP 9: INJECT CEMENT GROUT INTO DUCT FOR BOND AND CORROSION PROTECTION"]
    B9 --> B10["STEP 10: SEAL ANCHORAGE RECESS AND APPLY PROTECTIVE GROUT CAP"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pre-Tensioned vs Post-Tensioned System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PreT["PRE-TENSIONED CONSTRUCTION"]
        PT1["Stressing Bed (Abutment)"] --> PT2["Tendon Under Tension"]
        PT2 --> PT3["Concrete Cast Around Tendon"]
        PT3 --> PT4["Cure and Harden"]
        PT4 --> PT5["Detension and Bond Transfer"]
    end

    subgraph PostT["POST-TENSIONED CONSTRUCTION"]
        PST1["Duct Embedded in Concrete"] --> PST2["Concrete Cured First"]
        PST2 --> PST3["Tendon Inserted in Duct"]
        PST3 --> PST4["Tendon Tensioned by Jack"]
        PST4 --> PST5["Anchored Mechanically at Ends"]
    end

    PreT --- PostT
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Force Transfer Mechanism Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BlockPre["PRE-TENSIONING FORCE TRANSFER"]
        direction TB
        PTF1["Tendon"] -->|Bond Shear Stress| PTF2["Concrete"]
        PTF2 -->|Bond Shear Stress| PTF3["Tendon"]
    end

    subgraph BlockPost["POST-TENSIONING FORCE TRANSFER"]
        direction TB
        PSF1["Tendon"] -->|End Bearing via Anchor| PSF2["Steel Plate"]
        PSF2 -->|Compressive Reaction| PSF3["Concrete Section"]
    end
```

## 4.5 Cross-Section Stress Profile (Conceptual Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SE1["Top Fibre - Compression Induced"] --> SE2["Neutral Axis"]
    SE2 --> SE3["Bottom Fibre - Maximum Compression Due to Eccentric Tendon"]
    SE3 --> SE4["Tendon Located at Eccentricity e Below Centroid"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define prestressed concrete. Distinguish between pre-tensioning and post-tensioning in two points.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*Prestressed concrete* is a structural material in which permanent internal compressive stresses are induced in a concrete member by tensioning high-strength steel tendons against it, so as to counteract the tensile stresses produced by service loads. **(1 Mark)**

**Two distinguishing points:**
1. **Sequence:** In pre-tensioning, the tendons are stressed *before* casting the concrete; in post-tensioning, they are stressed *after* the concrete has hardened. **(1 Mark)**
2. **Force Transfer Mechanism:** Pre-tensioning transfers prestress to concrete via **bond** at the steel–concrete interface; post-tensioning transfers it via **mechanical anchorages** at the ends of the member. **(1 Mark)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any six types of losses of prestress in a post-tensioned concrete member.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — ½ mark per item):**
1. Elastic shortening of concrete
2. Shrinkage of concrete
3. Creep of concrete
4. Relaxation of steel
5. Anchorage slip
6. Friction (wobble + curvature effects in the duct)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently omit **friction loss** when listing losses, even though it is the *dominant* loss in long, curved post-tensioned tendons. Always remember the mnemonic **"E-S-C-R-A-F"** (Elastic, Shrinkage, Creep, Relaxation, Anchorage slip, Friction) — six items = full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **step-by-step procedure of pre-tensioning**. Mention any **three advantages** and **two limitations** of pre-tensioning. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular PSC beam, **150 mm wide × 300 mm deep**, is prestressed by a straight tendon carrying an initial prestress of **Pi = 500 kN** at an eccentricity of **e = 60 mm**. The **losses of prestress** amount to **80 kN**. The beam carries a service load that produces a maximum bending moment of **M = 45 kN·m** at midspan. Determine the stresses at the **top and bottom extreme fibres** of the concrete. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Procedure of Pre-Tensioning (5 marks for the step-wise write-up + sketch):**
1. The high-tensile steel tendons (typically 7-wire strands) are placed along a long-line casting bed between two fixed bulkheads (abutments). The bed may extend **100 m to 200 m**, allowing many similar units to be cast simultaneously.
2. Each tendon is tensioned using a hydraulic jack. The jack pulls the tendon to the required initial prestress force, which is typically **70%–80%** of the Guaranteed Ultimate Tensile Strength (GUTS). The force is measured using a pressure gauge and verified by tendon elongation.
3. Once stressed, the tendon is locked at the bulkhead using anchor grips.
4. Reinforcement cages (links, stirrups, distribution steel) are placed, and high-strength concrete (**M40 or higher**) is cast, completely embedding the stressed tendons.
5. The concrete is cured, often with **accelerated steam curing** to achieve the transfer strength of about **30–35 MPa** within 24–48 hours.
6. The hydraulic jacks are released gradually. As the tendons try to shorten (contract), they cannot slip because they are **bonded** to the surrounding concrete. The pre-compression is transferred to the concrete through **bond stresses** along the transfer length (**L_t**).

**Three Advantages (1 mark):**
- Mass production is highly economical; the same bed produces hundreds of units.
- The bond mechanism is self-anchoring — no end anchorages required, no grouting needed.
- Reliable quality control in a factory environment.

**Two Limitations (1 mark):**
- Heavy capital investment for the long-line bed; uneconomical for small or unique units.
- Limited to standard, repeatable shapes; large cast-in-situ members cannot be pre-tensioned on site.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- B = 150 mm, D = 300 mm
- Pi = 500 kN, Losses = 80 kN → Pe = 500 − 80 = 420 kN = 420 × 10³ N
- e = 60 mm (tendon below centroid)
- M = 45 kN·m = 45 × 10⁶ N·mm

**Step 1: Section Properties (1 mark)**

$$
A = B \times D = 150 \times 300 = 45{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{B D^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{150 \times 300^{2}}{6} = 2.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 2: Uniform Compressive Stress (1 mark)**

$$
\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{P_{e}}{A} = \dfrac{420 \times 10^{3}}{45{,}000} = 9.33 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

**Step 3: Bending Stress Due to Eccentric Prestress (1.5 marks)**

$$
\sigma_{b,p} = \dfrac{P_{e} \cdot e}{Z} = \dfrac{420 \times 10^{3} \times 60}{2.25 \times 10^{6}} = 11.20 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Bending Stress Due to External Load (1.5 marks)**

$$
\sigma_{b,\text{ext}} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{45 \times 10^{6}}{2.25 \times 10^{6}} = 20.00 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5: Net Stress at Bottom Fibre (1 mark)**

The bottom fibre is the side **nearest** the tendon (eccentric compression aids, external bending opposes at the bottom).

$$
\sigma_{\text{bottom}} = -9.33 - 11.20 + 20.00 = -0.53 \text{ MPa (compression)}
$$

**Step 6: Net Stress at Top Fibre (1 mark)**

$$
\sigma_{\text{top}} = -9.33 + 11.20 - 20.00 = -18.13 \text{ MPa (compression)}
$$

**Result:**
- Top fibre stress: **18.13 MPa (compressive)**
- Bottom fibre stress: **0.53 MPa (compressive)** — no tension, no cracking. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **step-by-step procedure of post-tensioning**. Discuss the **Freyssinet anchorage system** briefly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A post-tensioned concrete beam of section **250 mm × 500 mm** has a **parabolic tendon** with an eccentricity of **e = 100 mm** at midspan. The effective prestress is **Pe = 1000 kN**. Calculate the **uniform equivalent load** that the tendon exerts on the concrete, assuming the tendon has a **span of 10 m** and a **total angular change α = 0.40 radian** from one end to the midspan. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step-by-Step Procedure of Post-Tensioning (5 marks):**
1. The formwork is erected, and ducts (sheaths) — usually **50 mm to 100 mm** diameter corrugated metal or HDPE — are positioned in the formwork along the designed cable profile (parabolic for simply-supported beams, harped for continuous beams).
2. Anchorages (e.g., Freyssinet cones) are fixed at both the live (jacking) end and the dead (anchoring) end.
3. Concrete is cast around the ducts, ensuring the ducts do not collapse or displace.
4. After the concrete attains the design strength (**fck ≥ 40 MPa**), the tendons (strands, wires, or bars) are threaded through the ducts.
5. A hydraulic jack at the live end tensions the tendon. The applied force is verified by **jack pressure and tendon elongation**, and both should agree within **±5%** (per IS 1343).
6. When the required force is reached, the tendon is locked in the anchorage using **wedge grips or anchor nuts**. The jack is removed.
7. (For bonded systems) Cement grout is injected under pressure into the duct to fill voids, prevent corrosion, and develop bond.
8. The anchorage recess is sealed with a protective cap and grout to prevent moisture ingress.

**Freyssinet Anchorage System (2 marks):**
The Freyssinet system uses a **conical anchorage** with a cylindrical steel anchor head and a **trumpet-shaped housing**. Multiple strands (typically 6, 12, or more) pass through holes in the anchor head. **Hardened-steel conical wedges** (split into two or three segments) are driven into the conical recesses, gripping each strand as the jack releases. The Freyssinet system is among the most widely used multi-strand anchorages worldwide and is rated for tendons up to **5000 kN** or more.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Effective prestress: Pe = 1000 kN
- Eccentricity at midspan: e = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- Span L = 10 m (assume simply-supported)
- Total angular change α = 0.40 rad

**Concept: Equivalent Uplift Load (2 marks)**

A parabolic tendon exerts an **upward uniform load** on the concrete equivalent to the vertical component of the tendon force per unit length. For a parabolic profile with total sag = 2e over span L:

**Step 1: Calculate Equivalent Upward UDL (2 marks)**

$$
w_{e} = \dfrac{8 \cdot P_{e} \cdot e}{L^{2}} = \dfrac{8 \times 1000 \times 0.1}{(10)^{2}} = \dfrac{800}{100} = 8.0 \text{ kN/m (upward)}
$$

**Step 2: Verify the Friction Loss (2 marks)**

Using the friction formula with assumed μ = 0.25 and K = 0.0015/m (typical for metal sheaths):

$$
P_{x} = P_{e} \cdot e^{-(\mu \alpha + K L)} = 1000 \cdot e^{-(0.25 \times 0.40 + 0.0015 \times 10)}
$$

$$
P_{x} = 1000 \cdot e^{-(0.10 + 0.015)} = 1000 \cdot e^{-0.115} = 1000 \times 0.8914 = 891.4 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Friction Loss Magnitude (1 mark)**

$$
\Delta P_{\text{fric}} = 1000 - 891.4 = 108.6 \text{ kN} \; (\approx 10.86\% \text{ of effective prestress})
$$

**Final Answer:** The equivalent upward uniform load exerted by the parabolic tendon on the concrete is **8.0 kN/m**, and the friction loss along the tendon is **108.6 kN** (~10.9% of the effective prestress).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top Reasons Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** — many students write "tensile" stress at the bottom fibre when it is actually compressive. KTU strictly deducts **1 mark per wrong sign** in stress problems.
> 2. **Using Pi instead of Pe** — the problem statement says "losses = 80 kN", yet many students continue to use Pi = 500 kN instead of Pe = 420 kN. This is the **most common single error** and costs full method marks.
> 3. **Mixing up tendons' position in pre- and post-tensioning** — writing "tendon stressed after casting" for pre-tensioning is a **fatal conceptual error** (zero credit for that part).
> 4. **Not stating the bond-vs-anchorage distinction** — for 14-mark questions, the conceptual paragraph MUST distinguish both systems on the basis of (a) timing, (b) transfer mechanism, and (c) field use. Missing even one costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **Inadequate units** — leaving force in "kN" and stress in "N/mm²" without writing "MPa" or vice versa is a recurrent **½-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Core idea of prestressing:** Introduce *intentional pre-compression* in concrete to nullify tensile stresses under service loads. Concrete is strong in compression (≈**30–60 MPa**) but weak in tension (cracks at ≈**3–5 MPa**). The pre-compression **buys tensile capacity**.
- **Pre-tensioning** = stress tendons *first*, cast *second*, transfer force by *bond*. Used in **factories** for **sleepers, poles, planks, bridge girders**.
- **Post-tensioning** = cast *first*, stress *second*, transfer force by *anchorages* (Freyssinet, BBRV, etc.). Used for **long-span bridges, high-rise slabs, nuclear structures, LNG tanks**.
- **Initial prestress Pi** = force applied by the jack (≈**70–80% of fpu**).
- **Effective prestress Pe** = Pi − total losses (typically **15–25% of Pi lost**).
- **Six losses:** Elastic shortening, Shrinkage, Creep, Relaxation, Anchorage slip, Friction. Mnemonic: **E-S-C-R-A-F**.
- **Friction loss (PT only):** $\Delta P = P_{i} (1 - e^{-(\mu \alpha + K L)})$ — increases with tendon length, curvature, and unintended duct wobble.
- **Eccentricity rule:** For a section to remain *uncracked* under pure prestress alone, the tendon should lie within the **kern** (for rectangular sections: |e| ≤ **D/6**).
- **Combined-stress formula:** $\sigma_{c} = -P/A \pm P \cdot e \cdot y / I \pm M_{\text{ext}} \cdot y / I$ — the bedrock of every KTU numerical.
- **Allowable stresses (IS 1343):** Compression ≤ **0.45 fck**, Tension = **0** (for class 1 / no-tension members), Tension ≤ **3 MPa** (for class 2 / limited-tension members).
- **Transfer length (PT concrete):** L_t ≈ **50 φ to 100 φ** — region over which bond develops in pre-tensioned members; never design anchorage zones within this length.
- **High-strength materials** are non-negotiable: concrete **M40 to M60**; steel strands with **fpu = 1860–2070 MPa**. Ordinary mild steel (Fe415) is *not* used in PSC.
- **Common KTU keywords to memorise:** "Bond transfer", "Anchor wedge", "Equivalent load", "Load balancing", "Kern point", "Class 1/2/3 structure", "GUTS", "Fpu", "Modular ratio m", "Parabolic profile", "Stage prestressing".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction 3D Printing (Brief Discussion Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET611 - INTRODUCTION TO CONSTRUCTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Construction 3D Printing (Brief Discussion Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Construction 3D Printing (C3DP)** is an **additive manufacturing (AM)** process that uses computer-controlled robotic systems to deposit construction-grade material (typically concrete, mortar, geopolymer, or composite mixtures) in successive horizontal layers, following a pre-programmed digital 3D model, to fabricate building components or entire structures **without the use of traditional formwork**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic is categorized as a **disruptive construction technology** under Module 4, which focuses on modern, sustainable, and automated construction practices. The subject directly links to **Industry 4.0** applications in the built environment.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — "The Cake Icing Machine"** 🍰
> Imagine decorating a cake with a piping bag that moves automatically in precise 3D patterns. Each squeeze of icing adds a thin horizontal line, and when one layer is finished, the nozzle moves up slightly and lays the next layer on top — slowly building a solid, multi-tiered structure. **Construction 3D Printing works exactly this way**, except the "icing" is a specially formulated concrete mix, and the "cake" is an entire house, wall, or bridge. The piping bag is replaced by a **robotic arm, gantry system, or crane-mounted nozzle**, and the recipe is a digital **BIM (Building Information Modeling)** file.

## Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Plain English Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Additive Manufacturing** | Building objects by *adding* material layer-by-layer (opposite of cutting/carving). |
| **Gantry System** | A large overhead bridge-like frame that moves the print head along X-Y-Z axes. |
| **Nozzle / Print Head** | The outlet through which the concrete mix is extruded. |
| **Formwork** | Temporary molds used in conventional concreting; **eliminated in 3DCP**. |
| **Layer Height** | The thickness of each deposited layer, usually **10 mm to 30 mm**. |
| **Build Volume** | The maximum 3D space within which the printer can operate. |
| **Contour Crafting (CC)** | The pioneering extrusion-based 3DCP method by Dr. Behrokh Khoshnevis. |
| **D-Shape** | A binder-jetting based 3DCP method using sand and a binding agent. |

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The keyword here is *"Brief Discussion Only."* Students are expected to know the **principle, types, materials, advantages, limitations, and a few real-world examples** — not the deep chemistry of binder reactions or robotic kinematics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "How"

Construction 3D Printing follows a **closed-loop digital-to-physical workflow** that can be broken into 5 sequential stages:

1. **Digital Modeling:** A 3D model of the structure is created in software (e.g., Autodesk Revit, Rhino + Grasshopper, or specialized slicing software). The model is exported as an **STL or G-code file**.
2. **Slicing:** The software slices the 3D model into hundreds/thousands of **horizontal 2D cross-sections (layers)**. Each slice contains the path the nozzle must trace.
3. **Material Preparation:** A flowable, fast-setting construction material is prepared. The mix must be **pumpable** (to flow through hoses) yet **structurally stable** (to hold its shape immediately after extrusion).
4. **Layer-by-Layer Extrusion:** The print head deposits material along the path of the bottom-most slice. After completing one layer, the nozzle is raised by exactly one **layer height (h)**, and the next layer is printed directly on top.
5. **Curing & Finishing:** The printed structure is left to cure. Reinforcement (steel bars, cables) is often inserted manually between layers or via automated hybrid systems.

## 2.2 The "Why" — Core Engineering Drivers

- **Elimination of Formwork:** Conventional concrete construction spends **35–60% of its labor cost** on formwork. 3DCP removes this entirely.
- **Speed:** A single-story house wall can be printed in **under 24 hours** (e.g., Winsun, China — 10 houses in 24 hours).
- **Material Efficiency:** Only the exact volume of material needed is used — minimal waste.
- **Design Freedom:** Enables **complex curved geometries** that are impossible or uneconomical with traditional casting.
