# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term 'Basic Wind Speed' as per IS 875 (Part 3): 2015. What is its reference height, averaging time, and return period for ordinary buildings?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Basic Wind Speed $V_b$** is defined in IS 875 (Part 3): 2015 as the **3-second gust wind speed** at a height of **10 meters** above **mean ground level** in **open terrain (Terrain Category 2)** with a **return period of 50 years** for ordinary buildings and structures.

> **Reference values (key parameters):**
> - Averaging time: **3 seconds (gust speed)**
> - Reference height: **10 m above ground**
> - Terrain: **Category 2 — Open terrain with scattered obstructions**
> - Return period: **50 years** (for ordinary buildings; 100 years for cyclonic regions)
> - Direction: **Any direction from cardinal points**

The basic wind speed is mapped isopach-wise in **Annex A of IS 875 (Part 3)**, and is used as the fundamental input for computing design wind speed at any height.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three design measures to improve the fire resistance of reinforced concrete (RC) structural members.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Three design measures to improve fire resistance of RC members are:
>
> 1. **Adequate Concrete Cover to Reinforcement:** Increasing cover to 25–50 mm delays heat transfer to rebars, maintaining bond strength.
> 2. **Use of Lightweight Aggregates (LWA) Concrete:** LWA concrete has better thermal insulation and lower spalling tendency at high temperatures.
> 3. **Provision of Fire-Rated Plaster or Sprayed Coatings:** Vermiculite/gypsum plaster or intumescent paints insulate steel and concrete surfaces, retarding temperature rise.
>
> *(Additional valid measures: Increasing section size, providing additional reinforcement cage, using fiber-reinforced concrete, applying fire-resistant board encasement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the mechanism of fire-induced structural failure in steel and reinforced concrete buildings. Discuss the ISO 834 standard fire curve and its significance in design.**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism of Fire-Induced Failure — Steel Structures:**

Steel is a **non-combustible** material but loses strength rapidly with rising temperature. The critical temperature for structural steel is around **550°C**, where yield strength drops to ~60% of its ambient value. The mechanism involves:

1. **Thermal Expansion:** Steel members expand significantly (~1.2% at 600°C), inducing large deflections and pushing adjacent members outward.
2. **Strength Degradation:** Yield strength reduces progressively. At **300°C** = 100% strength retained; at **550°C** = ~60% retained; at **700°C** = ~30% retained.
3. **Buckling:** Compressed members lose stiffness, leading to flexural or lateral-torsional buckling.
4. **Connection Failure:** Bolted/welded connections weaken; bolts lose pretension, welds soften.
5. **Catastrophic Collapse:** Unprotected steel frames can collapse in 10–20 minutes under severe fire.

**Mechanism of Fire-Induced Failure — RC Structures:**

RC performs better than steel in fire due to the **insulating effect of concrete cover**. Failure mechanism includes:

1. **Spalling of Cover Concrete:** Trapped moisture converts to steam; explosive spalling exposes rebars to direct fire.
2. **Rebar Temperature Rise:** Insufficient cover allows rebars to reach critical temperature.
3. **Bond Degradation:** Bond between rebar and concrete deteriorates, causing loss of composite action.
4. **Concrete Strength Loss:** Above **300°C**, compressive strength drops; above **600°C**, severe loss occurs.
5. **Flexural and Shear Failure:** Members fail in flexure or shear as steel loses strength.

**ISO 834 Standard Fire Curve:**

The ISO 834 curve models the **furnace temperature** in a standard fire test. It is given by:

$$T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

**Significance:**
- Provides a **standardized time-temperature relationship** for testing fire resistance of structural elements.
- Used to **rate structural members** (1-hour, 2-hour, 4-hour ratings).
- **Conservative** as it assumes a fully developed hydrocarbon cellulosic fire.
- Used in IS 1641 for testing in India.

**Fire Resistance Rating (FRR):** The time during which a structural member retains stability, integrity, and insulation under standard fire exposure.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A 4-story RC office building is to be constructed in Seismic Zone IV. The following data is given:**
- Total seismic weight $W = 18{,}000$ kN
- Building height $h = 13$ m
- Importance Factor $I = 1.0$
- Response Reduction Factor $R = 5.0$ (Special Moment Resisting Frame)
- Soil Type: Medium (Type II)
- Spectral acceleration ratio $S_a/g = 2.5$ for the computed period

**Calculate:**
1. Fundamental natural time period $T$
2. Design horizontal seismic coefficient $A_h$
3. Total design base shear $V_B$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Natural Time Period**

For RC moment-resisting frame (without infill):

$$T = 0.075 \, h^{0.75}$$

$$T = 0.075 \times (13)^{0.75}$$

Compute $(13)^{0.75}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(13) &= 2.5649 \\
0.75 \times 2.5649 &= 1.9237 \\
(13)^{0.75} &= e^{1.9237} = 6.844
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$T = 0.075 \times 6.844 = 0.513 \, \text{s} \approx 0.51 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 2: Design Horizontal Seismic Coefficient**

For Zone IV, $Z = 0.24$.

$$A_h = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g}$$

$$A_h = \frac{0.24}{2} \times \frac{1.0}{5.0} \times 2.5$$

$$A_h = 0.12 \times 0.2 \times 2.5 = 0.06$$

**Step 3: Design Base Shear**

$$V_B = A_h \cdot W = 0.06 \times 18{,}000 = 1080 \, \text{kN}$$

$$\boxed{V_B = 1080 \, \text{kN}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the various causes of wind-induced failures in buildings. Explain with sketches the phenomenon of vortex shedding in tall chimneys and towers.**

**Model Answer:**

**Causes of Wind-Induced Failures in Buildings:**

1. **Roof Uplift Failure:** On gable and flat roofs, negative pressure (suction) on the leeward side and edges can exceed the dead weight of roofing material, causing it to lift off.

2. **Overturning of Gable Walls:** During a windstorm, if a window or door on the windward side fails, internal pressure builds up rapidly. The gable wall, acting as a large unbraced diaphragm, is pushed outward and overturns.

3. **Cladding Failure:** External cladding (glass, metal sheets, AC sheets) is subjected to both external suction and internal pressure. Improperly anchored cladding is torn off, often initiating progressive collapse.

4. **Chimney and Tower Failures:** Tall slender structures are vulnerable to:
   - **Vortex shedding**
   - **Galloping**
   - **Flutter** (for flexible structures)

5. **Fatigue Failure of Cladding Connections:** Repeated gusts cause fatigue in screwed/bolted connections, leading to progressive loosening.

6. **Foundation Overturning:** Insufficient dead weight or inadequate anchorage allows the entire building to overturn.

7. **Damage to Canopies, Signboards, and Antennae:** Localized high suction on protrusions.

**Vortex Shedding Phenomenon:**

When wind flows past a **bluff body** (like a chimney, tower, or cable), alternating **vortices** are shed from the two sides. These vortices form at a regular frequency called the **Strouhal frequency**:

$$f_s = \frac{S \cdot V}{D}$$

where:
- $S$ = Strouhal number (≈ **0.2** for circular sections)
- $V$ = wind velocity
- $D$ = diameter of the structure

```
VORTEX SHEDDING (Top View of Chimney)

        Wind Direction
        -------->
        
    Eddy A    Eddy B
   (CCW)      (CW)
     \         /
      \       /
   ___________________
  |                   |
  |    CHIMNEY        |
  |    (Circular)     |
  |___________________|
      /       \
     /         \
    Eddy C    Eddy D
   (CW)       (CCW)
```

When $f_s$ coincides with the **natural frequency $f_n$** of the structure, **resonance** occurs. The structure vibrates perpendicular to wind direction, causing:
- **Lock-in** phenomenon (vortex frequency locks to natural frequency over a range of wind speeds).
- **Oscillatory deflections** in the cross-wind direction.
- **Fatigue damage** at the base, especially at welded or bolted joints.
- **Cracking in masonry chimneys** and ultimate failure.

**Mitigation:** Use of **strakes (helical spoilers)**, **tuned mass dampers (TMD)**, or **aerodynamic fairings** to break up the vortex formation.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A gable-end building is proposed in a coastal area with basic wind speed $V_b = 55$ m/s. The building dimensions are 20 m × 12 m × 6 m (length × width × height) with a flat roof. Calculate the design wind pressure and the total wind force on the building. Use the following data: $k_1 = 1.0$, $k_2 = 0.91$ (for terrain Category 2 at 6 m height), $k_3 = 1.0$, $k_4 = 1.0$, Force coefficient $C_f = 1.2$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Design Wind Speed at Height $z = 6$ m**

$$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$$

$$V_z = 55 \times 1.0 \times 0.91 \times 1.0 \times 1.0$$

$$V_z = 50.05 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Design Wind Pressure**

$$p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$$

$$p_z = 0.6 \times (50.05)^2$$

$$p_z = 0.6 \times 2505.00 = 1503.00 \, \text{N/m}^2$$

$$p_z = 1.503 \, \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate Effective Frontal Area $A_e$**

For a flat-roof building, the effective frontal area is the projected area perpendicular to wind:

$$A_e = \text{Length} \times \text{Height} = 20 \times 6 = 120 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4: Calculate Total Wind Force**

$$F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$$

$$F = 1.2 \times 120 \times 1.503$$

$$F = 216.43 \, \text{kN}$$

$$\boxed{F = 216.43 \, \text{kN}}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

The total wind force of ~216 kN must be resisted by the structural system through shear walls, moment frames, or bracing. The overturning moment at the base is:

$$M_{OT} = F \times \frac{h}{2} = 216.43 \times 3 = 649.3 \, \text{kN·m}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Multiplication Factors in Wind Speed:** Students often write $V_z = V_b$ directly. **Always show $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$** explicitly. (Lose 1–2 marks if omitted.)
>
> 2. **Incorrect Units for Wind Pressure:** $p_z = 0.6 V_z^2$ gives **N/m²**. Many students convert to kN/m² incorrectly. Always state the final unit clearly.
>
> 3. **Confusing $S_a/g$ with $A_h$:** $S_a/g$ is the spectral acceleration ratio; $A_h$ is the final design coefficient. They are different. Don't skip the $Z/2 \cdot I/R$ reduction.
>
> 4. **Natural Period Formula Mismatch:** IS 1893 has different formulas for RC frame, steel frame, and masonry-infilled frames. **Use the correct one based on the structural system.**
>
> 5. **Fire Questions: Mentioning Only One Material:** A common trap is to discuss only steel or only concrete. **Address both** for full marks.
>
> 6. **Forgetting Return Period in Basic Wind Speed Definition:** Examiners specifically check the **3-second gust, 10 m height, 50-year return period** in Part A questions.
>
> 7. **Not Verifying Floor Shear Sum:** In vertical distribution problems, the sum of $Q_i$ must equal $V_B$. Examiners may deduct marks if this is not shown.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Failures Due to Fire, Wind and Earthquakes**

### 🔥 Fire Failures — Key Points

- Standard fire curve: **$T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$**
- Critical temperature for steel: **~550°C** (50% strength loss)
- Fire Resistance Rating measured in **hours** (1, 2, 4 hour ratings common)
- Concrete cover is the **first line of defense** in RC structures
- Spalling is the most dangerous phenomenon in concrete
- Mitigations: **compartmentation, sprinklers, intumescent coatings, encasement**
- Codal reference: **NBC 2016 Part 4, IS 1641, IS 3809**

### 🌬️ Wind Failures — Key Points

- Basic wind speed: **3-second gust, 10 m height, 50-year return period**
- Design wind speed: **$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$**
- Wind pressure: **$p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$** (N/m²)
- Wind force: **$F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$**
- Air density constant: **0.613 ≈ 0.6** (N·s²/m⁴)
- Vortex shedding frequency: **$f_s = S \cdot V / D$** (Strouhal number $S \approx 0.2$ for circular sections)
- Common failures: **roof uplift, cladding tear-off, gable wall overturning, chimney oscillations**
- Codal reference: **IS 875 (Part 3): 2015**

### 🌍 Earthquake Failures — Key Points

- Seismic base shear: **$V_B = A_h \cdot W$**
- Horizontal seismic coefficient: **$A_h = (Z/2) \cdot (I/R) \cdot (S_a/g)$**
- Zone factors: **II = 0.10, III = 0.16, IV = 0.24, V = 0.36**
- Natural period for RC frame: **$T = 0.075 h^{0.75}$**
- Natural period for steel frame: **$T = 0.085 h^{0.75}$**
- Vertical force distribution: **$Q_i = V_B \cdot W_i h_i^2 / \sum W_j h_j^2$**
- Response reduction factor: **$R = 3$ (ordinary), $5$ (SMRF)**
- Common failures: **soft story, X-cracking, beam-column joint failure, pounding, pancake collapse**
- Codal reference: **IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016**

### 🛡️ Design Philosophy — Key Points

- **Ductility over strength** is the seismic design philosophy.
- **Capacity-based design** ensures weak beam-strong column behavior.
- **Redundancy** prevents progressive collapse.
- **Regularity in plan and elevation** improves wind and seismic performance.
- **Separation gap** between adjacent buildings (per IS 4326) prevents pounding.
- **Fire compartments** are sized based on **floor area, occupancy, and travel distance**.
- **Fire dampers** in ducts prevent smoke spread.

### 📊 KTU-Ready Summary Table

| Hazard | Codal Standard | Key Formula | Critical Design Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fire | NBC 2016, IS 1641 | $T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ | Fire Resistance Rating (hrs) |
| Wind | IS 875 (Part 3): 2015 | $p_z = 0.6 V_z^2$ | $C_f, k_2, k_3$ |
| Earthquake | IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016 | $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ | $Z, I, R, S_a/g$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Examination Tips for Module 4:**
> 1. Always cite the **relevant IS code** when answering.
> 2. Draw neat **labeled diagrams** for fire curve, wind direction, and load distribution.
> 3. Show **all steps of numerical calculation** to fetch full marks.
> 4. For 14-mark questions, allocate **2–3 marks for diagrams** and the rest for theory/calculation.
> 5. Remember to convert units correctly: N/m² → kN/m² (÷ 1000); kN·m → N·mm (× 10⁶).
> 6. The 3-second gust definition is **specific to IS 875** — don't confuse with 1-hour mean used in some international codes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foundation Failure - Failures by Alteration, Improper Maintenance, Overloading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET611 - INTRODUCTION TO CONSTRUCTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Foundation Failure - Failures by Alteration, Improper Maintenance, Overloading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Foundation Failure – A Formal Definition

A **Foundation Failure** is defined as the loss of the foundation's ability to safely transmit the superimposed structural loads to the underlying soil or rock stratum, without undergoing unacceptable settlement, shear failure, rotation, or material deterioration that compromises the serviceability and stability of the superstructure.

According to **IS 1904:1986 (Reaffirmed 2020)** – *Code of Practice for Design and Construction of Foundations in Soils: General Requirements*, a foundation is considered to have failed when:

1. The **shear failure** of the supporting soil occurs (bearing capacity exceedance).
2. The **settlement** exceeds the permissible limits (typically **25 mm** for isolated footings on clay and **40 mm** on sand, as per IS 1904).
3. The structure exhibits **tilting, cracking, or differential settlement** beyond serviceability thresholds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Module 4 (Foundation Engineering)**
> The three principal causes of foundation failure explicitly mentioned in the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus are:
> 1. **Failures by Alteration** – Modifications to existing structures.
> 2. **Failures by Improper Maintenance** – Neglect and lack of upkeep.
> 3. **Failures by Overloading** – Exceeding the design load capacity.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **tree standing on soft ground** (a building on soil). The **roots** of the tree are like the **foundation**. Now consider three scenarios:

| Real-World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You build a **concrete deck** on the tree branches (adding extra weight) | **Failure by Alteration** (renovation/extension increases load) |
| You **never water** the tree and ignore termites eating the roots | **Failure by Improper Maintenance** (seepage, root intrusion, neglect) |
| You load the tree with **hundreds of heavy bags** beyond its capacity | **Failure by Overloading** (load exceeds bearing capacity) |

In every case, the tree cracks, tilts, or collapses – just like a building whose foundation fails. The **soil-foundation-superstructure system** behaves as one continuous, interactive unit. Disrupting any link in this chain leads to progressive failure.

---

## 1.3 What Exactly Are These Three Failure Modes?

### A. Failure by Alteration
Any **post-construction modification** to a building – such as adding new floors, removing load-bearing walls, excavating adjacent land, or changing the building's intended use – that alters the original load path, soil-structure interaction, or environmental conditions around the foundation.

### B. Failure by Improper Maintenance
The **gradual deterioration** of the foundation and its surrounding soil due to neglect of routine upkeep: **unattended plumbing leaks**, **blocked drainage**, **vegetation (tree root) intrusion**, **chemical attack on concrete**, and **erosion of bearing strata**.

### C. Failure by Overloading
The application of **loads greater than those considered in the original design** – whether through unauthorized construction, change of use, heavy machinery installation, storage of bulk materials, or seismic/dynamic events.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reference (IS 875: Part 2 – 1987, Reaffirmed 2018):**
> - **Dead Load (DL)**: Permanent static loads (self-weight, partitions, finishes).
> - **Live Load (LL)**: Variable imposed loads (occupancy, furniture, vehicles).
> - **Load Combination (Limit State)**: $1.5 \times (DL + LL)$
> - **Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC)** of soil is typically expressed in **kN/m²** or **kPa**.

---

## 1.4 Significance in KTU 2024 Curriculum

Foundation failures account for a **significant percentage of structural distress** in buildings across Kerala – particularly in **lateritic soils of midland regions**, **clayey soils of Kuttanad**, and **sandy coastal belts**. KTU expects students to understand the **causative mechanisms**, **warning signs**, and **preventive measures** for each failure type – not merely memorize definitions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Failure by Alteration – Operational Mechanism

Alteration failures occur when a building undergoes **modifications after original construction** without re-evaluating the foundation's adequacy. The stepwise mechanism is as follows:

1. **Original design** assumes specific loads ($DL_{original}$, $LL_{original}$) and soil bearing capacity ($q_{safe,original}$).
2. **Alteration** introduces new loads: extra floors ($DL_{new}$), heavier live loads, or removal of load-distributing elements.
3. The **effective stress on soil increases** beyond $q_{safe}$.
4. The soil undergoes **additional consolidation settlement** (in clays) or **elastic compression** (in sands).
5. **Differential settlement** occurs if the load increase is non-uniform.
6. **Cracks, tilting, and structural distress** appear in the superstructure.

### Common Sub-Types of Alteration Failures

- **Vertical Extension Failure**: Adding floors without strengthening the foundation.
  - E.g., A 2-storey building extended to 4 storeys on a footing designed for 2 storeys.
- **Horizontal Extension Failure**: Adding wings/blocks with mismatched foundations causing differential settlement at the junction.
- **Adjacent Excavation Failure**: Neighbour's basement or trench removes lateral confinement of the existing footing.
- **Change-of-Use Failure**: Converting a residential building (LL = 2 kN/m²) to a godown (LL = 10–20 kN/m²) or library (LL = 4–6 kN/m² per IS 875 Part 2).
- **Removal of Load-Bearing Element**: Knocking out a wall without proper redistribution transfers concentrated loads to a single footing.

---

## 2.2 Failure by Improper Maintenance – Operational Mechanism

Improper maintenance is a **slow-onset failure mode** governed by environmental and chemical degradation processes.

1. **Water ingress** through cracks, plumbing leaks, or broken drains saturates the foundation soil.
2. Saturated soil **loses shear strength** and may undergo **swelling (in expansive clays)** or **loss of bearing capacity**.
3. **Chemical attack**: Sulphates in soil/water react with tricalcite aluminate (C₃A) in concrete, causing **expansion and disintegration**.
4. **Vegetation roots** (especially from nearby trees like *Banyan*, *Peepal*, or *Mango*) extract moisture and physically displace the foundation.
5. **Erosion** of bearing strata by uncontrolled water flow.
6. **Corrosion of reinforcement** in RCC foundations reduces flexural strength and causes concrete spalling.
7. Progressive deterioration leads to **cracking, settlement, and eventual collapse**.

### Common Maintenance-Related Failure Triggers

| Trigger | Mechanism | Warning Sign |
|---|---|---|
| Leaking drain/sewer | Soil saturation, softening | Damp patches on plinth |
| Tree root intrusion | Soil displacement, drying cracks | Cracks near plinth |
| Sulphate attack | Concrete disintegration | Surface flaking, white deposits |
| Steel corrosion | Volume expansion (up to 6×) | Rust stains, spalling |
| Poor surface drainage | Ponding, soil wash-out | Water stagnation around building |

---

## 2.3 Failure by Overloading – Operational Mechanism

Overloading is the **direct exceedance of the design load**. The operational mechanism is governed by **Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory** (1943) and the **concept of safe bearing pressure**.

1. The foundation is designed such that:
   $$q_{applied} \le q_{safe} = \dfrac{q_{ult}}{F}$$
   where $F$ = Factor of Safety (typically **3.0** for static loads, per IS 6403:1981).
2. When $q_{applied} > q_{safe}$, the soil **shear strength is exceeded**.
3. Failure occurs as **general shear**, **local shear**, or **punching shear**, depending on soil density and depth ratio (D/B).
4. The result is **sudden settlement, tilting, or catastrophic collapse**.

### Overloading Sources (Real-World Scenarios in Kerala)

- **Stacked goods** in commercial godowns exceeding floor load capacity.
- **Water tanks** on rooftops filled beyond design capacity (1 m³ of water = 9.81 kN).
- **Heavy machinery** installed on factory floors (e.g., lathe, generator, press).
- **Vehicle movement** in basements/parking decks not designed for vehicular loads.
- **Earthquake/dynamic loading** beyond seismic zone design assumptions.
- **Flood-induced hydrostatic pressure** on basement walls and foundations.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board-Exam-Critical. Memorize the table.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_{ult} = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$ | Terzaghi's ultimate bearing capacity (general shear) | kN/m² | Strip footing in c-φ soil |
| 2 | $q_{net,ult} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f$ | Net ultimate bearing capacity | kN/m² | After subtracting overburden |
| 3 | $q_{safe} = \dfrac{q_{net,ult}}{F} + \gamma D_f$ | Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC) | kN/m² | Design check for all footings |
| 4 | $q = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Applied pressure under footing | kN/m² | Direct stress check |
| 5 | $P = 1.5(DL + LL)$ | Factored load (Limit State, IS 456:2000) | kN | Load combination for design |
| 6 | $S = \dfrac{C_c \cdot H}{1+e_0} \log\left(\dfrac{\sigma_0' + \Delta\sigma}{\sigma_0'}\right)$ | Consolidation settlement (clay) | m | Soft clay layers |
| 7 | $\Delta\sigma = q_0 \cdot I$ | Stress increase at depth (Boussinesq) | kN/m² | Settlement calculation |
| 8 | $S_{allowable} \le 25$ mm (clay), $40$ mm (sand) | Permissible total settlement (IS 1904) | mm | Serviceability limit |
| 9 | $\Delta/L \le 1/500$ to $1/300$ | Angular distortion limit (IS 1904) | – | Differential settlement |
| 10 | $e_{max} = B/6$ | Kern limit for no tension | m | Footing eccentricity check |

> **Legend:** $c$ = cohesion, $\varphi$ = angle of internal friction, $B$ = footing width, $D_f$ = depth of foundation, $\gamma$ = unit weight of soil, $q$ = surcharge, $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors (function of $\varphi$), $F$ = Factor of Safety, $C_c$ = Compression index, $H$ = thickness of clay layer, $e_0$ = initial void ratio, $\sigma_0'$ = initial effective stress, $\Delta\sigma$ = stress increment, $I$ = Influence factor, $P$ = total load, $A$ = footing area, $L$ = span, $e$ = eccentricity.

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE**: In the table above, absolute value notation is intentionally **not used** to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. All quantities are scalar positive values in their respective units.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **In alteration assessment**: Engineers re-compute $q_{applied}$ and compare with original $q_{safe}$. If exceeded, **underpinning** (strengthening of existing foundation) is prescribed.
- **In maintenance engineering**: Settlement ($S$) is monitored periodically using **precision leveling**. If $S$ approaches $S_{allowable}$, remedial drainage or **grouting** is done.
- **In overloading control**: Load cells, weighing platforms, and **structural health monitoring (SHM)** sensors are installed in industrial buildings to track $q_{applied}$ in real time.
- **In forensic engineering** (post-failure investigation): The failure load $P_f$ is back-calculated, the actual $q_{applied}$ is determined, and responsibility is assigned.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations – Building the Equations from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Applied Pressure Equation $q = P/A$

This is the most fundamental relation in foundation design. A footing of plan dimensions $B \times L$ transmits the total column load $P$ (including self-weight of footing and soil above it) to the soil below.

**Step 1 – Define the total load arriving at the foundation level:**
$$P = P_{column} + W_{footing} + W_{soil,above}$$

**Step 2 – Define the contact area of the footing:**
$$A = B \times L$$

**Step 3 – By Newton's Third Law and static equilibrium, the soil exerts an equal and opposite pressure (bearing pressure) on the footing base:**
$$q_{applied} = \dfrac{P}{A}$$

**Step 4 – For a square footing ($B = L$):**
$$q_{applied} = \dfrac{P}{B^2}$$

**Step 5 – Units check:** If $P$ is in **kN** and $A$ is in **m²**, then $q$ is in **kN/m²** (or **kPa**). ✓

> **Why this matters for failure analysis**: When alteration or overloading increases $P$ (say, by adding a floor), $q_{applied}$ rises linearly. If $B$ (footing size) is unchanged, the soil experiences higher stress. The ratio $q_{applied} / q_{safe}$ is the **Margin of Safety** (MoS).

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Terzaghi's Ultimate Bearing Capacity Equation

The classical **Terzaghi (1943)** equation for a **strip footing** under **general shear failure** is derived by assuming:
- Soil is a perfectly plastic Mohr-Coulomb material.
- Footing is rough, shallow, and at depth $D_f$.
- Failure zone consists of three zones: triangular (under footing), radial shear, and passive Rankine wedge.

**Step 1 – Identify the three failure zones:**
- **Zone I** (under footing): Triangular elastic wedge at angle $\varphi$ with horizontal.
- **Zone II**: Radial shear zone (log-spiral).
- **Zone III**: Passive Rankine zone.

**Step 2 – Express the equivalent surcharge at foundation level:**
$$q = \gamma \cdot D_f$$

**Step 3 – Sum the three resistance components against shear failure:**

**(a) Cohesive resistance** (from the term $c$ along the slip surface):
$$q_c = c \cdot N_c$$

**(b) Surcharge resistance** (from the equivalent overburden $q$):
$$q_q = q \cdot N_q = \gamma D_f \cdot N_q$$

**(c) Frictional/weight resistance** (from soil self-weight in the failure zone):
$$q_\gamma = 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma$$

**Step 4 – Combine the three components to get the ultimate bearing capacity:**
$$q_{ult} = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

**Step 5 – The bearing capacity factors are functions of $\varphi$ only:**
$$N_q = \tan^2\left(45 + \dfrac{\varphi}{2}\right) \cdot e^{\pi \tan\varphi}$$
$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\varphi$$
$$N_\gamma = 1.8(N_q - 1)\tan\varphi \quad \text{(Terzaghi's original)}$$

**Step 6 – For a square footing, multiply by shape factors:**
$$q_{ult,square} = 1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.4 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

**Step 7 – For a circular footing:**
$$q_{ult,circular} = 1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.3 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

**Step 8 – Net ultimate bearing capacity (used in design to remove overburden effect):**
$$q_{net,ult} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f$$

**Step 9 – Apply Factor of Safety (F = 3 for general shear, per IS 6403:1981):**
$$q_{safe} = \dfrac{q_{net,ult}}{F} + \gamma D_f$$

**Step 10 – Final check – the applied pressure must not exceed $q_{safe}$:**
$$q_{applied} = \dfrac{P_{service}}{A} \le q_{safe}$$

---

### 3.1.3 Worked Example – Checking a Footing for Alteration Failure

**Given** (a typical KTU problem):
- Original 2-storey residential building, square footing $B = 1.5$ m.
- Soil: $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $c = 10$ kPa, $\varphi = 30°$.
- Foundation depth $D_f = 1.0$ m.
- Original total service load = **225 kN** (DL + LL combined).
- **Alteration proposal**: Convert to a commercial shop; new LL = 5× old LL.
- New total service load = **300 kN** (DL = 150 kN + LL = 150 kN).

**Step 1 – Compute the surcharge:**
$$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.0 = 18 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2 – Read Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors for $\varphi = 30°$:**
$$N_q \approx 18.4, \quad N_c \approx 30.1, \quad N_\gamma \approx 22.4$$

**Step 3 – Compute $q_{ult}$ for square footing:**
$$q_{ult} = 1.3 \times 10 \times 30.1 + 18 \times 18.4 + 0.4 \times 18 \times 1.5 \times 22.4$$
$$q_{ult} = 391.3 + 331.2 + 241.9 = 964.4 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 – Net ultimate bearing capacity:**
$$q_{net,ult} = 964.4 - 18 = 946.4 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5 – Safe Bearing Capacity (F = 3):**
$$q_{safe} = \dfrac{946.4}{3} + 18 = 333.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 6 – Original applied pressure:**
$$q_{applied,old} = \dfrac{225}{1.5 \times 1.5} = 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
Original utilization: $\dfrac{100}{333.5} \times 100\% = 30\%$ ✓ Safe.

**Step 7 – New applied pressure after alteration:**
$$q_{applied,new} = \dfrac{300}{1.5 \times 1.5} = 133.3 \text{ kN/m}^2$$
New utilization: $\dfrac{133.3}{333.5} \times 100\% = 40\%$ ✓ Still safe, but **margin reduced**.

**Step 8 – Footing size required if loads grow further:**
For 500 kN load at 50% utilization:
$$A_{req} = \dfrac{500}{0.5 \times 333.5} = 3.0 \text{ m}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_{req} = \sqrt{3.0} = 1.73 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion**: The existing 1.5 m footing is **marginally safe** for the alteration. **Recommendation**: Either jacketing/underpinning of the footing OR restricting the new live load.

---

### 3.1.4 Settlement Verification (Clausen et al. Method)

To check whether **serviceability** (not just strength) governs the failure:

**Step 1 – Compute the stress increase at the center of the clay layer (Boussinesq, using 2:1 method):**
$$\Delta\sigma = \dfrac{q_0 \cdot B \cdot L}{(B + z)(L + z)}$$

**Step 2 – For square footing and clay layer of thickness $H$ at depth $z$:**
$$\Delta\sigma = \dfrac{q_0 \cdot B^2}{(B + z)^2}$$

**Step 3 – Compute consolidation settlement using the standard equation:**
$$S = \dfrac{C_c \cdot H}{1 + e_0} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sigma_0' + \Delta\sigma}{\sigma_0'}\right)$$

**Step 4 – Compare with $S_{allowable}$:**
- For isolated footings on clay: $S_{allowable} = 25$ mm.
- For isolated footings on sand: $S_{allowable} = 40$ mm.

**Step 5 – If $S > S_{allowable}$, the foundation fails in serviceability** even if bearing capacity is adequate.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Cause-Effect-Failure-Prevention

| Failure Type | Root Cause | Mechanism | Visible Symptom | Prevention |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Alteration** | Renovation/extension/change of use | Stress increase on soil | New cracks after renovation | Structural audit before alteration |
| **Adjacent Excavation** | Neighbour's basement | Loss of lateral confinement | Tilting towards excavation | Sheet piling, shoring, underpinning |
| **Improper Drainage** | Blocked drains, broken pipes | Soil saturation, softening | Damp plinth, efflorescence | Periodic drain cleaning, slope maintenance |
| **Tree Root** | Proximity of large trees | Soil desiccation (clay) | Seasonal cracks (summer) | Root barriers, distance ≥ 1.5 × tree height |
| **Sulphate Attack** | High sulphate soil/water | Concrete disintegration | Surface flaking, white crust | Sulphate-resistant cement (SRC) |
| **Steel Corrosion** | Moisture + chlorides | Volume expansion, spalling | Rust stains, concrete cover loss | Adequate cover (50 mm), good concrete |
| **Overloading** | Heavy machinery, stored goods | Bearing capacity exceedance | Floor sag, column shortening | Load posting, structural health monitoring |
| **Dynamic Load** | Vibrating machinery | Liquefaction (sands), fatigue | Settlement during operation | Isolation pads, dynamic analysis |
| **Flood/Uplift** | Rising water table | Buoyancy, hydrostatic pressure | Basement heave, wall cracks | Dewatering, anchoring, waterproofing |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram – Foundation Failure Causal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Foundation Failure] --> B[Alteration]
    A --> C[Improper Maintenance]
    A --> D[Overloading]
    
    B --> B1[Vertical Extension]
    B --> B2[Horizontal Extension]
    B --> B3[Change of Use]
    B --> B4[Adjacent Excavation]
    
    C --> C1[Water Seepage]
    C --> C2[Tree Root Intrusion]
    C --> C3[Sulphate Attack]
    C --> C4[Steel Corrosion]
    C --> C5[Poor Drainage]
    
    D --> D1[Heavy Machinery]
    D --> D2[Bulk Material Storage]
    D --> D3[Dynamic Loads]
    D --> D4[Hydrostatic Uplift]
    
    B1 --> E[Increased Stress on Soil]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    B4 --> E
    C1 --> F[Soil Strength Loss]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> G[Material Degradation]
    C4 --> G
    C5 --> F
    D1 --> H[Bearing Capacity Exceedance]
    D2 --> H
    D3 --> H
    D4 --> I[Uplift Failure]
    
    E --> J[Settlement]
    F --> J
    H --> J
    J --> K[Cracking, Tilting, Collapse]
    G --> K
    I --> K
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – Load Path and Failure Mechanism (Alteration Case)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OriginalState[ORIGINAL DESIGN STATE]
        L1[DL = 100 kN] --> F1[Footing B = 1.5 m]
        L2[LL = 50 kN] --> F1
        F1 --> S1[q applied = 67 kN/m2]
        S1 --> SBC1[SBC = 200 kN/m2]
        SBC1 --> SAFE1[Margin OK]
    end
    
    subgraph AlteredState[AFTER ALTERATION]
        L3[DL = 200 kN] --> F2[Footing B = 1.5 m UNCHANGED]
        L4[LL = 150 kN] --> F2
        F2 --> S2[q applied = 156 kN/m2]
        S2 --> SBC2[SBC = 200 kN/m2]
        SBC2 --> CHECK{Margin Adequate?}
    end
    
    CHECK -->|No| FAIL[Foundation Failure]
    CHECK -->|Yes| OK[Safe]
    
    FAIL --> REM[Remedial Underpinning Required]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture – Improper Maintenance Failure Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plumbing Leak] --> S1[Soil Saturation]
    P2[Broken Drain] --> S1
    P3[Cracked Plinth] --> S2[Water Ingression]
    
    S1 --> SOFT[Soil Softening]
    S2 --> SOFT
    SOFT --> LOSS[Loss of Bearing Capacity]
    LOSS --> SETTLE[Uneven Settlement]
    SETTLE --> CRACK[Wall Cracks]
    
    P4[Tree Roots] --> DRY[Soil Drying]
    DRY --> SHRINK[Shrinkage Cracks in Clay]
    SHRINK --> HEAVE[Foundation Heave or Settlement]
    
    P5[Chemical Attack] --> CONC[Concrete Disintegration]
    CONC --> EXP[Steel Exposure]
    EXP --> COR[Corrosion]
    COR --> SPALL[Spalling of Cover Concrete]
```

## 4.4 Schematic – Overloading Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DSGN[Original Design Load P design] --> F[Footing Area A]
    F --> QSAFE[Safe Bearing Pressure q safe]
    DSGN --> QAPP[Applied Pressure q applied = P/A]
    
    OVR[Overloading Event] --> NEW[New Load P new > P design]
    NEW --> QNEWP[New Applied Pressure q new = P new / A]
    
    QNEWP --> CMP{Is q new ≤ q safe?}
    CMP -->|YES| SAFE2[Margin Reduced but Safe]
    CMP -->|NO| FAIL2[Bearing Capacity Exceeded]
    
    FAIL2 --> SHF[General / Local / Punching Shear]
    SHF --> COL[Sudden Settlement or Collapse]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram – Preventive Maintenance Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INSPECT[Periodic Inspection] --> DOC[Document Condition]
    DOC --> ANALYZE[Compare with Allowable Limits]
    ANALYZE --> DECIDE{Defects Found?}
    DECIDE -->|NO| MON[Continue Monitoring]
    DECIDE -->|YES| REMEDY[Plan Remedial Action]
    REMEDY --> EXEC[Execute Repair]
    EXEC --> VERIFY[Post-Repair Verification]
    VERIFY --> MON
    MON --> INSPECT
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define "Failure by Alteration" in foundation engineering. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Failure by alteration refers to the loss of foundation adequacy caused by post-construction modifications that change the original load path, magnitude, or distribution on the foundation system, without corresponding re-design of the foundation.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Vertical extension** – Adding one or more floors to an existing building without strengthening the footings.
2. **Change of building use** – Converting a residential structure (Live Load ≈ 2 kN/m²) into a commercial showroom or library (LL ≈ 4–6 kN/m² per IS 875 Part 2), increasing the imposed load beyond the design assumption.

*Self-explanatory diagram or example cited from local context earns full 3 marks.*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**List any four causes of foundation failure due to improper maintenance.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Unattended plumbing/drainage leaks** causing soil saturation and loss of shear strength.
2. **Tree root intrusion** from nearby large trees, causing soil shrinkage in clays and physical displacement of foundation.
3. **Sulphate attack** from aggressive soil/water, leading to disintegration of concrete.
4. **Corrosion of reinforcement** due to ingress of moisture and chlorides through cracked cover concrete, causing spalling and loss of section.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Always **cite the relevant IS code clause** (e.g., IS 1904, IS 875 Part 2, IS 6403) wherever applicable – 1 mark is reserved for "Code Awareness".
> - **Draw a clear labeled diagram** (footing cross-section, failure pattern, or load diagram). 2 marks are reserved for **diagrams**.
> - **State assumptions** explicitly in derivations (e.g., "Assume general shear failure", "Use Terzaghi's equation for strip footing").
> - Students commonly lose marks by: (a) omitting the Factor of Safety step, (b) confusing net vs. gross SBC, (c) not converting units (kN/m² to MPa correctly), (d) forgetting to subtract overburden in net bearing capacity.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various ways in which **"Failure by Alteration"** of a building leads to foundation distress. Mention at least four sub-types with sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-storey residential building is proposed to be converted to a 3-storey commercial building. The original design had a square footing of size **2.0 m × 2.0 m** at a depth of **1.2 m** in soil with $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, $c = 15$ kPa, $\varphi = 25°$. The original service load (DL + LL) was **400 kN**. Estimate the new service load if the LL is increased by 60% and DL increases by 30% due to the new floor. Check whether the existing footing is safe. Use Terzaghi's method with $F = 3$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

Failure by alteration occurs when a building is **modified after original construction** in a way that disturbs the assumptions made during foundation design. The four major sub-types are:

1. **Vertical Extension (Adding Floors):**
   - New dead loads from additional slabs, walls, and finishes.
   - Increases stress ($q_{applied} = P/A$) on the existing soil.
   - Differential settlement possible if extension is partial.
   - *Sketch*: Cross-section of original 2-storey + proposed 3rd floor with arrow showing increased column load.

2. **Horizontal Extension (Adding Wings/Blocks):**
   - New footings of different sizes cause **differential settlement** at the junction.
   - Torsional and bending stresses develop at the interface.
   - *Sketch*: Plan view of L-shaped or T-shaped extension.

3. **Change of Building Use:**
   - Live load per IS 875 Part 2 increases substantially.
   - Examples: Residence (2 kN/m²) → Library (4–6 kN/m²) → Godown (10–20 kN/m²).
   - *Sketch*: Table comparing LL for different occupancies.

4. **Adjacent Excavation (Neighbour's Basement/Trench):**
   - Removes **lateral confinement** of the existing soil.
   - Reduces the passive resistance $K_p$ component of bearing capacity.
   - Foundation may tilt towards the excavation.
   - *Sketch*: Cross-section showing existing footing + adjacent deep excavation + slip surface.

**Preventive Measures:**
- Conduct **structural audit** before any alteration.
- Engage a **licensed structural engineer** for re-design.
- Use **underpinning** (mass concrete, jet grouting, micro-piles) if needed.

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Footing size: $B = L = 2.0$ m, $D_f = 1.2$ m
- Soil: $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, $c = 15$ kPa, $\varphi = 25°$
- Original service load: $P_{old} = 400$ kN
- $F = 3$ (Factor of Safety)

**Step 1 – Compute the surcharge at foundation level:**
$$q = \gamma D_f = 17 \times 1.2 = 20.4 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2 – Read Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors for $\varphi = 25°$:**

$$N_q = \tan^2\left(45 + \dfrac{25}{2}\right) \cdot e^{\pi \tan 25°} \approx 12.7$$
$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot 25° = 11.7 \times 2.145 \approx 25.1$$
$$N_\gamma = 1.8(N_q - 1)\tan 25° = 1.8 \times 11.7 \times 0.466 \approx 9.8$$

**Step 3 – Compute $q_{ult}$ for square footing:**
$$q_{ult} = 1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.4 \gamma B N_\gamma$$
$$q_{ult} = 1.3 \times 15 \times 25.1 + 20.4 \times 12.7 + 0.4 \times 17 \times 2.0 \times 9.8$$
$$q_{ult} = 489.5 + 259.1 + 133.3 = 881.9 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 – Net ultimate bearing capacity:**
$$q_{net,ult} = 881.9 - 20.4 = 861.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5 – Safe Bearing Capacity:**
$$q_{safe} = \dfrac{861.5}{3} + 20.4 = 287.1 + 20.4 = 307.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 6 – Footing area:**
$$A = 2.0 \times 2.0 = 4.0 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 7 – Maximum safe load on the footing:**
$$P_{max} = q_{safe} \times A = 307.5 \times 4.0 = 1230 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 8 – New service load after alteration:**
Assume original $DL = 250$ kN, $LL = 150$ kN (typical residential ratio).
- New DL: $250 \times 1.30 = 325$ kN
- New LL: $150 \times 1.60 = 240$ kN
- Total new service load: $P_{new} = 325 + 240 = 565$ kN

**Step 9 – New applied pressure:**
$$q_{applied,new} = \dfrac{565}{4.0} = 141.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 10 – Safety check:**
$$q_{applied,new} = 141.25 \text{ kN/m}^2 \ll q_{safe} = 307.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Conclusion:** The footing is **SAFE** with adequate margin (utilization ≈ 46%). The alteration is permissible without underpinning.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model] – Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Failure by Overloading** and **Failure by Improper Maintenance**. List five symptoms each and the corresponding preventive measures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory floor is designed for an imposed load of **10 kN/m²**, but the owner has stacked machinery and raw materials imposing an actual load of **25 kN/m²** on an area of **3 m × 4 m**. The soil has $q_{safe} = 150$ kN/m², $D_f = 1.0$ m, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. Verify whether the foundation is overstressed. If yes, suggest a suitable remedial measure. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

| Aspect | Failure by Overloading | Failure by Improper Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Sudden or progressive exceedance of design load | Slow-onset material/soil degradation |
| **Time Scale** | Short (immediate to few years) | Long (years to decades) |
| **Cause** | Operational, unauthorized, accidental | Neglect, environmental exposure |
| **Detection** | Cracks, tilting, structural distress | Dampness, efflorescence, rust |
| **Remediation** | Load reduction, strengthening, underpinning | Drainage repair, grouting, repair of cover concrete |

**Five Symptoms of Overloading:**
1. Cracks in beams and slabs (flexural failure).
2. Column shortening and buckling.
3. Excessive deflection of floors.
4. Bearing failure – soil heave adjacent to footing.
5. Tilting of the structure.

**Five Symptoms of Improper Maintenance:**
1. Damp patches on plinth and walls.
2. Efflorescence (white salt deposits).
3. Rust stains on RCC members.
4. Cracks appearing in dry season (shrinkage).
5. Vegetation growth on walls.

**Preventive Measures:**
- **Overloading**: Load posting, structural audit, real-time load monitoring.
- **Maintenance**: Periodic inspection, drainage cleaning, waterproofing, vegetation control.

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Designed LL = 10 kN/m²; Actual LL = 25 kN/m²
- Loaded area = 3 m × 4 m = 12 m²
- $q_{safe} = 150$ kN/m², $D_f = 1.0$ m, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³
- Assume a square pad footing beneath the loaded area.

**Step 1 – Total imposed load (actual):**
$$P = 25 \times 12 = 300 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 – Assume footing area = 12 m² (matching loaded zone):**
$$A = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ m}^2$$
$$q_{applied} = \dfrac{300}{12} = 25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 – Compare with $q_{safe}$:**
$$q_{applied} = 25 \text{ kN/m}^2 \ll q_{safe} = 150 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Wait** – the foundation is **NOT** overstressed in bearing! The overloading concern is at the **floor slab level**, not the soil.

**Step 4 – Floor slab check (one-way / two-way):**
For a 10 cm thick RCC slab ($M_{25}$ concrete, $F_e 415$ steel), safe imposed load ≈ 10–15 kN/m² for short spans. The 25 kN/m² load is **excessive for the slab** but the **soil is still safe**.

**Step 5 – Conclusion:**
The foundation soil is **NOT overstressed**, but the **floor slab may fail in flexure or shear**. The remedial measure is:
- Install **additional support beams** or **transfer girders** below the loaded zone.
- Alternatively, **distribute the load** over a larger area using a steel platform base plate.
- If slab failure is critical, **post-tensioning** or **FRP strengthening** of the slab may be required.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Foundation Failure (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Three failure types** in KTU Module 4: **Alteration, Improper Maintenance, Overloading**.
- ✅ **Terzaghi's SBC equation** is the foundation (literally) of all bearing capacity checks – memorize $q_{ult} = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$.
- ✅ **Net vs. Gross SBC**: Always subtract overburden ($\gamma D_f$) for net calculations.
- ✅ **Factor of Safety (F = 3)** is mandatory for static general shear failure (IS 6403:1981).
- ✅ **Permissible settlement** (IS 1904): 25 mm for clay, 40 mm for sand under isolated footings.
- ✅ **Angular distortion** limit: 1/500 to 1/300 (avoid cracking of partitions).
- ✅ **Shape factors** for square (1.3, 1.0, 0.4) and circular (1.3, 1.0, 0.3) footings.
- ✅ **Alteration** = post-construction change in load/path; check $q_{new} \le q_{safe}$.
- ✅ **Improper maintenance** = gradual degradation; preventive maintenance is cheaper than repair.
- ✅ **Overloading** = $P_{actual} > P_{design}$; bearing capacity is the first check, but **slab/beam** checks come first in many cases.
- ✅ **IS codes to remember**:
   - **IS 1904:1986** – General foundation design.
   - **IS 875: Part 2 (1987)** – Imposed (Live) loads.
   - **IS 6403:1981** – Bearing capacity calculation.
   - **IS 456:2000** – RCC design.
   - **IS 2974:1982** – Foundation vibrations/machine foundations.
- ✅ **Common KTU 14-mark question pattern**: Derive/apply SBC equation + check alteration impact + comment on safety.
- ✅ **Common KTU 3-mark question pattern**: Define a failure type + cite 2 examples.
- ✅ **Always state units** in your final answer (kN/m² or kPa, never N/m² unless asked).
- ✅ **Diagrams** are compulsory for 14-mark answers – practice neat, labeled cross-sections.

> **Final Note from KTU Examiner's Desk:** Foundation engineering is a **conceptual subject disguised as a numerical one**. Examiners award 2–3 marks per 14-mark question purely for **conceptual clarity, code awareness, and diagram quality**. Do not just plug numbers – **explain the logic, cite the code, and draw a sketch**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET611 - INTRODUCTION TO CONSTRUCTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Retrofitting of Structural Components - Beams, Columns and Slabs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — RETROFITTING OF STRUCTURAL COMPONENTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Retrofitting** is the process of modifying and upgrading an existing structure to make it more resistant to seismic activity, increased loads, or to remedy design/construction deficiencies. It involves a calculated alteration of the structural system (rather than the non-structural components) to improve its performance during future events.

**KTU 2024 Scheme Standardized Terminology:**

- **Repair:** Restoring a damaged structure to its original design strength and serviceability without altering its capacity.
- **Rehabilitation:** Restoring a structure to a desirable level of performance, often involving some enhancement over the original design.
- **Retrofitting / Strengthening:** Substantially upgrading the strength, stiffness, and ductility of a structure to meet contemporary code requirements, including seismic provisions.
- **Reconstruction:** Demolition and complete rebuilding, which is *not* a form of retrofitting.

The governing Indian standard for seismic evaluation and retrofitting of existing buildings is **IS 15988:2013** ("Seismic Evaluation and Strengthening of Existing Reinforced Concrete Buildings — Guidelines").

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Suit Alteration" Metaphor**
>
> Imagine you bought a suit (your building) ten years ago that was built for a young man weighing **70 kg**. Today, that man has gained weight (increased occupancy load), or the city's earthquake zone has been reclassified (increased seismic demand), or the fabric has started tearing at the seams (concrete spalling, rebar corrosion). You have three choices:
> 1. Throw it away and buy a new one (Reconstruction).
> 2. Stitch up the torn seams (Repair).
> 3. Add extra panels at the back, reinforce the buttons, and insert shoulder pads so the suit now fits a **100 kg** man and even survives a strong gust of wind (Retrofitting).
>
> Retrofitting is the **engineering equivalent of tailoring the existing suit to new performance demands**, *not* replacing it.

### 1.3 Why Retrofit? — The Engineering Drivers

| Driver | Typical Cause | Engineering Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Seismic Deficiency** | Old design codes (pre-IS 1893:2002) ignored ductility | Building located in Zone III/IV/V |
| **Increased Loading** | Change of occupancy (residential → storage/machinery) | Live load exceeding original **4 kN/m²** design |
| **Material Degradation** | Carbonation, chloride ingress, rebar corrosion | Concrete spalling visible; cover loss > 25 mm |
| **Design Errors** | Insufficient reinforcement, soft-story formation | Column weaker than beam (weak column-strong beam) |
| **Code Revisions** | IS 456, IS 1893, IS 13920 upgrades | Compliance audit failure |
| **Accidental Damage** | Fire, impact, blast | Loss of cross-section capacity > 15% |

### 1.4 Performance Levels for Retrofitting (Per IS 15988:2013)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three target performance levels are recognized:
>
> - **PL-1: Immediate Occupancy (IO)** — Damage is limited; structure remains functional. Target: **1.0 × DBE** (Design Basis Earthquake).
> - **PL-2: Life Safety (LS)** — Significant damage but no collapse. Target: **1.0 × DBE** with inelastic excursions.
> - **PL-3: Collapse Prevention (CP)** — Severe damage, structure on the verge of collapse. Target: **1.5 × DBE** (Maximum Considered Earthquake, MCE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Retrofitting Techniques

Retrofitting techniques are classified by their **intervention depth**, from surface-level to system-level:

```
Level 1: Component Level  →  Jacketing, FRP wrapping, section enlargement
Level 2: Element Level    →  Adding shear walls, infill walls, braces
Level 3: System Level     →  Base isolation, mass reduction, TMDs
```

For the KTU syllabus (Module 4), the focus is **Level 1 (Component-level retrofitting)** of beams, columns, and slabs.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of a Typical Retrofit Design

The five engineering "WH" steps (highly tested in KTU 14-mark questions):

1. **Why retrofit?** — Damage assessment, load increase, seismic reclassification.
2. **What is deficient?** — Flexure? Shear? Axial? Confinement? Bond/anchorage?
3. **What technique to use?** — Choice matrix: cost, disruption, gain in capacity.
4. **How to design?** — Capacity-based design using **IS 15988**, IS 456, IS 13920.
5. **How to execute?** — Surface preparation, curing, quality control via **NDT** (Non-Destructive Testing) like rebound hammer, UPV (Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **most frequently tested formulas** in KTU ESE for this topic. Memorize the variable definitions and standard values.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Standard Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_u = V_c + V_s$ | Total design shear of retrofitted beam | $V_c$ = concrete contribution; $V_s$ = steel/FRP contribution |
| 2 | $V_c = \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d$ | Concrete shear capacity (IS 456 Cl. 40) | $\tau_c$ from Table 19 of IS 456; $b$ = width; $d$ = effective depth |
| 3 | $V_s = \dfrac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d}{s_v}$ | Vertical stirrup shear resistance | $f_y$ = **415 N/mm²** (Fe415); $s_v$ = stirrup spacing |
| 4 | $P_u = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}$ | Axial capacity of jacketed column (IS 15988 Cl. 9) | $A_c$ = gross area; $A_{sc}$ = longitudinal steel |
| 5 | $M_u = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st} \cdot d \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st} \cdot f_y}{b \cdot d \cdot f_{ck}}\right)$ | Flexural capacity of retrofitted beam (IS 456) | $A_{st}$ = total tension steel after retrofit |
| 6 | $f_{cc}' = f_c' \left(1 + 2.05 \cdot \dfrac{t_f}{b}\right)$ | Confined concrete strength (Lam & Teng, FRP model) | $t_f$ = FRP thickness; $b$ = column width |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{cu,c} = \varepsilon_{cu}\left(1.3 + \dfrac{1.5}{n \cdot t_f / b}\right)$ | Ultimate strain in confined concrete (FRP wrapping) | $n$ = number of FRP layers |
| 8 | $L_d = \dfrac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot \phi}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$ | Development length of rebar (IS 456 Cl. 26.2) | $\phi$ = bar diameter; $\tau_{bd}$ = bond stress (Table 26) |
| 9 | $w_{min} = 0.20\%$ of $bd$ | Minimum shear reinforcement (IS 456) | Prevents brittle shear failure |
| 10 | $\Delta_{allow} = \dfrac{L}{250}$ | Allowable deflection (IS 456) | $L$ = span; for retrofit to restore serviceability |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Context:**
> - **Post-2001 Bhuj Earthquake (Gujarat):** IS 15988 (originally published as IITK-GSDMA guidelines) became the national benchmark — thousands of public schools, hospitals, and government buildings in **Kerala's Zone III** districts (Thiruvananthapuram, Kollam, Pathanamthitta, Kottayam, Idukki, Ernakulam) are now being audited under this code.
> - **Kerala's 2018 & 2019 Floods:** Many buildings suffered foundation settlement, requiring column jacketing and slab overlay retrofitting before re-occupancy.
> - **Heritage Structures:** Fort Kochi, Mattancherry, and traditional *Nalukettu* homes in Thrissur require FRP-based, low-invasive retrofitting to preserve heritage fabric while upgrading seismic capacity.

### 2.5 Decision Matrix — Choosing a Retrofit Technique

| Criterion | RC Jacketing | Steel Jacketing | FRP Wrapping | External Prestressing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Strength Gain** | 50–100% | 30–60% | 20–40% | 50–150% |
| **Section Enlargement** | Yes (40–100 mm) | No | No (negligible) | No |
| **Site Disruption** | High | Medium | Low | High |
| **Skilled Labour** | Moderate | High | Moderate | Very High |
| **Cost (per m²)** | ₹3,000–6,000 | ₹4,000–8,000 | ₹5,000–10,000 | ₹8,000–15,000 |
| **Best Use** | Severely damaged columns | Steel columns | Seismic confinement | Long-span beams |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Axial Capacity of an RC Jacketed Column (IS 15988:2013)

This is the **most-tested 14-mark derivation** in KTU ESE for this module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A square RC column of original size **300 × 300 mm** is to be retrofitted by RC jacketing to increase its size to **450 × 450 mm**. Original longitudinal steel is 4 nos. **16 mm dia.** Fe415. Additional jacket steel = 4 nos. **20 mm dia.** Fe415. Concrete grade = M25 (original) and M30 (jacket). Determine the enhanced axial capacity.

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{c,\text{orig}} &= 300 \times 300 = 90{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{c,\text{jacket}} &= 450 \times 450 - 300 \times 300 = 112{,}500 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{c,\text{total}} &= 450 \times 450 = 202{,}500 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{sc,\text{orig}} &= 4 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 4 \times 201.06 = 804.25 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{sc,\text{add}} &= 4 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 4 \times 314.16 = 1256.64 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{sc,\text{total}} &= 804.25 + 1256.64 = 2060.89 \text{ mm}^2 \\
f_{ck,\text{jacket}} &= 30 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad ; \quad f_y = 415 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Write the IS 15988 axial capacity equation:**

$$
P_u = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}
$$

This equation treats the column as a short, axially loaded member (eccentricity considered via additional moment magnification if required).

**Step 2 — Substitute the jacket concrete properties (using $f_{ck} = 30$ for the new jacket shell):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{u,\text{concrete}} &= 0.4 \times 30 \times 202{,}500 \\
&= 2{,}430{,}000 \text{ N} \\
&= 2430 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the longitudinal steel contribution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{u,\text{steel}} &= 0.67 \times 415 \times 2060.89 \\
&= 572{,}825.5 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 572.83 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum both contributions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{u,\text{total}} &= 2430 + 572.83 \\
&= 3002.83 \text{ kN} \\
&\approx 3003 \text{ kN} \quad \text{or} \quad 3.00 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the original column capacity (sanity check):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{u,\text{original}} &= 0.4 \times 25 \times 90{,}000 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 804.25 \\
&= 900{,}000 + 223{,}575 = 1{,}123{,}575 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 1123.6 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Capacity enhancement factor} = \dfrac{3002.83}{1123.58} = 2.67}
$$

The jacketing yields a **2.67-fold increase** in axial load capacity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Shear Capacity of a Beam Retrofitted with FRP (ACI 440.2R & IS 15988 Analog)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A simply supported beam of width **b = 250 mm** and effective depth **d = 450 mm** is wrapped with a single layer of **CFRP (Carbon FRP)** of thickness **t_f = 1.0 mm** at a 90° orientation, spaced at 100 mm c/c over the shear span. Compute the FRP shear contribution $V_f$.

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= 1.0 \text{ mm} \\
w_f &= 100 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(width of FRP strip)} \\
s_f &= 100 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(spacing center-to-center)} \\
E_f &= 165{,}000 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{(typical for CFRP)} \\
\varepsilon_{fe} &= 0.004 \quad \text{(effective strain, design)} \\
d_{fv} &= 450 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the FRP shear contribution (per ACI 440.2R, Eq. 10-3):**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{A_{fv} \cdot f_{fe} \cdot d_{fv}}{s_f}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FRP area $A_{fv}$ (2 plies per strip, one on each side of web):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{fv} &= 2 \cdot t_f \cdot w_f \\
&= 2 \times 1.0 \times 100 \\
&= 200 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective stress $f_{fe}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{fe} &= E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{fe} \\
&= 165{,}000 \times 0.004 \\
&= 660 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into $V_f$ formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \dfrac{200 \times 660 \times 450}{100} \\
&= 5{,}94{,}000 \text{ N} \\
&= 594 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Add to concrete shear capacity (assuming M25, $\tau_c = 0.62$ N/mm² from IS 456 Table 19 for $p_t = 1.0\%$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d \\
&= 0.62 \times 250 \times 450 \\
&= 69{,}750 \text{ N} \\
&= 69.75 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Total shear capacity after retrofit:**

$$
\boxed{V_{u,\text{total}} = V_c + V_f = 69.75 + 594 = 663.75 \text{ kN}}
$$

This represents an **~8.5× increase** in shear capacity, demonstrating why FRP wrapping is the most effective shear-strengthening technique per unit added weight.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Slab Retrofit Calculations

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A two-way slab of effective depth **125 mm** spanning **4.0 m × 4.0 m**, originally designed for **3.0 kN/m²** live load, must be upgraded for **5.0 kN/m²**. The retrofit option is a **40 mm thick concrete overlay** (M30) with a welded wire mesh of **6 mm dia @ 100 mm c/c** in both directions. Calculate the upgraded moment capacity.

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{total}} &= 125 + 40 = 165 \text{ mm} \\
A_{st,\text{add}} &= \dfrac{1000}{100} \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 6^2 = 10 \times 28.27 = 282.7 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m} \\
f_{ck} &= 30 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
f_y &= 415 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{(mild steel is Fe415, since welded mesh is cold-worked, but KTU assumes 415)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Depth of neutral axis (using $b = 1000$ mm strip):**

$$
x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st}}{0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b}
$$

$$
x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \times 415 \times 282.7}{0.36 \times 30 \times 1000} = \dfrac{102{,}143.5}{10{,}800} = 9.46 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Lever arm:**

$$
z = d - 0.42 \cdot x_u = 165 - 0.42 \times 9.46 = 161.03 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Moment capacity:**

$$
\boxed{M_u = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st} \cdot z = 0.87 \times 415 \times 282.7 \times 161.03 = 16{,}470{,}707 \text{ N·mm/m} \approx 16.47 \text{ kN·m/m}}
$$

This is sufficient to resist the new design moment from a **5.0 kN/m²** live load on a 4 m two-way slab.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - April 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between repair, rehabilitation, and retrofitting of a structural component.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Repair | Rehabilitation | Retrofitting |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Restore to original | Restore to good condition | Upgrade beyond original |
| **Capacity Change** | None | Marginal increase | Significant increase |
| **Typical Use** | Localized damage | Widespread damage | Code revision, seismic upgrade |
| **Example** | Patching of spalled concrete | Epoxy injection of cracks | Column jacketing, FRP wrap |
| **Code Reference** | IS 456 | IS 457 | IS 15988, IS 13920 |

> **3-Mark Key Points:** (i) Repair = no capacity enhancement; (ii) Rehabilitation = minor enhancement; (iii) Retrofitting = major capacity/demand upgrade to meet new design code requirements.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - December 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. List any three techniques for retrofitting of RC columns and state the purpose of confinement in column retrofitting.**

**Model Answer:**

**Three Techniques for Column Retrofitting:**
1. **Reinforced Concrete (RC) Jacketing** — Increases cross-section and adds longitudinal steel.
2. **Steel Jacketing / Caging** — Uses steel angles and plates to provide lateral confinement.
3. **Fibre-Reinforced Polymer (FRP) Wrapping** — Carbon or Glass FRP sheets provide passive confinement.

**Purpose of Confinement:**
- **Enhances axial load capacity** by increasing the effective compressive strength of concrete.
- **Improves ductility** by allowing the column to undergo large plastic deformations without sudden brittle failure.
- **Prevents buckling of longitudinal bars** under seismic compression-tension cycles.
- **Increases shear capacity** by providing additional lateral resistance to diagonal tension.

> **3-Mark Key Points:** (i) Three techniques named; (ii) Confinement = lateral pressure that increases $f_{ck}$ to $f_{cc}'$; (iii) Ductility enhancement is the primary seismic benefit.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for 14-Mark Questions:**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** the design code — use **IS 15988:2013** for retrofitting and **IS 456:2000 / IS 13920:2016** for original design.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.67 factor** for steel in axial capacity — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Not mentioning surface preparation** before jacketing — examiners look for "roughening, cleaning, dowels" as a quality-control step. Loss of 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $f_{ck}$ of original and jacket concrete** — always clarify that the jacket uses higher-grade concrete (M30–M40) to ensure composite action.
> 5. **Skipping the development length** of new rebar — L_d check is mandatory per IS 456 Cl. 26.2.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Beam & Column Retrofitting

> **[KTU University Exam - April 2024, Model Question]**

**(a) Explain in detail the various techniques used for retrofitting of RC beams. Discuss the design philosophy behind flexural and shear strengthening with suitable sketches. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**RC Beam Retrofitting Techniques (Classification):**

1. **Flexural Strengthening Techniques:**
   - **RC Jacketing** — Adding a layer of concrete (40–75 mm) with additional tension steel on the soffit and side faces.
   - **External Steel Plate Bonding** — Bolting/epoxy-bonding steel plates to the tension face.
   - **FRP Plate Bonding** — CFRP/GFRP plates bonded with epoxy to the soffit (very popular due to low weight).
   - **External Post-Tensioning** — Installing high-tensile strands outside the beam profile and anchoring at the ends.

2. **Shear Strengthening Techniques:**
   - **FRP U-Wraps or Full Wraps** — Carbon/Glass FRP sheets bonded to the sides of beams in U or full wrap configuration.
   - **Additional Stirrups** — Cross-hole drilling and grouting of new stirrups through the slab.
   - **Side-bonded FRP Strips** — CFRP strips at 45° to resist diagonal tension.

3. **Confinement/Seismic Techniques:**
   - **FRP Confinement in Plastic Hinge Region** — Wrapping the beam ends (length = 2d) to improve ductility.
   - **Steel Cage Jacketing** — Plates and angles to encase the beam-column joint.

**Design Philosophy:**

The retrofit design follows **capacity-based principles**:
- **Flexural retrofit:** Increase $A_{st}$ to raise $M_u$ (moment capacity) above the demand $M_{\text{demand}}$ from the new load combination. Ensure $A_{st,\text{new}} \leq A_{st,\text{max}}$ to prevent over-reinforcement (ductile failure mode).
- **Shear retrofit:** Provide additional $V_s$ such that $V_c + V_s > V_u$, with shear strength greater than the flexural overstrength ($V_u \geq 1.4 M_u / L$) to enforce a **strong-column weak-beam** mechanism.

**Suitable Sketch (Textual):**

```
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │   Slab / Floor      │ ← top
   ├═════════════════════┤  ← Original beam top steel (intact)
   │   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │  ← Original web
   │   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │
   │   ░░░╔══════╗░░░░░  │  ← New CFRP plate bonded to soffit (40–100 mm wide, 1.2–2.0 mm thick)
   └─────╚══════╝────────┘
       (Beam bottom)
```

**Marks Distribution:** Techniques classification (2), Flexural methods (2), Shear methods (2), Design philosophy (1).

---

**(b) An RC column of size 400 × 400 mm with 8 numbers of 20 mm diameter longitudinal bars and 8 mm ties @ 200 mm c/c is to be retrofitted by RC jacketing to increase its size to 550 × 550 mm. The new jacket uses 4 additional 16 mm bars at the corners and 8 mm ties @ 100 mm c/c. Original concrete is M25 and jacket concrete is M35, with Fe415 steel. Compute the enhanced axial load capacity. (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{g,\text{orig}} &= 400 \times 400 = 1{,}60{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{g,\text{jacket,total}} &= 550 \times 550 = 3{,}02{,}500 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{c,\text{total}} &= 3{,}02{,}500 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{sc,\text{orig}} &= 8 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 8 \times 314.16 = 2513.27 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{sc,\text{add}} &= 4 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 4 \times 201.06 = 804.25 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{sc,\text{total}} &= 2513.27 + 804.25 = 3317.52 \text{ mm}^2 \\
f_{ck,\text{jacket}} &= 35 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad ; \quad f_y = 415 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply IS 15988:2013 axial capacity equation:**

$$
P_u = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}
$$

**Step 2 — Concrete contribution (using $f_{ck} = 35$ for the jacket grade):**

$$
P_{c} = 0.4 \times 35 \times 3{,}02{,}500 = 42{,}35{,}000 \text{ N} = 4235.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Steel contribution:**

$$
P_{s} = 0.67 \times 415 \times 3317.52 = 9{,}22{,}432.6 \text{ N} \approx 922.43 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Total enhanced capacity:**

$$
\boxed{P_{u,\text{retrofit}} = 4235.0 + 922.43 = 5157.43 \text{ kN} \approx 5158 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with original:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{u,\text{original}} &= 0.4 \times 25 \times 1{,}60{,}000 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 2513.27 \\
&= 16{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}98{,}705.2 = 22{,}98{,}705.2 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 2298.7 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Enhancement factor:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Factor} = \dfrac{5157.43}{2298.7} = 2.24}
$$

**Result:** The column capacity increases by **2.24 times** (a **124% gain**).

**Marks Distribution:** Data identification (1), Equation writing (1), Concrete contribution (2), Steel contribution (2), Final answer and comparison (1).

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Slab & Alternative Beam Strengthening

> **[KTU University Exam - December 2023, Model Question]**

**(a) Describe the different methods of retrofitting RC slabs. Explain the design of a concrete overlay for upgrading the live load capacity of an existing slab. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**RC Slab Retrofitting Techniques:**

1. **Concrete Overlay (Screed Overlay):**
   - **Thin Overlay (25–50 mm):** Bonded via epoxy resin; for moderate load increases.
   - **Thick Overlay (50–100 mm):** Composite action via shear studs; for large load increases.

2. **External Bonding (FRP/Steel Plates):**
   - CFRP strips bonded to the tension face (soffit of slab).
   - Steel plates for industrial floors.

3. **Post-Tensioning:**
   - External tendons for long-span industrial slabs.

4. **Section Enlargement:**
   - Adding concrete below the existing slab (drop panel construction).

5. **Strengthening of Negative Moment Regions:**
   - CFRP laminates at column heads for flat slabs.

**Design of Concrete Overlay:**

**Step 1 — Determine New Design Load:**
$$
w_{\text{new}} = 1.5 \times (\text{DL}_{\text{new}}) + 1.5 \times (\text{LL}_{\text{new}})
$$
where the new DL includes the self-weight of the overlay.

**Step 2 — Compute Additional Moment Demand:**
$$
M_{\text{add}} = \dfrac{w_{\text{new}} \cdot L^2}{8} \quad \text{(for simply supported one-way strip)}
$$

**Step 3 — Check Composite Action:**
- Provide **shear connectors** (studs at 200–300 mm c/c).
- Surface must be **roughened to 6 mm amplitude**.
- Apply **bonding agent** (SBR or epoxy).

**Step 4 — Design Additional Reinforcement:**
- Treat as monolithic section with new effective depth $d_{\text{new}} = d_{\text{old}} + t_{\text{overlay}}$.
- Compute $A_{st,\text{add}}$ from:
$$
M_u = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st,\text{add}} \cdot d_{\text{new}} \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st,\text{add}} \cdot f_y}{b \cdot d_{\text{new}} \cdot f_{ck}}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Serviceability Check:**
- Verify deflection $\leq L / 250$.
- Check crack width $\leq 0.3$ mm.

**Marks Distribution:** Methods listed (3), Overlay design steps (3), Shear/connection detailing (1).

---

**(b) An existing RC beam of size 230 × 450 mm (effective cover = 40 mm) is provided with 3 numbers of 16 mm diameter bars as tension reinforcement. The beam is to be retrofitted by adding an external CFRP plate of width 100 mm and thickness 1.4 mm bonded to the soffit. Given: $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm², $E_f = 165{,}000$ N/mm². Compute the additional flexural moment capacity contributed by the CFRP plate. (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= 230 \text{ mm} \\
d &= 450 - 40 = 410 \text{ mm} \\
A_{s,\text{orig}} &= 3 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 3 \times 201.06 = 603.19 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_f &= 100 \times 1.4 = 140 \text{ mm}^2 \\
E_f &= 165{,}000 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
E_s &= 200{,}000 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
n_f &= E_f / E_s = 0.825
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent steel area (transform FRP to steel using modular ratio):**

$$
A_{f,\text{eq}} = n_f \cdot A_f = 0.825 \times 140 = 115.5 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Total equivalent steel:**

$$
A_{st,\text{eq}} = 603.19 + 115.5 = 718.69 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Depth of neutral axis:**

$$
x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st,\text{eq}}}{0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b} = \dfrac{0.87 \times 415 \times 718.69}{0.36 \times 25 \times 230}
$$

$$
x_u = \dfrac{2{,}59{,}548.7}{2070} = 125.39 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Lever arm:**

$$
z = d - 0.42 \cdot x_u = 410 - 0.42 \times 125.39 = 410 - 52.66 = 357.34 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Additional moment due to FRP plate (per ACI 440.2R, the FRP contribution):**

$$
M_f = n_f \cdot A_f \cdot f_y \cdot \left(d - 0.42 x_u\right) \cdot 0.87
$$

$$
M_f = 0.825 \times 140 \times 415 \times 357.34 \times 0.87
$$

$$
\boxed{M_f = 0.825 \times 140 \times 415 \times 357.34 \times 0.87 = 14{,}87{,}290 \text{ N·mm} \approx 1.49 \text{ kN·m}}
$$

**Step 6 — Total enhanced moment capacity (for reference):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,\text{total}} &= 0.87 \times f_y \times A_{st,\text{eq}} \times z \\
&= 0.87 \times 415 \times 718.69 \times 357.34 \\
&\approx 92{,}64{,}000 \text{ N·mm} \\
&\approx 92.64 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The CFRP plate provides an additional flexural capacity of approximately **1.49 kN·m**, representing a **~1.6%** enhancement. (In practice, FRP plates are most efficient when original $A_{st}$ is small or when significant loss has occurred.)

**Marks Distribution:** Modular ratio concept (1), Equivalent area (1), Neutral axis (2), Lever arm (1), Final moment (2).

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (For Last-Minute KTU ESE Revision):**

#### **I. Conceptual Definitions**
- Retrofitting ≠ Reconstruction. Retrofit = **modify in place** to meet new demands.
- Three Performance Levels: **IO → LS → CP** (per IS 15988).
- Governed by **IS 15988:2013** (retrofit) + **IS 456:2000** (concrete design) + **IS 13920:2016** (ductile detailing).

#### **II. Beam Retrofitting — Must-Know**
- Flexural: **FRP plate bonding, RC jacketing, external PT**.
- Shear: **FRP U-wrap, additional stirrups, side-bonded strips**.
- Code formula: $V_u = V_c + V_s + V_f$ (when FRP used).
- Modular ratio for FRP: $n_f = E_f / E_s$.

#### **III. Column Retrofitting — Must-Know**
- Three methods: **RC Jacketing, Steel Jacketing, FRP Wrapping**.
- RC Jacket equation: $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ — **memorize the 0.4 and 0.67 factors**.
- Confinement formula (FRP): $f_{cc}' = f_c'(1 + 2.05 t_f/b)$.
- Critical details: **135° hooks in ties, 100 mm c/c in plastic hinge region**.

#### **IV. Slab Retrofitting — Must-Know**
- **Concrete overlay** = most common; thickness 25–100 mm.
- Bonded via **SBR latex or epoxy** + **shear studs** for composite action.
- New effective depth: $d_{\text{new}} = d_{\text{old}} + t_{\text{overlay}}$.
- Always check **deflection** ($L/250$) and **crack width** (0.3 mm).

#### **V. Codes & Standards**
- **IS 15988:2013** — Seismic evaluation and retrofitting.
- **IS 456:2000** — Concrete design.
- **IS 13920:2016** — Ductile detailing (columns should be stronger than beams).
- **ACI 440.2R-17** — Externally bonded FRP systems (reference for design).

#### **VI. Common Exam Triggers**
- "Differentiate between" questions = 3 marks = comparison table mandatory.
- "Design the retrofit" = 7 marks = numerical with steps.
- "Explain techniques" = 7 marks = classification + sketch + design philosophy.

> **Final Tip:** In KTU valuation, **sketches carry 1–2 marks** even in numerical questions. Always include a labeled cross-section, even if simple.
